A Connoisseur would focus on her impressive collection of paintings, while a Spiteful woman would warn against visiting her, labeling her a dangerous siren who incites jealousy. An Attache might recall her beauty a decade ago, while a Distinguishing type would comment on her elite connections despite modest living.

A Duke would claim not to know her, recognizing only those presented at court. A Ninny would mistakenly assert she used to sing at the Opera, while two old ladies would discuss her lineage, revealing ties to the noble Cadignan family.

An Original would assure you her salon is safe to visit, as any scoundrels present are of high quality. An Observer would describe her as a beautiful woman who rarely rises to greet guests, suggesting she is a mystery, married to a mythical husband; he would note her charm and conversational skill, hinting at many admirers but asserting none favored.

A Contradictor would claim she was once Murat’s mistress, while an Envious person would dismiss her wealth, calling her a coquette who ruined a young man’s prospects. This species of envy is well-known, yet its persistence is perplexing.

Thus Madame Firmiani emerges as a complex figure, seen through many lenses—each perspective revealing more about the observer than the observed. These varied accounts, shaped by personal biases and experience, invite readers to explore interpretations while reflecting on the deeper emotional truths that connect them all.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In January 1824, opinions about Madame Firmiani varied so widely that no clear picture of her emerged. She might be a widow or a wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or otherwise, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. This range of interpretations showed how society distorted a reputation, much like lithographic blocks producing different images from slight variations.

Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly country magnate, grew intrigued by Madame Firmiani that winter. A provincial planter used to managing his estate and tenants, he was shrewd. He came to Paris from Touraine to learn more, but the gossip he found only increased his confusion. He was a man of honor with a beloved nephew, Octave de Camps, in whom he had high hopes. Octave had recently sold an estate without consulting him, which angered the old man. A distant relative claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and was now teaching mathematics to survive—a revelation that troubled Monsieur de Bourbonne.

Despite the cousin’s report, Monsieur de Bourbonne refused to accept the worst about his nephew and went to Paris to investigate Octave’s situation. Using his connections in the faubourg Saint-Germain, he learned more about Madame Firmiani and resolved to meet her under the name de Rouxellay, hoping to discern the truth of her relationship with Octave. He chose a time when he knew Octave would be occupied, and soon confirmed that the rumors of his nephew’s financial distress were true.

Monsieur de Rouxellay was not a typical provincial uncle; once a member of the King’s guard, he was versed in society’s manners and politics. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomere, he expressed pity for Octave, wondering how a woman of such luxury could be linked to someone in such straits. Yet on seeing Madame Firmiani, his anger melted. Her beauty, heightened by the salon’s atmosphere, captivated him and made him reconsider his initial judgments.

Madame Firmiani, said to be twenty-five but closer to twenty-eight, had married the invisible Firmiani at sixteen. At the height of her desirability, she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit. She was admired in high society, respected by rivals and peers alike. That popularity, however, made her a target for malicious gossip; many envied her apparent happiness and propriety. Though the rumors were unfounded, they persisted, showing the dangers of societal suspicion.

This brief account only hints at Madame Firmiani’s true nature. She embodied a complex blend of qualities that inspired admiration and intrigue. Her beauty, grace, and natural charm made her a figure of fascination, capable of evoking deep emotions and reflecting the multifaceted nature of womanhood and the human experience.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had left; Madame Firmiani found him still seated before her as the clock struck two in the morning. 

“Madame,” he began as she rose, signaling he should go, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”

Madame Firmiani sat back down, visibly affected. The old man, Monsieur de Bourbonne, could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure—provincial men often misread women's restrained emotions. She gave him a sharp look that left him perplexed.

“Someone in my province tells me my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth,” he said bluntly. “Forgive my rustic honesty; you should know these rumors.”

“Stop, monsieur,” she cut him off. “I know of them. You are too polite to press further and too gallant to think you may question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe in my contempt for money, though I married without fortune. Your nephew’s wealth or poverty matters not; I welcome him as a friend. All my friends respect one another; I cannot accept those I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip and dishonesty.”

Her voice rose slightly then settled into composed wit.

“Madame,” Monsieur de Bourbonne said, trembling, “I am old, almost a father to Octave. Pardon me for asking one thing. I swear as a gentleman your answer shall remain here.” He placed his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”

“To any other man I would answer with a look,” she replied. “To you I ask what you would think of a woman who answered such a question. To confess love is an effort; it is the lover’s reward. To tell another—” She did not finish, rose, bowed, and withdrew to her private rooms.

He left thinking her either sly or angelic.

The next morning, around eight, Monsieur de Bourbonne found Octave in his garret. The old man berated him for squandering the advantages of a comfortable uncle who meant to leave him an estate in Touraine. “Have you been gambling? Lost at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie, I’ll return to my youthful ways.”

“I saw your Madame Firmiani yesterday,” he went on, approvingly. “Have you ruined yourself for her?”

“Yes, uncle,” Octave admitted.

“I guessed as much. In my day women were cleverer at ruining men—this one is a piece of the last century,” the old man said.

“You are mistaken, uncle. Madame Firmiani deserves esteem and adoration,” Octave said tenderly. He produced a letter from her, given in a portfolio she had given him. “Read this; it explains almost everything.”

Octave began to read aloud: “‘My beloved—’”

“Then you are still intimate with her?” his uncle interrupted.

“Of course,” Octave said. “We are married.”

“Heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Then why do you live in a garret?”

“Let me continue.” Octave read, sometimes pausing with emotion: “‘My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; this may be a misfortune, for a wife should be gay and caressing. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is in the man she loves. Esteem, consideration, honor—must they not belong to him who receives our all? Well, my angel has fallen. The tale you told me has tarnished my past joys. I feel humiliated in you—you whom I thought the most honorable of men. I must have deep confidence to make this avowal, which costs me much. How can you, knowing your father unjustly deprived others of their property, keep it? You told me of this in the room where our love began; you are a gentleman, you think yourself noble, and I am yours. I find some excuses in your youth and thoughtlessness, but I cannot respect a man who knowingly is smirched for a sum of money. Five francs stolen at play or five times a hundred thousand won by a legal trick equally dishonor. Love, to a woman, means boundless confidence and reverence. If you come to me poor, my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. I do not wish you to make restitution because I urge it; consult your conscience. A man who loves a woman sincerely respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself. I am your wife, not your mistress. I blame myself for preaching, but scold me if you must—only a little. Dear, between us the power is yours—you alone must perceive your faults.’”

There was silence. Octave wiped his eyes. “There’s more,” he said, but the rest he smiled away as a lover’s secret.

“You teach mathematics, and you have your uncle’s capital?” Monsieur de Bourbonne asked. Octave explained that the letter had sparked a revolution in him. He had been haunted by conscience—every pleasure felt like theft—and he rushed to Madame Firmiani. Together they calculated what was due to the Bourgneufs, whom Octave’s father had wronged. “I condemned myself to pay three percent interest,” he said, though his means did not cover the full amount. Madame Firmiani, whose position had made her careful, offered her savings and he accepted.

“You mean that adorable woman actually saves money?” the uncle exclaimed.

“Do not laugh at her,” Octave said. “Her husband went to Greece in 1820 and died three years later. Legal proof of his death and his will, leaving everything to her, were lost or stolen during the troubles there. Unsure whether she might lose her fortune, she lived prudently. I wanted to acquire property of my own to provide for her if she lost hers.” He added that he gave lessons to avoid living at anyone’s expense and that the act of restitution had brought him joys beyond millions. He found the Bourgneufs living miserably: an old father agent, daughters keeping books and house, a sick mother. To bring them relief felt like being Providence; words could not convey the happiness of making amends. He called Madame Firmiani “my dear conscience”—she inspired a delicacy of feeling he thought he had lacked—and he hoped to become rich by his own industry.

Touched, the old man said, “Ah, my boy, you have your mother’s soul.” Then, from the street, the sound of a carriage announced Madame Firmiani. She entered, slightly annoyed to find Monsieur de Bourbonne there but smiling. “I came to humbly entreat my husband to accept my fortune,” she said. “The Austrian Embassy has sent me a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet kept safe for me. Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here” —she laid her hand on his heart— “to which none but God can add.” She then laid her head on his breast in unrestrained happiness.

Monsieur de Bourbonne concluded, “In my day we made love; in yours, you love. Women are all that is best in humanity; they are not even guilty of their faults, for they come through us.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once a dark, winding lane near the Hotel de Ville, ended at the Rue Martroi and was marked by a turnstile that stood until 1823. Less than six feet wide at its broadest, it often pooled gutter water and was perpetually muddy; in summer sunlight briefly reached it, but dampness lingered.

Residents lit lamps at five in June and kept them burning through winter. Approaching the Marais felt like walking through cellars, as many old Paris streets were similarly gloomy. The corner house at the junction of Rue du Tourniquet and Rue de la Tixeranderie showed its age: iron rings remained from chains once used for public safety.

Built to combat humidity, the house had cellars raised above ground and an entrance reached by three steps. The door bore a closed arch with a keystone carved as a female head. Three high windows, protected by iron bars, lit a small dwelling within. Observers could see two beds draped in green serge and an old woman tending a brazier, preparing a stew typical of porters’ wives. The kitchen-dining room was sparsely furnished yet kept with evident cleanliness and thrift.

The old woman’s pale, wrinkled face seemed as much part of the house as its walls. She sat by the fireplace with an expression of mischief mixed with resignation, while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. With slow fingers and antiquated spectacles, the mother occasionally glanced out the window; the daughter bent over her work with focused modesty.

Their modest home reflected the lives of working-class Parisian women. Despite surroundings of poverty, the girl retained youthful charm—rosy cheeks and bright eyes suggesting a spirit not wholly dimmed by hardship. The old woman speculated about passersby and hoped for a better future for her daughter; the girl, absorbed in embroidery, usually kept her gaze down.

One August day in 1815 the mother noticed a new face among the regular passersby: a tall, thin man in black whose solemn bearing intrigued her. She fancied he was a clerk from the Prefet’s office, and her excitement was apparent. Caroline, the girl, roused from indifference by her mother’s remarks, glimpsed the stranger, but he had already passed.

The old woman eagerly awaited his return, convinced he would come by again. When he did, Caroline caught another glimpse and felt an unfamiliar sympathy, noting the traces of suffering on his face. This was the first time a passerby had stirred such feeling in her; she usually regarded passersby with indifference, focused only on her work and daily routine.

The man’s presence left a lasting impression on Caroline, differing sharply from other sight-seers. For the first time she felt compassion for someone beyond her own troubles. As she resumed her embroidery, she found herself looking forward to another sight of him, intrigued by the possibility that this new acquaintance might alter the course of her life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For over two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved erratically, not always passing the Rue du Tourniquet at the same time. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted manner—often lost in thought and seldom acknowledging her or her daughter, Caroline. One mid-September morning, Caroline’s lively presence caught his eye; he watched the animated scene at the window, and a silent connection began, made of fleeting glances that suggested a deeper understanding.

Weeks passed and the Gentleman began to pass twice daily; their brief encounters became a routine they both anticipated. Their silent exchanges conveyed mutual concern: Caroline sensed when he was troubled, and he could tell when she was anxious about her work. Yet both remained cautious, fearing that greater intimacy might bring complications or heartache.

Madame Crochard, irritated by her daughter’s indecision, turned her displeasure toward the Gentleman, who had once been the object of her mild approval. As winter neared, the hardships of life weighed on Caroline, and the Gentleman noticed how it wore on her spirit. One night he overheard a mother-and-daughter conversation that made their financial struggles plain. Touched, he secretly left money for them; Caroline found it the next day, and a moment of silent gratitude passed between them.

In early May, Caroline proposed a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—unexpectedly joined them. During the journey Madame Crochard pretended to sleep, giving Caroline and the Gentleman a chance for light-hearted talk that revealed their growing affection. The countryside’s beauty and the warmth of their exchange lifted his spirits; he began to see Caroline differently.

Their bond deepened as they shared personal stories. Caroline spoke of her family’s hardships and her mother’s sacrifices. The Gentleman, now called Roger, was drawn to her honesty and resilience. Their day in Montmorency brimmed with laughter and joy, a sharp contrast to their previously somber meetings.

As evening approached, Caroline voiced her newfound happiness, and Roger reflected on the possibility of joy beyond that day. Their shared understanding hinted at a deeper bond. Preparing to leave, they playfully held hands, solidifying the connection. Madame Crochard pretended not to notice, but the gesture marked a meaningful step in their relationship—an unspoken promise and the hope of a brighter future together.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The adventures of Roger and Caroline continue in modern Paris, where newly built apartments seem ideal for honeymooners. In a pristine building on Rue Taitbout, Caroline has lived over a month in a charming second-floor apartment, furnished with care and style. The rooms, freshly painted and elegantly appointed, reflect current trends influenced by English design.

Caroline's bedroom has gray hangings trimmed with green silk, comfortable chairs, and a writing table for love letters. A bronze clock showing Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-patterned carpet complete the cozy setting. As she prepares for Roger's visit, her hairdresser flatters her, reminding her of Roger's admiration.

On a chilly September day in 1816, Caroline wears a green grenadine gown trimmed with chinchilla. Eagerly, she steps onto the balcony to watch for Roger; her heart races as she anticipates his arrival. After a quarter-hour of waiting, impatience replaces excitement, but she brightens when she finally sees him approaching.

Roger arrives, and they embrace passionately, rejoicing in their reunion after three long days apart. He then tells her he must attend a meeting with the Attorney-General, which disappoints Caroline. She playfully objects to the idea of enjoying the theatre without him, declaring her deep affection.

Despite the setback, Caroline quickly turns back to the joy of being with Roger. They share tender moments, and she expresses her wish to take his name—Caroline de Bellefeuille—as a sign of commitment. As they reminisce, Caroline perches on Roger's lap and talks about using her earnings to help the poor, underscoring her gratitude for their life together.

Her excitement grows while she plays a sonata on the piano, but Roger, mindful of his obligations, prepares to leave. They exchange a playful farewell, and Caroline watches him drive away, savoring the moment and longing for his return.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Five years after Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille settled in her home on Rue Taitbout, she sits serenely with her four-year-old son, Charles, who energetically plays with a rocking horse. Caroline, now twenty-four, radiates beauty shaped by happiness and fulfillment. She has embraced motherhood, focusing on nurturing her children and cultivating their appreciation for goodness and beauty. Despite these humble pleasures, she remains blissfully indifferent to societal expectations, having never desired luxury or status.

As she rocks her infant daughter, Eugenie, Caroline reflects on the joyful times with Roger during visits to Bellefeuille. Their love has flourished in the simplicity of their shared life, free from the complications of social convention. Caroline wonders quietly why Roger seeks happiness in secrecy, curious about the nature of their relationship.

On May 6, 1822, the anniversary of a pivotal day in their lives, Caroline prepares for dinner, eagerly anticipating Roger's arrival. He brings a document outlining Eugenie’s marriage portion, prompting a discussion about different financial provisions for their children. Roger explains he believes Charles, as a boy, should learn self-sufficiency, while Eugenie, as a girl, deserves a fortune.

Their evening is warm and lively, ending with a magical lantern show for Charles. But as night deepens, Roger must leave, and Caroline feels the bittersweet pang of separation. He reassures her he has three days off, yet the weight of duty looms.

Shortly after this anniversary, Caroline hurries to her mother, Madame Crochard, who is gravely ill. The old widow’s acquaintances, unaware Caroline is her daughter, have summoned a priest to offer last rites. Caroline arrives to a scene of confusion and deceit: the old women hoping to benefit from Madame Crochard’s wealth. Despite their intentions, Caroline’s love for her mother shows as she tries to care for her.

Madame Crochard, in her final moments, expresses regret over estrangement from Caroline and reveals that the priest has made her confess about Caroline’s benefactor. This revelation hints at a complex web of relationships and secrets to follow.

To understand these connections, we look back to events leading up to Madame Crochard’s death. In November 1805, a young barrister, Monsieur de Granville, finds himself in a precarious position after leaving a gathering at the High Chancellor’s residence and is offered a chance to advance his career through his uncle’s influence.

Granville receives a letter from his father urging a swift return home: a possible marriage to Mademoiselle Angelique Bontems could secure his future. The letter details the financial benefits of the union, despite the social stigma attached to the Bontems family. Torn between ambition and feeling, Granville recalls childhood affection for Angelique, whom he has not seen in years.

The next day he travels to Bayeux, greeted by his father and taken to the Bontems’ home, where the atmosphere is stifling under the widow’s strict religious observances. Granville dreads the match, but his father’s insistence weighs heavily.

During a church service, Granville glimpses Angelique, whose beauty captivates him anew. Their brief interaction rekindles his feelings, though he is acutely aware of family constraints. As the service ends, his impatience leads him to approach Angelique, setting the stage for a reunion that could alter their lives.

The intertwining stories of Caroline and Granville reveal the complexities of love, duty, and social expectation, as both characters navigate personal desires against familial obligations and ambitions.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he visited her regularly in her dreary drawing-room. During these visits he observed Angelique marking her trousseau; she never initiated discussions about religion. When Granville playfully handled her rosary or mocked religious practices, she would quietly return it to its bag with a serene smile. She firmly believed in the Church’s teachings, insisting a mother should have a religion and questioning how one could judge between disbelievers and God.

Granville, captivated by her conviction, sometimes felt tempted to adopt her beliefs, mistakenly equating his desire for her with love. Angelique was content to reconcile feeling and duty, leaving Granville unsure about his own emotions. He was grateful to Madame Bontems for instilling religious principles in Angelique, which he thought would help her face life’s challenges.

On the day of their marriage contract, Madame Bontems required Granville to promise to respect Angelique’s religious practices and allow her freedom of conscience. Angelique’s innocent gaze convinced him to agree; the Abbe Fontanon, who guided their consciences, smiled approvingly. Shortly after their wedding the couple moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor.

Angelique persuaded him to choose a large ground-floor apartment in the Marais, close to a church and chapel, arguing it would benefit their future children. Granville, who had preferred a more fashionable area, yielded. He set up his study and left the home decoration to Angelique, who delighted in furnishing their new home. But Granville was dismayed by the cold, solemn atmosphere she created, which reflected her austere upbringing.

Despite efforts to understand her choices, Granville grew increasingly frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm. He attributed the decor to Angelique’s Bayeux background, where taste seemed less refined, and tried to comfort her by insisting happiness came from her love and gentleness rather than material things.

From April through winter the couple appeared harmonious, Granville kept busy with work, and he remained largely unaware of Angelique’s strict religious observances—Lenten meals she disguised as carelessness. He accompanied her to church on Sundays but did not grasp her fervor until a significant event disrupted their lives.

In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, who had guided Angelique, arrived in Paris alarmed by what he saw as her moral decline and sought to reclaim her from a lax lifestyle. Angelique adopted stricter practices, which caused misunderstandings, particularly over social obligations. Granville grew irritated by her frequent excuses to avoid events, especially balls.

After a tense evening he confronted her about her demeanor and attire, urging her to embrace her social role. Arguments escalated: Angelique cited her director’s prohibitions against attending balls; Granville insisted her priest was overstepping. The dispute became theological and culminated in Angelique writing to the Pope for guidance.

The Pope’s reply affirmed Granville’s position, saying a wife should accompany her husband even if it meant compromising her virtue. Rather than resolve matters, this deepened the divide; Angelique and her director dismissed the Pope’s advice. Frustrated by her strict observances, Granville began to assert his preferences more forcefully, ordering meat daily despite her objections.

As the home atmosphere grew oppressive, Granville withdrew from social life, weighed down by Angelique’s bigotry. The household became stifling and gloom-filled, affecting everyone including the servants. He recognized that their home had been transformed into a place devoid of joy, marked by rigid piety.

Granville’s discontent deepened as Angelique’s beauty was overshadowed by austerity. He felt trapped in a marriage where duty had replaced love, and her devotion left little room for affection. Their children, raised under her strict rules, suffered from the oppressive climate; Granville sent his sons to school early and resolved to marry off his daughters as soon as they came of age.

His isolation increased as he struggled with his marriage’s reality. He sought solace in work, attempting to write a book on law, but Angelique’s attempts to reconvert him only added to his frustration. The relationship deteriorated: Granville felt increasingly alienated from his wife and their home, which had become a place of coldness and rigidity rather than warmth and companionship.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The history of the de Granville household between 1806 and 1825 was marked by few events. Madame de Granville remained unchanged after losing her husband's affection, continuing to pay for Masses hoping to understand her faults and restore their relationship. Despite her fervent prayers, Granville, now a prominent judge, had taken to living in the entresol of their home to avoid her. Each morning a housekeeper relayed messages between them, with Granville consistently declining breakfast invitations, citing important court business.

Madame de Granville, now thirty-five and looking older, awaited her husband's return, eager to engage him with her narrow-minded remarks. When Granville was offered the presidency of a provincial court, he declined, prompting the Countess's friends and confessor to suspect an illicit affair. They speculated his refusal stemmed from a liaison, which deeply troubled Madame de Granville, who believed her husband incapable of such sins.

During Lent in 1822 her health deteriorated from stress and low fever, exacerbated by Granville's indifference. Despite attempts to be more affectionate, her bigotry often resurfaced, undoing her efforts. By late May, as she recovered, she reflected on her past and sought guidance from the Abbe Fontanon, who delivered devastating news: Granville had been living in sin with a concubine for seven years, fathering two children and squandering their fortune.

In disbelief, Madame de Granville struggled with the revelation, torn between faith and betrayal. The Abbe urged her to forgive and wait for Granville's enlightenment, but she was consumed by a whirlwind of emotions. After a tumultuous day, she left home, determined to confront her husband.

Meanwhile, Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille were in a tense moment: she expressed hatred for his wife, whom she blamed for their unhappiness. Roger reassured her, hoping for a future together, but their conversation was interrupted by Madame de Granville's sudden fainting. Upon regaining consciousness she confronted Granville, questioning his love and fidelity. Granville accused her of sacrificing his happiness to her piety, claiming she had failed to fulfill her role as a loving wife.

Their exchange revealed deep issues in their marriage. Granville insisted true love requires sacrifice and understanding, while Madame de Granville clung to moral righteousness. His coldness and her desperate pleas for love highlighted the chasm between them. He explained that while he had sought happiness elsewhere, she had neglected their relationship for religious devotion.

As the conversation escalated, Madame de Granville's jealousy and anger flared, producing a painful recognition of their irreconcilable differences. Granville's indifference to her suffering and his acknowledgment of love for another woman left her devastated. Despite tears and pleas for forgiveness, Granville remained resolute, saying he could not rekindle a love that had faded through years of neglect.

The dialogue culminated in a stark acknowledgment of their failed marriage: love could not be commanded or revived at will. Madame de Granville's despair deepened as she faced the reality of her husband's emotional detachment, left to grapple with the consequences of their choices and the future of their relationship.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Early in December 1833, a man with white hair and worn features, aged by grief rather than years, walked along the Rue Gaillon at midnight. He paused to look up at a modest two-story house, where a dim light flickered through the attic windows, some patched with paper. As he watched, a young man emerged from the house. Recognizing him, the young man approached hesitantly. “What, is it you, Monsieur le President? Alone at this hour, so far from the Rue Saint-Lazare. Allow me to give you my arm. The pavement is so greasy this morning that if we do not hold each other up, we shall find it hard to escape a tumble.”

The Comte de Granville replied, “But, my dear sir, I am no more than fifty-five, unfortunately for me. A physician of your celebrity must know that at that age a man is still hale and strong.”

“Then you are in waiting on a lady, I suppose,” said Horace Bianchon. “You are not in the habit of going about Paris on foot. When a man keeps such fine horses—”

“Still, when I am not visiting in the evening, I commonly return from the Courts or the club on foot,” Granville responded.

“And with large sums of money about you, perhaps!” exclaimed the doctor. “It is a positive invitation to the assassin’s knife.”

“I am not afraid of that,” Granville answered with melancholy indifference.

“Then do not stand about,” Bianchon urged, leading the Count towards the boulevard. “A little more and I shall believe that you are bent on robbing me of your last illness, dying by some other hand than mine.”

“You caught me playing the spy,” Granville admitted. “For some time past, I have observed at a window on the third floor of your house the shadow of a person who seems to work with heroic constancy.”

Granville paused, as if in pain. “I take as great an interest in that garret as a citizen of Paris must feel in the finishing of the Palais Royal.”

“Well,” Bianchon said eagerly, “I can tell you—”

“Tell me nothing,” Granville interrupted. “I would not give a centime to know whether the shadow that moves across that shabby blind is that of a man or a woman, nor whether the inhabitant of that attic is happy or miserable. Though I was surprised to see no one at work there this evening, I stopped to look solely for the pleasure of indulging in conjectures as numerous and as idiotic as those of idlers who see a building left half-finished. For nine years, I have ceased to wonder that old men amuse themselves with growing flowers and planting trees; the events of life have taught them disbelief in all human affection. I grew old within a few days. I will no longer attach myself to any creature but to unreasoning animals, or plants, or superficial things. I think more of Taglioni’s grace than of all human feeling. I abhor life and the world in which I live alone. Nothing, nothing can move or interest me.”

“But you have children?” Bianchon asked.

“My children!” Granville repeated bitterly. “Yes—my eldest daughter is the Comtesse de Vandenesse. The other will, through her sister’s connections, make a good match. My sons have succeeded; the Viscount was public prosecutor at Limoges and is now President of the Court at Orleans; the younger is public prosecutor in Paris. My children have their own cares, their own anxieties. If one of those hearts had been devoted to me, if one had tried to fill the void I have here,” he said, striking his breast, “that one would have failed in life, sacrificing it to me. And why should he? To bring sunshine into my few remaining years—and would he have succeeded? Might I not have accepted such generosity as a debt? But, doctor,” he added with deep irony, “it is not for nothing that we teach them arithmetic and how to count. At this moment perhaps they are waiting for my money.”

“O Monsieur le Comte, how could such an idea enter your head—you who are kind, friendly, and humane! If I were not myself a living proof of the benevolence you exercise so liberally—”

“To please myself,” Granville replied. “I pay for a sensation, as I would pay a pile of gold to recover the most childish illusion that would make my heart glow. I help my fellow-creatures for my own sake, just as I gamble; I look for gratitude from none. I should see you die without blinking; and I beg of you to feel the same with regard to me. The events of life have swept over my heart like the lavas of Vesuvius over Herculaneum. The town is there—dead.”

“Those who have brought a soul as warm and as living as yours to such a pitch of indifference are indeed guilty!” Bianchon exclaimed.

“Say no more,” Granville said, shuddering with aversion.

“You have a malady which you ought to allow me to treat,” Bianchon insisted.

“What, do you know of a cure for death?” Granville cried irritably.

“I undertake, Monsieur le Comte, to revive the heart you believe to be frozen.”

“Are you a match for Talma, then?” Granville asked satirically.

“No, Monsieur le Comte. But Nature is as far above Talma as Talma is superior to me. Listen: the garret you are interested in is inhabited by a woman of about thirty, and her love is carried to fanaticism. The object of her adoration is a young man of pleasing appearance but endowed with every conceivable vice. This fellow is a gambler, and it is hard to say which he is more addicted to—wine or women; he has committed acts deserving punishment by law. To him, this unhappy woman sacrificed a life of ease, a man who worshiped her, and the father of her children.”

“What is wrong, Monsieur le Comte?” Granville asked.

“Nothing. Go on,” he replied.

“She has allowed him to squander a perfect fortune; she would give him the world if she had it; she works night and day; and many times she has seen the wretch she adores rob her even of the money saved for her children’s clothes and food. Only three days ago she sold her hair, the finest I ever saw; he came in, and she could not hide the gold piece quickly enough. For a smile, for a kiss, she gave up the price of a fortnight’s life and peace. Is it not dreadful, and yet sublime? But work is wearing her cheeks hollow. Her children’s crying has broken her heart; she is ill, and at this moment on her wretched bed. This evening they had nothing to eat; the children have not strength to cry; they were silent when I went up.”

Bianchon stood still. Granville, despite himself, put his hand into his waistcoat pocket.

“I can guess, my young friend, how it is that she is yet alive if you attend her,” Granville said.

“O poor soul!” Bianchon cried. “Who could refuse to help her? I only wish I were richer, for I hope to cure her of her passion.”

“But how can you expect me to pity a form of misery of which the joys to me would seem cheaply purchased with my whole fortune!” Granville exclaimed, withdrawing his hand empty of the notes Bianchon had assumed he was feeling for. “That woman feels; she is alive! Would not Louis XV. have given his kingdom to rise from the grave and have three days of youth and life? And is not that the history of thousands of dead men, thousands of sick men, thousands of old men?”

“Poor Caroline!” Bianchon lamented.

At the mention of the name, Granville shuddered and grasped the doctor’s arm tightly.

“Her name is Caroline Crochard?” he asked, his voice breaking.

“Then you know her?” Bianchon replied, astonished.

“And the wretch’s name is Solvet. Ay, you have kept your word!” Granville exclaimed. “You have roused my heart to the most terrible pain it can suffer until it is dust. That emotion, too, is a gift from hell, and I always know how to pay those debts.”

By this time, they had reached the corner of the Rue de la Chaussee d’Antin. A night scavenger, with a shriveled face, stood by the curb.

“Do you ever pick up a thousand-franc note?” Granville asked.

“Now and then, master,” the scavenger replied.

“And you restore them?”

“It depends on the reward offered.”

“You're the man for me,” Granville said, handing him a thousand-franc note. “Take this, but remember, I give it to you on condition of your spending it at the wineshop, getting drunk, fighting, beating your wife, blacking your friends’ eyes. That will give work to the watch, the surgeon, the druggist—perhaps to the police, the public prosecutor, the judge, and the prison warders. Do not try to do anything else, or the devil will be revenged on you sooner or later.”

A draughtsman would need the pencil of Charlet and Callot, the brush of Teniers and Rembrandt to capture the night scene accurately.

“Now I have squared accounts with hell and had some pleasure for my money,” Granville said, pointing out the scavenger’s grotesque face to Bianchon, who stood stupefied. “As for Caroline Crochard—she may die of hunger and thirst, hearing the heartrending cries of her starving children, convinced of the baseness of the man she loves. I will not give a sou to rescue her; and because you have helped her, I will see you no more—”

Granville left Bianchon standing like a statue and walked briskly to the Rue Saint-Lazare, where he found a carriage waiting at his door.

“Monsieur, your son, the attorney-general, came about an hour ago,” the servant said. “He is waiting for you in your bedroom.”

Granville dismissed the servant and entered the room. “What motive can be strong enough to require you to infringe the order I have given my children never to come to me unless I send for them?” he asked his son.

“Father,” the younger man replied tremulously, “I hope you will forgive me when you have heard me.”

“Your reply is proper,” Granville said. “Sit down,” he gestured to a chair. “But whether I walk up and down or take a seat, speak without heeding me.”

“Father,” the son continued, “this afternoon, at four o’clock, a very young man who was arrested in the house of a friend of mine, whom he had robbed, appealed to you. He says he is your son.”

“His name?” Granville asked hoarsely.

“Charles Crochard.”

“That will do,” Granville said, waving his hand imperiously.

He paced the room in silence, and his son remained quiet.

“My son,” he began, his voice mild and fatherly, causing the young lawyer to start. “Charles Crochard spoke the truth. I am glad you came to me tonight, my good Eugene.” He handed him a bundle of banknotes. “Here is a considerable sum of money; you can make any use of it in this matter. I trust you implicitly and approve whatever arrangements you may make, either now or in the future. Eugene, my dear son, kiss me. We part perhaps for the last time. I shall tomorrow crave my dismissal from the King, and I am going to Italy.

“Though a father owes no account of his life to his children, he is bound to bequeath to them the experience Fate sells him dearly—is it not a part of their inheritance? When you marry,” he continued, involuntarily shuddering, “do not undertake it lightly; that act is the most important of all which society requires of us. Remember to study the character of the woman who is to be your partner; consult me too, I will judge of her myself. A lack of union between husband and wife, from whatever cause, leads to terrible misfortune; sooner or later we are always punished for contravening the social law. But I will write to you on this subject from Florence. A father who presides over a supreme court of justice must not have to blush in the presence of his son. Good-bye.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The incident in this sketch occurred in late November 1809, during the height of Napoleon’s empire. The echoes of the Battle of Wagram still resonated in Austria as peace was being negotiated between France and the Coalition. Kings and princes orbited Napoleon, who reveled in his power, showcasing the splendor of Parisian entertainments around his marriage to an Austrian archduchess. The opulence of the French aristocracy was unmatched, with diamonds and gold embellishing women and military uniforms, a stark contrast to the Republic's earlier austerity. The atmosphere was intoxicating as military leaders indulged in wealth amassed through the sacrifices of countless soldiers.

Many women adopted a carefree attitude reminiscent of the Louis XV era. Whether influenced by the fallen monarchy or the Imperial family's conduct, both men and women embraced a hedonistic lifestyle that seemed to foreshadow doom. The allure of military men became a frenzy aligned with the Emperor’s interests, since frequent calls to arms made every peace treaty temporary. Women’s affections shifted rapidly; many moved from mistress to widow in a matter of weeks.

The appeal of soldiers stemmed from prospects of early widowhood, the lure of historical legacy, or the romantic notion of courage. Women were drawn to the idea that their passions would remain secret, buried on the battlefield. This fascination with military men, seen as embodiments of honor and adventure, produced a culture where bravery was prized. The period was marked by a mania for glittering possessions: diamonds and fireworks were in vogue, and men wore jewels following extravagant examples like Murat.

Comte de Gondreville, formerly Citizen Malin, had risen to prominence and postponed a grand entertainment to outshine other courtiers and impress Napoleon. His drawing-rooms filled with ambassadors, influential figures, and a few princes, all expecting the Emperor. But a private dispute between Napoleon and Josephine, hinting at divorce, kept him from the festivities, though the guests remained unaware of the tension.

The most beautiful women in Paris flocked to Gondreville’s event, embodying luxury and elegance. The financial elite, proud of their wealth, sought to rival the military’s splendor, using grand balls to introduce daughters to Napoleon’s inner circle, hoping to trade fortunes for favor. Women confident in their beauty attended to test their allure, masking their true intentions behind smiles and friendly facades. Amidst the gaiety, many were more wary of their friends than of enemies, as the pursuit of wealth and power underpinned the social interactions of the time.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents surrounding the little imbroglio that is the subject of this study, reflecting the tone prevalent in Paris drawing-rooms at the time.

“Look towards the pedestal supporting that candelabrum—do you see the young lady with her hair styled a la Chinoise? She’s in the corner to the left, with bluebells in her chestnut curls. She’s so pale you might think she’s ill, delicate-looking, and very small. Now she’s turning her head; her almond-shaped blue eyes seem made for tears. She’s leaning forward to see Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd.”

“I see her now. You only had to mention her lovely complexion; I would have recognized her. She has the whitest skin here, but she’s either a prude or a coquette, as her bodice barely hints at her beauty. What shoulders! What lily-whiteness!”

“Who is she?” asked the first speaker.

“I don’t know.”

“An aristocrat! Do you want to keep them all to yourself, Montcornet?”

“You of all people to tease me!” replied Montcornet with a smile. “Do you think you can insult a poor general like me just because you’re a rival of Soulanges? Or is it because I’ve only just arrived in this Promised Land? Come, Monsieur le Maitre des Requetes, let us glean in the field you can only hold precariously. We have a right to live! If you knew the German women, you might help me with the Parisian you love best.”

“Well, General, since you’ve noticed that lady, have you seen her dance?”

“Where have you been? There’s a triple rank of the most undaunted coquettes of Paris between her and the swarm of dancing men. It’s a miracle anyone could spot her in that corner, buried in gloom despite the candles. Between her and us is a sparkle of diamonds and glances, so many floating plumes and fluttering lace that it’s impossible for any dancer to detect her among those stars. How could you think she’s the wife of some sous-prefet from Lippe or Dyle?”

“Oh, he will be!” exclaimed the Master of Appeals.

“I doubt it,” replied the Colonel of Cuirassiers, laughing. “She seems as raw in intrigue as you are in diplomacy. I bet you don’t know how she got there.”

The lawyer looked at the Colonel with a mix of contempt and curiosity.

“Well,” Montcornet continued, “she probably arrived punctually at nine, the first of the company, and embarrassed the Comtesse de Gondreville, who can’t think straight. Repulsed by the mistress of the house and driven into the corner by jealous ladies, she became a victim of their coalition against her. Each of those sweet faces would have conspired to keep the men from taking her out. That’s how those tender-looking women would have formed a coalition against the stranger without saying a word beyond, ‘Do you know that little woman in blue?’ If you care to run the gauntlet of flattering glances and inviting questions, try to get through the rampart defending that Queen of Dyle, Lippe, or Charente. You’ll see how even the dullest woman will invent a wile to keep the plaintive stranger in the shadows. Doesn’t she look like an elegy?”

“Do you think so, Montcornet? Then she must be married?”

“Why not a widow?”

“She would be less passive,” said the lawyer, laughing.

“She might be the widow of a gambler,” replied the handsome Colonel.

“Of course; since the peace, there are many widows of that sort!” said Martial. “But my dear Montcornet, we are simpletons. That face is too innocent; there’s too much youth and freshness for her to be married. What splendid flesh-tints! Nothing has sunk in the modeling of her nose. Her lips and chin are as fresh as a white rosebud, though her expression is veiled by clouds of sadness. Who could make that young creature weep?”

“Women cry for little,” said the Colonel.

“I don’t know,” replied Martial; “but she’s not crying because she’s left without a partner; her grief is not new. It’s clear she beautified herself for this evening with intention. I would wager she’s in love already.”

“Bah! She’s perhaps the daughter of some German princeling; no one talks to her,” said Montcornet.

“Dear! How unhappy a poor child may be! Can there be anything more graceful and refined than our little stranger? Yet not one of those furies around her, who think they can feel, will say a word to her. If she would only speak, we’d see if she has fine teeth.”

“Bless me, you boil over like milk at the least increase of temperature!” cried the Colonel, a bit nettled at finding a rival in his friend.

“What!” exclaimed the lawyer, ignoring the Colonel’s question. “Can nobody here tell us the name of this exotic flower?”

“Some lady companion!” said Montcornet.

“A companion! Wearing sapphires fit for a queen and a dress of Malines lace? Tell that to the marines, General. You wouldn’t shine in diplomacy if you jump from a German princess to a lady companion.”

Montcornet stopped a man by taking his arm—a fat little man with iron-gray hair and clever eyes, mingling unceremoniously with various groups.

“Gondreville, my friend,” said Montcornet, “who is that charming little woman sitting under that huge candelabrum?”

“The candelabrum? Ravrio’s work; Isabey made the design.”

“Oh, I recognized your lavishness and taste; but the lady?”

“Ah! I don’t know. Some friend of my wife’s, no doubt.”

“Or your mistress, you old rascal.”

“No, on my honor. The Comtesse de Gondreville is the only one capable of inviting people no one knows.”

Despite this acerbic comment, the fat little man’s lips retained a smile from the Colonel’s suggestion. Montcornet returned to the lawyer, who had rejoined a neighboring group, intent on asking, but in vain, for information about the fair unknown. He grasped Martial’s arm and said in his ear:

“Mind what you’re about. Madame de Vaudremont has been watching you for some time with ominous attentiveness; she can guess by the movement of your lips what you say to me. Our eyes have already told her too much; she’s thinking even more than we are about the little blue lady.”

“That’s too old a trick in warfare, my dear Montcornet! However, what do I care? Like the Emperor, when I make a conquest, I keep it.”

“Martial, your fatuity cries out for a lesson. You, a civilian, and so lucky as to be the husband-designate of Madame de Vaudremont, a widow of two-and-twenty with four thousand napoleons a year—who slips such a diamond on your finger,” he added, taking the lawyer’s left hand, which the young man complacently allowed; “and you affect the Lovelace, as if you were a colonel obliged to maintain the military reputation! Fie! Just think of all you may lose.”

“At any rate, I shall not lose my liberty,” replied Martial with a forced laugh.

He cast a passionate glance at Madame de Vaudremont, who responded only with a smile of unease, having seen the Colonel examining the lawyer’s ring.

“Listen to me, Martial. If you flutter around my young stranger, I shall set to work to win Madame de Vaudremont.”

“You have my full permission, my dear Cuirassier, but you will not gain this much,” and the young Maitre des Requetes put his polished thumb-nail under an upper tooth with a mocking click.

“Remember that I am unmarried,” said the Colonel; “that my sword is my whole fortune; and that such a challenge is setting Tantalus down to a banquet he cannot devour.”

“Prrr.”

This defiant roll of consonants was the only reply to the Colonel’s declaration, as Martial looked him over before turning away.

The fashion of the time required men to wear white kerseymere breeches and silk stockings at a ball. This costume showcased Montcornet’s fine shape. At thirty-five, he attracted attention with his stalwart height, emphasized by the handsome uniform of the Cuirassiers of the Imperial Guard. A black moustache highlighted his soldierly countenance, with a broad forehead, aquiline nose, and bright red lips. Montcornet’s manner, marked by a certain superiority from his command experience, might please a woman sensible enough not to aim at making a slave of her husband. The Colonel smiled as he looked at the lawyer, one of his favorite college friends, whose small figure made it necessary for Montcornet to look down slightly as he answered his raillery with a friendly glance.

Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon was a young Provencal favored by Napoleon; his fate might lead to a splendid embassy. He had won the Emperor with his Italian suppleness, a talent for intrigue, and a knowledge of manners, which are good substitutes for the higher qualities of a sterling man. Though young and eager, his face had acquired the rigid brilliancy of tinned iron, a characteristic of diplomatists that allows them to conceal emotions. The heart of a diplomat may be an insoluble problem, as the most illustrious ambassadors of the time were distinguished by enduring hatreds and romantic attachments.

Martial, however, was one of those men capable of reckoning on the future amid their enjoyment; he had learned to judge the world and hid his ambition under the guise of a lady-killer, cloaking his talent under the commonplace of mediocrity as he observed the rapid advancement of those who gave the master little umbrage.

The two friends parted with a cordial handshake. The introductory tune, signaling the ladies to form squares for a fresh quadrille, cleared the men from the space they had filled while talking in the large drawing-room of Gondreville’s mansion. Their whispered conversation had taken place during the usual interval between dances. The chandeliers and flambeaux on the chimney-shelf illuminated their faces, failing to conceal the faint expressions of their feelings from the keen-sighted countess or the artless stranger. This espionage of thoughts is perhaps one of the pleasures idle people find in society, while many disappointed individuals are bored without daring to admit it.

To fully appreciate the interest of this conversation, it’s necessary to relate an incident that would serve as an invisible bond, drawing together the actors in this little drama, currently scattered through the rooms.

At about eleven o’clock, just as the dancers were returning to their seats, the company observed the entrance of the handsomest woman in Paris, the queen of fashion, the only person missing from the brilliant assembly. She made it a rule never to appear until a party reached a pitch of excitement that doesn’t allow women to preserve the freshness of their faces or dresses. This brief hour is the springtime of a ball. An hour later, when pleasure falls flat and fatigue sets in, everything is spoiled. Madame de Vaudremont never committed the blunder of remaining at a party to be seen with drooping flowers, hair out of curl, tumbled frills, and a face courting sleep—not always without success. She took care not to let her beauty be seen drowsy, as her rivals did; she cleverly maintained her reputation for smartness by always leaving a ballroom in brilliant order, as she had entered it. Women whispered with envy that she planned and wore as many different dresses as parties she attended in one evening.

On this occasion, Madame de Vaudremont was not destined to leave when she wished. Pausing on the threshold, she shot swift, observant glances at the women present, hastily scrutinizing their dresses to assure herself that her own eclipsed them all.

The illustrious beauty presented herself to the admiration of the crowd at the same moment as one of the bravest colonels of the Guards’ Artillery and the Emperor’s favorite, the Comte de Soulanges. The transient association of these two had an air of mystery. Upon hearing the names announced, a few women sitting by the wall rose, and men hurried in from side-rooms, pressing forward to the principal doorway. One of the jesters in the assembly remarked that “women have as much curiosity about seeing a man faithful to his passion as men do in studying a woman difficult to enthrall.”

Though the Comte de Soulanges, a young man of about thirty-two, possessed a nervous temperament that gives rise to fine qualities, his slender build and pale complexion were not initially attractive. His black eyes revealed great vivacity, but he was taciturn in company, and nothing in his appearance hinted at the oratory that would later distinguish him in the legislative assembly under the Restoration.

The Comtesse de Vaudremont, a tall woman, somewhat plump, with dazzlingly white skin and a small head carried well, inspired love through her gracious manner. She was one of those beings who keep all the promise of their beauty.

The pair, for a few minutes, became the center of general observation, but did not long give curiosity a chance to exercise itself. The Colonel and the Countess seemed to understand that accident had placed them in an awkward position. Martial, as they approached, hastened to join a group of men by the fireplace, watching Madame de Vaudremont with the jealous anxiety of a first flame of passion from behind a barrier of heads. A secret voice warned him that the success he prided himself on might be precarious. However, the coldly polite smile with which the Countess thanked Monsieur de Soulanges and her little bow of dismissal as she sat down by Madame de Gondreville relaxed the rigidity of his face, which jealousy had made tense. Seeing Soulanges still standing near the sofa where Madame de Vaudremont sat, apparently unaware of the glance that conveyed to him that they were both playing a ridiculous part, the volcanic Provencal knitted his brows and smoothed his chestnut curls to maintain his composure, watching the faces of the Countess and M. de Soulanges while still chatting with his neighbors. He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their acquaintance, but he listened without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.

Soulanges gazed calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep around the immense ballroom, admiring the display of diamonds, rubies, gold, and shining hair, which almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cut glass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s indifference unsettled the lawyer. Unable to control his impatience, Martial approached Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. Upon seeing the Provencal, Soulanges cast him a covert glance and impertinently turned away his head. A solemn silence reigned in the room, where curiosity peaked. Eager faces wore strange mixed expressions; everyone anticipated an outbreak that men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly, the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor to hide his agitation. Spotting the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and the company thought Soulanges had publicly surrendered his position out of fear of the ridicule that follows a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened so inattentively that he missed her words spoken behind her fan:

“Martial, please don’t wear that ring you took from me. I have my reasons and will explain them when we leave. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”

“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.

“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”

Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. It was then that the little blue lady became the object of curiosity that agitated the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.

When the friends parted after their challenge, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the intoxication dancing produces in a woman and the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress, which enhances their attractions as much as it does women’s, Martial thought he might yield to the charm that drew his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he managed to hide his first glances towards the lady in blue from the Countess’s watchful eyes, he was soon caught in the act; and while he excused himself once for his absent-mindedness, he could not justify the unseemly silence that followed the most insinuating question a woman can ask:

“Do you like me very much this evening?”

The more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.

While Martial danced, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting the good humor of even the most indifferent, he resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed at liberty to ask her the name of the mysterious lady. He noticed a little space cleared between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several chairs vacant, forming rows of fortifications held by mothers or middle-aged women; the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade of shawls and wraps. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and from one to another, through compliments, he finally reached the empty space next to the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dripping wax, to Martial’s annoyance.

The Colonel, too tactful to speak suddenly to the little blue lady on his right, began by addressing a plain woman seated on the left:

“This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! Every woman here is pretty! You’re not dancing—because you don’t care for it, no doubt.”

This vapid conversation aimed to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; however, she, silent and absent-minded, paid no attention. The officer had prepared several phrases leading to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped for great things. But he was surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing at Madame de Vaudremont.

“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked hesitantly.

“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.

“And your husband is here, of course?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”

The mournful lady smiled sadly.

“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will ensure not to bring you back here. When so many are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball that your beauty may claim.”

“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”

The curt tone of her replies was so discouraging that the Colonel found himself compelled to withdraw. Martial, guessing what the officer’s last request had been and the refusal he had met with, began to smile, stroking his chin and making the diamond on his finger sparkle.

“What are you laughing at?” asked the Comtesse de Vaudremont.

“At the poor Colonel’s failure, who just put his foot in it----”

“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.

“I did not hear you.”

“If you can hear nothing this evening, at least you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, vexed.

“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.

“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”

“I thank you for telling me his name,” she said; “he seems an agreeable man.”

“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”

“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” asked the lady, looking inquiringly at the Colonel.

“On the very best.”

The unknown turned pale.

“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”

“I fancied Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering from the suppressed grief that had clouded her beauty.

“For a week past, the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he’s still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”

“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone that signified dismissal.

At that moment, the quadrille was coming to an end. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, telling himself, “She is married.”

“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe the fresh air from the garden, “how are you getting on?”

“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”

“What does that matter?”

“Oh, deuce take it! I’m a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no intention of courting a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me she did not intend to dance.”

“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”

“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile, I’m going to look for Soulanges; he might know the lady, as she seems interested in him.”

“You’ve lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”

“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I’m an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”

With these words, the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges sitting at a bouillotte table. Though there was no friendship between the two soldiers beyond the superficial comradeship from the perils of war and service, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was alarmed to see the Colonel of Artillery, known to be prudent, playing a game that could lead to ruin. The heaps of gold and notes piled on the fateful cards showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood around the players at the table. Occasionally, a few words were spoken—pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken—but the five motionless men seemed to converse only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, approached him, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table, completely cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he didn’t even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, defying fortune rather than being grateful for her favors.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” He thought he was helping by pulling him away from the gaming table. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”

“What is that?” asked Soulanges.

“That you will answer a question.”

The Comte de Soulanges abruptly rose, carelessly gathering his winnings into his handkerchief, his expression so fierce that no one objected to his departure. In fact, the players seemed relieved to see his morose face disappear from the light of the gaming table.

“Those soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” remarked a diplomat who took Soulanges’ place. A single tired face turned to the newcomer and said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”

“My dear fellow,” Montcornet said to Soulanges, leading him aside, “the Emperor praised you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”

“The Master does not love the Artillery.”

“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said that men who married in Paris during the campaign are not in disgrace. Well?”

Soulanges looked confused.

“And now I hope you will tell me if you know a charming woman sitting under a huge candelabrum----”

At this, the Count’s face brightened; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” he said, his voice changed, “if anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull with this gold. Leave me, I beg you. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than----I hate everything I see. In fact, I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”

“My poor friend!” Montcornet replied gently, squeezing the Count’s hand. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you that Martial is thinking so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is quite smitten with that little lady?”

“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling with rage, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!”

He sank into an easy chair, and Montcornet slowly left, realizing Soulanges was too furious for pleasantries to soothe him.

Returning to the ballroom, Montcornet first noticed Madame de Vaudremont, who appeared agitated. He took a seat next to her.

“I wager something has vexed you?” he said.

“A mere trifle, General. I want to leave for a ball at the Grand Duchess of Berg’s, but I must first stop at the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is amusing himself by flirting with the dowagers.”

“That’s not the whole secret of your disturbance. I bet a hundred louis you will stay here the whole evening.”

“Impertinent man!”

“Then I’ve hit the truth?”

“Well, tell me, what am I thinking?” she asked, tapping his fingers with her fan. “I might reward you if you guess right.”

“I won’t accept the challenge; I have too much advantage.”

“You are presumptuous.”

“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet----”

“Of whom?” she exclaimed, feigning surprise.

“Of that candelabrum,” he replied, glancing at the fair stranger and scrutinizing the Countess.

“You have guessed it,” she said, hiding her face behind her fan. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as an old monkey, just told me that Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is flirting with that stranger, who sits here like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face, so cruelly beautiful and pale. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac, who only goes to a ball to watch everything while pretending to sleep, has made me miserable. Martial shall pay dearly for this trick. Urge him, since he is your friend, not to make me unhappy.”

“I just left a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that lady. And he is a man, madame, to keep his word. But I know Martial; such threats encourage him. Besides, we have wagered----” The Colonel lowered his voice.

“Can it be true?” asked the Countess.

“On my word of honor.”

“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” Madame de Vaudremont replied, her glance inviting.

“Will you do me the honor of dancing with me?”

“Yes; but the next quadrille. During this one, I want to find out what will come of this little intrigue and ascertain who the little blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”

Understanding Madame de Vaudremont wished to be alone, the Colonel retired, pleased to have made a good start.

At most entertainments, women like Madame de Lansac gather like old sailors watching younger mariners struggle with the tempest. At this moment, Madame de Lansac, interested in the unfolding drama, could easily guess the Countess’s agitation. The lady might fan herself gracefully, smile at the young men who bowed to her, and employ all the arts to hide her emotion, but the Duchess, a clear-sighted and mischievous figure from the previous century, could read her heart.

The old lady detected the slightest movements revealing the soul’s impressions. The faintest frown, quiver of cheeks, or curl of lips was like a book to her. From her large armchair, filled by her dress, the coquette of the past admired the younger coquette, seeing how bravely she disguised her annoyance and grief. Madame de Vaudremont felt as much sorrow as she feigned cheerfulness; she had believed Martial was a man of talent who could adorn her life with power, but now she realized her mistake, injurious to her reputation and self-esteem. Her sudden passion for Martial, though recent, caused her acute pain, akin to the amputation of a healthy limb.

The old Duchess, watching for an opportunity to speak to the Countess, hastily dismissed her Ambassador; in comparison to a lover’s quarrel, every other interest paled. Madame de Lansac shot a sardonic glance at the younger lady, making the Countess fear her fate was in the dowager’s hands. There are looks between women that are like torches at the climax of a tragedy. No one who did not know the Duchess could appreciate the terror her expression inspired in the Countess.

Madame de Lansac was tall, and her features led people to say, “That must have been a handsome woman!” She coated her cheeks with rouge, making wrinkles scarcely visible, but her eyes looked dim. She wore many diamonds and dressed tastefully. Her sharp nose promised epigrams, and her well-fitted teeth preserved a smile of irony reminiscent of Voltaire. The exquisite politeness of her manners softened her mischievous mind, making it impossible to accuse her of spitefulness.

The old woman’s eyes lit up, and a triumphant glance, accompanied by a smile that said, “I promised you as much!” crossed the room, bringing a blush of hope to the pale cheeks of the young woman languishing under the chandelier. The alliance between Madame de Lansac and the stranger did not escape the practiced eye of the Comtesse de Vaudremont, who sensed a mystery and was determined to uncover it.

At this moment, the Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned all the dowagers without success about the blue lady’s name, turned in despair to the Comtesse de Gondreville, who replied unsatisfactorily, “A lady whom the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”

By chance, the lawyer caught the glance of intelligence the Duchess sent to the stranger. Despite being on bad terms with her, he decided to speak. The Duchess, seeing the jaunty Baron approaching, smiled with sardonic irony and looked at Madame de Vaudremont, which made Montcornet laugh.

“If the old witch pretends to be friendly,” thought the Baron, “she is certainly going to play me some spiteful trick.” “Madame,” he said, “you have, I am told, undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”

“Do you take me for a dragon?” said the old lady. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added sweetly, reviving Martial’s hopes.

“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of all these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”

“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, whom you men do not know; she goes nowhere.”

“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature. May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you will tell me all I want to know, I promise to urge a petition for the restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands on the Emperor.”

The younger branch of the house of Navarreins bears the arms of Lansac, and the Duchess’s liaison with Louis XV had earned her husband the title of duke. The young lawyer’s suggestion to petition for an estate belonging to the elder branch was sheer trickery.

“Monsieur,” the old woman said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room has reached the same curiosity as yours. All eyes are involuntarily turned towards the corner where my protégée has modestly placed herself; she is reaping all the homage the women wished to deprive her of. Happy the man she chooses for her partner!” She interrupted herself, fixing her eyes on Madame de Vaudremont with a look that plainly said, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from the lips of your handsome Countess than from mine.”

There was such marked defiance in the Duchess’s attitude that Madame de Vaudremont rose, approached her, and took the chair Martial placed for her. Without acknowledging him, she said, “I can guess, madame, that you are talking of me; but I admit my lack of perspicacity; I do not know whether it is for good or evil.”

Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s pretty hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and replied in a low, compassionate tone, “Poor child!”

The women looked at each other. Madame de Vaudremont understood that Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious expression, saying, “Leave us.”

The Baron, displeased to see the Countess under the spell of the dangerous sibyl, gave one of those looks a man can give—potent over a blinded heart but ridiculous in the eyes of a woman beginning to criticize the man who attracted her.

“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” said Madame de Vaudremont, turning to give an ironical sidelong gaze at the lawyer.

Martial, a man of the world with wit and acumen, would not risk breaking with a woman favored at Court, whom the Emperor wished to see married. He counted on the jealousy he intended to provoke in her as the surest means of discovering the secret of her coolness and withdrew willingly, as a new quadrille began.

With an air of making room for dancing, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two ladies talk. He followed the glances they directed at the stranger. Comparing the Countess with the new beauty, made attractive by mystery, the Baron fell prey to the self-interest common to adventurous lady-killers, hesitating between a fortune within reach and indulging his caprice. The blaze of light highlighted his anxious, sullen face against the white silk hangings, making him resemble an evil genius. Observers likely remarked, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”

Meanwhile, the Colonel, leaning against the doorway between the ballroom and the cardroom, laughed undetected under his moustache.
