He enjoyed watching the dance’s turmoil, reading the agitation in the faces of the Countess and Martial, and wondering about the connection between Soulanges’ gloomy looks and the fair unknown’s expression, which alternated between hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles from many beautiful women. As a Colonel of the Imperial Guard, he was one of the best matches in the army.

It was nearly midnight. The conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, interests, rivalries, and scheming had reached a fervor that made a young man exclaim, “A fine ball!”

“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you are enduring a thousand deaths, I thought I might give you some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty means spoiling your future; it is like tearing the gown you must wear. My dear, it is not much later that we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, sweetheart, making clever enemies and friends without sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”

“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly exclaimed.

“My child, at your age, you must learn to choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not wise enough to be a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to squander your fortune; still, that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left him. In three years, he will be a wreck. Then he will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business acumen and be able to gossip agreeably, but he is too presumptuous to have any real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is calculating you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught a second time in the same trap? A new alliance should be a good speculation on your part, and in marrying again, you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Marechale!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As she spoke, both women naturally focused on Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.

“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “then you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passions. I came here to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve turned into a lovesick fool for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married for two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has experienced remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come and see the result of your actions.”

The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.

“There,” said Madame de Lansac, indicating a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child gives her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”

The Countess looked away, lost in sad thoughts.

The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”

The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. Players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amid festivity, struck the Countess with foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not completely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.

“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”

“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.

“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.

“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.

“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.

“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.

Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”

“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”

“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”

“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”

“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”

“But she loves her husband.”

“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”

“But she is married.”

“A whimsical objection!”

“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”

“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”

“You deserve that I should send you to her.”

“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”

“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”

“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”

He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.

At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.

Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.

“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”

This thought stirred her pride more than curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control. The Colonel, misreading her embarrassment, became more ardent. Old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues. The passions of the two couples were evident in the crowded rooms, reflected in the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of impotence.

Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty up close, captivated by her fresh neck and graceful figure. At that time, women wore sashes tied under the bosom, a style that flattered those with perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.

“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”

“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.

Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.

“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.

“Yes, very much, monsieur.”

Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred something in him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”

Artless confusion colored her cheeks.

“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”

“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”

“Precisely.”

“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through their physical grace. Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the most brilliant women prioritized dancing above all. As the orchestra played, the Baron felt immense pride surveying the ladies forming the square. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled even Madame de Vaudremont’s, who stood opposite him and the lady in blue. All eyes briefly turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur showed she was the topic among the men. The admiration and envy made the young woman blush, enhancing her charm. When she raised her eyes, she looked at her partner, as if wishing to transfer the glory of admiration to him, showing she valued his opinion above all others. She blended innocence and vanity, displaying a budding affection that youthful hearts possess. As she danced, onlookers might have believed she showcased her grace solely for Martial; though modest and inexperienced in ballroom arts, she knew how to glance at him at just the right moments.

When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial dismissed the concern: “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”

After the quadrille, whispers circulated. the less attractive women made moral comments about Martial and the Comtesse, while the more beautiful marveled at her apparent ease in surrendering to him. The men were baffled by the Baron’s luck, not finding him particularly charming. A few sympathetic women warned against judging the Countess harshly, suggesting young wives would be in a dire situation if a mere glance or graceful dance could compromise a reputation.

Only Martial understood his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the moulinet, he clasped the Countess’s hand, feeling her response through the thin glove. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only benefit you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”

Madame de Soulanges yielded to his charm, and they walked through the crowded rooms. She hesitated at each door, peering in before entering, only reassured when he said, “He is not here.” They reached a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper approached, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, draped with flowers and blue hangings. The noise faded. Initially startled, the Countess firmly resisted following him but eventually sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they could be seen.

“This room is charming,” she remarked, admiring the decor. “All here is love and delight!” Martial, deeply moved, noticed her face in the dim light: a mix of unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, which seemed to quell her internal struggle. Flirtatiously she took his left hand and removed the ring he wore, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”

Martial, intoxicated by her touch, looked at her with eyes as bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He trailed off, captivated by her rapture. She asked, “You give it to me?” with surprise. “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured her.

She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her, she suddenly stood and said clearly, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.”

“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied with a smile. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring, revealing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair, then fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. He noticed the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughing nearby, and the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial took their banter in stride, grateful for their silence regarding the evening’s events, as he exchanged his horse for a young wife.

As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris to her home in Faubourg Saint-Germain, she was consumed by anxiety. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville, she searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her mind. A silent witness to her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont had ensnared him, she had hoped for his repentance. Now she feared she had made a grave mistake by agreeing to her aunt’s scheme.

The evening darkened her innocent spirit. Initially alarmed by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed upon seeing her rival’s beauty, feeling the weight of societal corruption. As she crossed the Pont Royal, she discarded the hair from the diamond, a token of pure affection. Tears flowed as she recalled her long-standing grief, and she shuddered at the thought that a woman seeking peace must bury her pain without complaint.

“Alas!” she pondered, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.”

Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”

“Oh, my dear Leon,” she coaxed, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!”

Her words softened the Count’s stern demeanor, for he had been dreading her return, fearing she would learn of his infidelity. In a bid to deflect her anger, he had tried to be the first to criticize her. Delighted to see him smile and grateful for his presence in a room he had frequented less often, the Countess looked at him tenderly, blushing and lowering her eyes. Her kindness further enchanted Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully; gratitude often accompanies love.

“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”

General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding, as she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In September 1835, Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. The Marquis had squandered much of his fortune, but Clementine was a great heiress through her mother’s family; settlements by relatives ensured her a yearly income on marriage.

Comte Adam, though an exile, was no burden on the French government. He came from an illustrious Polish family but faced prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was not highly regarded. Initially regarded as a student enjoying Paris after losing political prospects, Adam navigated a divided Polish community in France—split between republican and noble exiles—and became the target of Parisian humor, with some questioning his wealth and status.

In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he was established in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clementine. Their marriage elevated his status; he was soon recognized as fashionable. The couple’s home was a luxurious creation, blending various architectural styles and filled with exquisite decor, reflecting the wealth and tastes of the new bourgeoisie.

Clementine, now a countess, began to notice the mysterious presence of Captain Paz, a factotum who managed their affairs yet remained largely invisible. She questioned Adam about Paz, who was a constant figure in their lives, handling everything from finances to horse care. Adam explained that Paz came from a poor branch of the Paz family, had fought bravely for Poland, and had become a close friend through shared hardships.

Paz’s loyalty and dedication were evident; he ran the household with remarkable efficiency and discretion. Adam admired Paz’s character, insisting their friendship rested on mutual respect and understanding. Despite social hierarchies, Adam regarded Paz as an equal; their bond had been strengthened by shared experiences and challenges.

Clementine expressed admiration for Paz, recognizing his noble spirit and simplicity. Adam recounted once offering Paz a financial gift, which Paz declined—an example of the depth of their friendship and commitment. Their relationship transcended typical social boundaries, with both men valuing their connection over societal expectations.

As the couple prepared for a day out, Clementine’s affection for both Adam and Paz grew, highlighting the unique dynamics among them. The narrative emphasizes loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th-century Paris, showing how personal ties can defy social norms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Paz lived a reclusive life, largely unnoticed by Parisian society, until the Comtesse Laginska was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. During their outing, Clémentine insisted Thaddeus dine with them, prompting him to dress for the occasion. At dinner he behaved like a subordinate, listening to Clémentine and answering minimally, which frustrated Adam, who urged him to be more lively. Despite his dull demeanor, Thaddeus was keenly aware of his feelings for Clémentine, which he struggled to conceal.

After dinner Thaddeus agreed to go to the Opera, where he remained detached and seemingly uninterested. Clémentine, feeling slighted, remarked on his lack of engagement, while Adam defended him, suggesting Thaddeus was simply being disagreeable. Thaddeus, however, was grappling with unrequited love for Clémentine, a feeling that intensified after her return from a trip. He felt a bittersweet joy in being indispensable to their household, which would falter without his management.

Clémentine, now more beautiful and confident in her marriage, focused on her social standing in Paris and had little space for thoughts of Thaddeus. Yet she noticed him one day while driving home and invited him to join them, which flustered him. Their interactions were charged with unspoken emotions; Thaddeus felt a mix of gratitude and longing.

As days passed Thaddeus returned to routine, feeling overlooked by Clémentine, who was consumed by social obligations. He found solace in his role managing their affairs, but his feelings remained a source of inner turmoil. He reflected on the nature of his love, recognizing its depth and the pain of being unreciprocated.

One day Clémentine hosted a reception and Thaddeus’s absence went unnoticed. He resumed solitary habits, content in his silent devotion, yet deeply affected by the realization that his love was both joy and suffering. He admired her from afar, cherishing little moments of her happiness.

Clémentine’s Parisian life was a constant battle for attention; she became increasingly distracted by rivals. Despite her efforts to maintain status she felt the strain and often neglected her relationship with Adam. Thaddeus remained a steadfast presence, quietly managing their affairs while wrestling with his feelings.

In May, as Clémentine prepared to leave for her uncle’s estate, she spotted Thaddeus again and their brief exchange revived her curiosity. She insisted he join them for dinner; though he hesitated, he complied. During the meal he tried to engage more, but shyness and duty kept him from fully expressing himself.

As the evening progressed Clémentine’s interest in Thaddeus grew and she began to see him differently. Their conversation revealed a deeper connection, and Thaddeus felt momentarily emboldened. Yet his loyalty to Adam held him back from pursuing his feelings openly.

After dinner Clémentine voiced dissatisfaction with Adam’s behavior and a desire for more passion. Thaddeus, torn between love for her and friendship with Adam, struggled with the complexity of the situation. He recognized the tension and felt the urge to confess but hesitated.

Clémentine’s insecurities led her to question love and loyalty. Thaddeus sensed her vulnerability and felt hope but remained cautious. Their talk deepened and the silence between them grew charged with unspoken emotion.

To divert attention, Thaddeus fabricated a story about being in love with a circus performer named Malaga, portraying her as captivating and agile, a contrast to Parisian women. The ruse let him express longing for a simpler, more genuine connection without revealing his true feelings for Clémentine.

Thaddeus’s visits to Malaga became an escape from his unrequited love, though he remained emotionally distant. He provided for her, ensuring she lived comfortably, yet did not enter a romantic relationship. His feelings for Clémentine continued to haunt him, leaving him torn between loyalty to Adam and desire for Clémentine.

As Malaga’s fortunes changed she began to question Thaddeus’s intentions, producing misunderstandings and tension. Despite these challenges he stayed committed as her benefactor while wrestling with his own heart.

In the end Thaddeus received a heartfelt letter from Malaga expressing despair over their separation and longing for his companionship. The letter struck a chord, reminding him of the depth of his own feelings for Clémentine. He realized his devotion was unwavering even as he navigated the intricacies of love, loyalty, and sacrifice.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess spoke about Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus's misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his relationship with a circus performer. Clementine expressed disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga.

Paz, who had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, was seen by the countess as a potential ruin for Adam. Despite her concerns, Adam believed Thaddeus would eventually leave Malaga if it served his interests; the countess remained skeptical.

Days later Paz noticed a change in Clementine's demeanor toward him, sensing her contempt. This plunged him into despair, convinced he had caused her to despise him. Despite his heartache, he found joy in silently admiring her at social events and devoted himself to her well-being and the household.

Paz's love for Clementine was profound, yet he felt unworthy of her respect. He fell ill from his emotional turmoil but later recovered. Adam then lost a significant sum gambling and asked Thaddeus to cover it by attributing the loss to Malaga. Thaddeus reluctantly agreed, feeling the weight of obligations.

Clementine discovered Thaddeus's financial generosity toward Paz and voiced her frustration, questioning his loyalty. Thaddeus, in turn, felt burdened by her expectations and the need to manage her affairs. He resolved to teach her household management, believing she could handle it independently.

As spring approached, Adam fell seriously ill and Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him. Clementine, aware of Adam's precarious state, sought the best medical care. Thaddeus's dedication was unwavering; through attentive care he managed to keep the count alive.

After weeks of nursing Adam improved, and Thaddeus received praise for his efforts. Yet he was tormented by his feelings for Clementine and the realization he could never have her. Concluding that leaving was the only escape from unrequited love, he decided to go to the Caucasus.

Before departing Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, confessing his deep feelings and the sacrifices he had made for her happiness. He acknowledged that staying in Paris would only prolong his pain.

Clementine, on receiving the letter, reflected on her feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus's sacrifices, lamented the departure, believing their bond could have withstood trials.

In the years that followed Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. She remained unaware of his true feelings and the depth of his devotion, while he lived in the shadows, forever changed by his love. As time passed Clementine was drawn into a new romantic entanglement, but the memory of Thaddeus lingered—a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she preserves a virtuous façade to support her husband's political prospects. While some women await his elevation to peerage before judging him, the marquise's conduct is strictly principled.

The marquis is a bland figure, known more for faults than virtues. A silent deputy who votes correctly but lacks charisma, he has earned the nickname "dull weather." His unremarkable nature makes him a safe match for a virtuous woman, being incapable of folly.

Despite a reserved manner, the marquise elicits both respect and disdain from men who attempt to flirt with her, receiving only contempt in return. She converses with agreeable men without attracting gossip, unlike others with ulterior motives. She does not play coy; her behavior is genuinely principled.

The narrator, an admirer, sketches her appearance: neither plain nor conventionally pretty, she possesses a captivating presence. Her animated eyes reveal a deeper soul, and she exudes charm without pursuing it. This quality shows in her recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man combining modesty and vivacity.

Rastignac, skilled at navigating society, speaks with the marquise at a ball about topics from opera to women's duties. Their exchange reflects mutual interest, though he abruptly leaves her afterward. The next morning Rastignac, still preoccupied by their talk, reflects on it as he writes letters in a comfortable setting, increasingly absorbed by thoughts of her.

He imagines the pleasures of romance and is interrupted by his friend Horace, with whom he discusses various matters, including a possible expedition to Morea.

Meanwhile, the marquise receives a letter from Rastignac while preparing for the day. It begins with affectionate terms that spark her curiosity. Initially inclined to discard it, she instead reads on, wanting to understand his sentiments. The letter's conclusion leaves her fatigued as she contemplates the impertinence of his tone and the implications of his words.

This moment captures the tension between her virtuous exterior and emerging emotion, foreshadowing the complexities of her character and the dynamics of her relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac and resolved to show him more than disdain in society, deeming his insolence beyond what she had tolerated. She had considered keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it.

"Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline told the housemaid, who was surprised.

That evening Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant, expecting Rastignac. By two in the morning she realized he was not coming, despite intending to confront him with coldness. Stendhal called this mental process "crystallization."

Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet Joseph for misplacing letters. Joseph, distracted, recalled one was for Madame de Listomere and another for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned the letter was not for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed, recognizing his heart had strayed.

Eugene's mistakes began with his plan to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the blunder, then worsened by not visiting for several days, allowing her resentment to crystallize.

When he finally called, the porter said Madame de Listomere was out. The Marquis de Listomere, unaware of the tension, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs, Rastignac noted several social blunders.

On seeing Eugene, Madame de Listomere blushed, which he noticed. He thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere, absorbed in his newspaper, was oblivious to the undercurrent.

Despite initial embarrassment, Madame de Listomere quickly regained composure. When Eugene asked about her health she said she was well. He suggested that if his visit was unwelcome it was because of the marquis's introduction; she replied her husband did not share all her secrets, hinting at a complex marriage.

Trying to ease the mood, Eugene said silence would be the best excuse for his visit. She promised forgetfulness while keeping a dignified air. When he mentioned the letter she feigned offense but could not hide amusement.

Eugene insisted no offense was intended and the letter was not meant for her. Madame de Listomere, pretending to be offended, said she could laugh at the situation but warned him against further mistakes.

Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted to mention a mutual acquaintance's death. This led to a discussion that exposed the marquise's irritation with Eugene's earlier comments. He naively suggested she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only increased her curiosity.

When Eugene brought up Madame de Nucingen, Madame de Listomere's demeanor shifted. She rang for wood, signaling it was time for him to leave, and coldly confronted him about the letter, asking how his pen could have written her name. Embarrassed, Eugene stumbled and departed.

Days later Madame de Listomere confirmed his claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, while her physician recognized it as a nervous condition she used to justify seclusion.

The episode highlights the complexity of social interactions and misunderstandings in romantic contexts, revealing characters' emotional responses and social roles.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

At Paris, balls often feature two gatherings: the official party, where guests exchange superficial pleasantries, and a more intimate meeting afterward, where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, often hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, preserves the old spirit of French wit, allowing free-flowing ideas and camaraderie.

One memorable evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon, a group of notable individuals, including the Minister de Marsay, engaged in lively discourse. The atmosphere brimmed with wit and ease, showcasing the conversational style Paris is known for. Anecdotes and confessions flowed freely, creating a delightful tapestry of social interaction.

As the night progressed, the talk turned to de Marsay's reflections on his early experiences with love, particularly a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He recounted youthful infatuation marked by intense devotion and secrecy. Despite his fervor, he maintained a facade of indifference to shield their relationship from society's prying eyes.

De Marsay described the complexities of their love as a careful dance of appearances and emotion. He told how he would ride past her carriage, exchanging silent signals through the flowers she carried. Their relationship thrived on mutual admiration and the thrill of secrecy, yet he remained blissfully unaware of any potential betrayal.

A turning point came when he fell ill and sent her a note to cancel their meeting. Overcome with jealousy and suspicion, he decided to visit her despite his condition. Upon arriving, he saw her with another man, which ignited a storm of emotion. Still, her calm demeanor and tender concern momentarily soothed his doubts.

De Marsay's narrative darkened as he grappled with the realization of betrayal. He felt a profound shift within himself, marking the end of his innocence and the start of a more cynical view of love. He reflected on revenge and the complexities of passion, ultimately concluding that true love should transcend the desire for vengeance.

His story resonated with his audience, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the societal expectations that shape relationships. De Marsay's experience served as a poignant reminder of the fragility of trust and the transformative power of love, leaving a lasting impression on those present.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Cured of my cold and my pure love, I embarked on an adventure with a charming heroine, whose beauty sharply contrasted with that of my deceptive angel. I was careful not to quarrel with this clever woman, doubting whether true love could match the delights of her skilled deceit. Such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue.

I sought a skilled artist in the Rue Boucher to create a keepsake from my hair for my new angel. He monopolized hair mementos and showed me work surpassing fairy tales, describing trends like marking linen with hair. Suspicion rose when I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, confirming my doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections. I left the shop still believing in pleasure but skeptical about love.

Two months later, I was in the boudoir of the ethereal being, holding her hand as we explored the depths of sentiment. In a moment of tenderness, I seized the chance to elicit her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed love, claiming I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally.

The women listening to de Marsay seemed offended by his portrayal of their airs and attitudes. Just as I might have believed her sweet falsehoods, I asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” The directness startled her; her reaction revealed guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him, as he was rich and devout.

Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me. I urged her to be truthful, and she swore before God. I confessed doubts about her fidelity, which she quickly turned against me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape the confrontation, but curiosity compelled her to ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke multiple times; she dismissed it as jealousy.

I explained that her marriage to the Duke would elevate her status in society and advised her to embrace her new role, as he was virtuous and would expect propriety. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had only spoken sooner, we could have been happy. I maintained it was too late and she had gone too far with the Duke.

She worried about the Duke’s suspicions; I reassured her he likely had none but that she needed to prove devotion. As she paced the boudoir, she shifted demeanor, held out her hand, and declared I was noble and charming, vowing never to forget me.

Her tactics were admirable, and I played the distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed I had made advances to another woman. This startled Charlotte, who accused me of being unfaithful. I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer.

Charlotte expressed disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but pointed out that taking love seriously in society was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess.

Her bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat.

The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay maintained a slight smile. The conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley noted that women often did not see their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, saying duchesses and marquises were disappearing and the Civil Code had leveled social hierarchies.

Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay observed that countesses might survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women.

The Comte de Vandenesse commented on how the current structure allowed anyone to claim nobility, while Lord Dudley noted property had not been organized, leading to the aristocracy’s decline.

Joseph Bridau observed that the beauty of womanhood had diminished and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez noted that the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who could have shaped society.

The discussion turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the era, embodying good taste and grace but lacking the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen explained that a woman’s worth was no longer tied to lineage but to character and presentation.

Count Adam Laginski sought clarification on what constituted a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described the ideal woman as exuding elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration. He painted a vivid picture of her appearance and demeanor, emphasizing grace and poise in various social settings.

The dialogue highlighted the complexities of modern femininity: the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, blending beauty and intellect to captivate those around her. The guests considered whether this new type of woman compensated for the vanished greatness of earlier aristocratic women, or merely reflected a reshaped society where merit and presentation replaced inherited splendour.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Now, you will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet remarked gravely. “In contrast, even if you know a lady is married, she will skillfully conceal her husband, making him difficult to find. Often you only notice her looking at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and departs. She has ordered her carriage and leaves.

“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed beneath a golden canopy of a delightful dream, which may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates.

“At home, the lady sees no one before four; she is shrewd enough to keep you waiting. Her house is tastefully arranged; her luxury is for daily use and regularly renewed. You will find no dusty glass shades or remnants of wrappings. The staircase is warm, flowers abound—her only accepted gift and only from chosen people, since they symbolize a promise. Fashionable trinkets lie about, but not like museum pieces. She sits by the fire in a low chair, not rising to greet you. Her conversation at home differs from that at the ball; there, she was our creditor; here, she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are nuances she masters. What she seeks is a man to widen her circle, an object for her attentions. To draw you into her drawing-room, she will be charming. This shows how isolated women are today and why they want a little world of their own where they can shine like a constellation. Conversation there often relies on generalities.

“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have caught the flaw of our age. The epigram—a volume in a word—no longer strikes at people or things but at sordid events, fading quickly.”

“Hence,” Blondet continued, “a lady’s intelligence, if she has any, lies in doubting everything. Here is the difference between two women; the townswoman is virtuous, while the lady hesitates and contends with her virtue. This uncertainty is one of the last graces left to her by our dreadful times. She rarely goes to church yet will discuss religion; if you express free thought, she will try to convert you, sliding into the familiar phrases and gestures of such women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society is tottering, and you deprive it of its support. Religion means you and me; it is property and the future of our children! Let us not be selfish! Individualism is the disease of the age, and religion is the only remedy; it unites families which your laws put asunder,’ and so on. She then drifts into a neo-Christian discourse laced with political notions—neither Catholic nor Protestant—but moral? Oh, very moral!—a muddle of modern doctrines at odds with each other.”

The women laughed at Blondet’s satire.

“This explanation, dear Count Adam,” Blondet said to the Pole, “shows that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies both intellectual and political confusion, surrounded by flashy yet fleeting products of an industry that replaces its work with something new. When you leave her, you think: She certainly has superior ideas! You believe it more because she has delicately probed your heart and asked your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows, even when she does. You alone feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart. The great ladies of old flaunted their affairs; today’s lady arranges her passions like a musical score, with notes and rests, pauses and sharps. A merely weak woman is anxious not to compromise her love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, position, and fortune no longer shield her from scrutiny. The aristocracy no longer protects her. Unlike the great lady of the past, she lacks lofty antagonism; she cannot crush anything; rather, she would be crushed. Thus she masters Jesuitical compromises, moving between propriety and anonymous passion. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. This woman—so free at a ball, so charming in a walk—is a slave at home; she is never independent except in privacy or theoretically. Her task is to preserve her position as a lady.

“For in our day, a woman repudiated by her husband, reduced to a meager allowance, without a carriage, luxury, or opera box, is neither wife nor maid nor townswoman; she is adrift, a chattel. The Carmelites will not accept a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover still want her? That is the question. Thus, your perfect lady may give rise to calumny, but never to slander.”

“It is all so horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.

“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ exists between English hypocrisy and the frankness of the eighteenth century—a bastard system symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere, and everything is a matter of degree; all great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes purely personal. I am convinced it is impossible for a woman, even if born near a throne, to acquire before age twenty-five the encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the important small things, the musical tones of coloring, the angelic mischief and innocent cunning, the speech and silence, the seriousness and banter, the wit and obtuseness, the diplomacy and ignorance that make up the perfect lady.”

“And where, according to your sketch,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked Blondet, “would you classify the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman comme il faut?”

“When she has no genius, she is a woman comme il n’en faut pas,” Blondet replied, emphasizing the phrase with a glance that might be praise for Camille Maupin. “This epigram is not mine, but Napoleon’s,” he added.

“You need not hold a grudge against Napoleon for that,” Canalis interjected. “He was weak in being jealous of literary genius—he had his flaws. Who can ever explain or depict Napoleon? A man with arms folded who did everything, the greatest force known, the most concentrated, the most caustic; a singular genius who spread armed civilization without fixing it anywhere; a man who could do everything because he willed everything; a prodigious phenomenon of will, conquering illness through battle, yet destined to die of disease in bed after living amid balls and bullets; a man with a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear-sighted spirit that foresaw everything but his own fall; a capricious politician who risked men for economy yet spared three heads—Talleyrand, Pozzo de Borgo, and Metternich—whose deaths might have saved the Empire; a man to whom nature, as a rare privilege, gave a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind at midnight among women, and the next morning manipulating Europe as a young girl might splash in her bath! Hypocritical and generous; loving tawdriness and simplicity; devoid of taste yet protecting the arts; and despite these contradictions, truly great by instinct or temperament; Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty; and then, like my grocer buried in Père Lachaise, a good husband and father. In short, he improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—with more range than precision. Did he not aim to make all Europe France? After making us weigh on the earth in such a way as to change the laws of gravitation, he left us poorer; he who had taken an empire by his name lost his name on the frontier of his empire in a sea of blood and soldiers. A man of thought and action, who understood Desaix and Fouché.”

“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.

“Ah! what a pleasure it is to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.

“But do you think the treat we are giving you is common?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you had to pay for the charms of conversation as you do for those of dancing or music, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”

“And are we really so much diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked, smiling skeptically at the women. “In these days, under a regime that makes everything small, you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books—does that prove women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? In all ages, passions remain the same. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—that glory, if you will—that once gave luster to the errors of some women. But even if one has not saved a King of France, one is not less an Agnès Sorel. Do you believe our Marquise d’Espard is not the equal of Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffant, in whose rooms so much evil was spoken and done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Or Malibran the equal of Saint-Huberti? Are not our poets superior to those of the eighteenth century? If, at this moment, due to the Grocers who govern us, we lack a style of our own, did not the Empire have its distinguishing stamp as the age of Louis XV. did, and was not its splendor fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”

“I completely agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” the Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear in proportions as fine as the most beautiful women of the past? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand now in Paris who certainly write better than she did and do not publish their letters. Whether the Frenchwoman is called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.

“Émile Blondet has painted the fascinations of a woman of the day; but, when needed, this creature who bristles or preens, who echoes the ideas of this or that man, can be heroic. And it must be said, your faults, mesdames, are all the more poetic because they are surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much of the world—perhaps too late; but where the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I have always observed the effects of what you may call Providence, inevitably overwhelming those we consider light women.”

“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways——”

“Oh, let the Comte de Vandenesse preach to us!” Madame de Serizy exclaimed.

“With all the more reason because he has preached a great deal by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.

“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you are very fond of,” he said, glancing at Blondet—“in which the finger of God has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was almost by my act——”

“Well, tell us all about it!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”

“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay replied, casting an eye at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.

“During the campaign of 1812,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may be of use to you, Doctor Bianchon,” turning to me, “since, while you devote yourself to the body, you concern yourself much with the mind; it may help solve some problems of the will.

“I was on my second campaign; I loved danger and laughed at everything, like the young and foolish lieutenant of artillery I was. When we reached the Beresina, the army had lost all discipline. It was a medley of men of many nations, instinctively making their way from north to south. Soldiers would drive a ragged, barefoot general away from their fire if he brought neither wood nor food. After crossing this river, disorder did not lessen. I had come quietly and alone, without food, from the marshes of Zembin, wandering in search of a house where I might be taken in. Finding none or being driven away, I finally saw a wretched Polish farm, like the wooden houses of Lower Normandy or poorest Beauce farms. These dwellings consist of a single room, one end divided by a partition as a forage store-room.

“In the twilight I saw faint smoke above the house. Hoping to find comrades more compassionate than those I had met, I approached. Inside, the table was laid. Several officers, with a woman—a common sight—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh broiled over charcoal, and frozen beetroots. I recognized artillery captains from my first regiment. I was welcomed with shouts, which would have amazed me on the other side of the Beresina; but at that moment the cold was less intense; my comrades were warm, they had food, and the straw-strewn floor promised a night’s rest. We did not ask for much then. My comrades could be philanthropists for free—one of the commonest ways of being philanthropic. I sat to eat on a straw bundle.

“At the end of the table, by the door to the hay-filled division, sat my old colonel, one of the most extraordinary men I ever met among the mixed collection of men I knew. He was Italian. Whenever human nature is truly fine in the South, it is sublime. I do not know if you have noticed the extreme fairness of Italians when fair. It is exquisite under artificial light. When I read the fantastical portrait of Colonel Oudet by Charles Nodier, I found my own sensations in Nodier’s elegant phrases. Italian, as were most officers of his regiment, borrowed from Eugene’s army, my colonel was tall, at least eight or nine inches above average, admirably proportioned—perhaps a little stout but prodigiously powerful, active, and clean-limbed like a greyhound. His abundant black curls contrasted with his complexion, as white as a woman’s; he had small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and an aquiline nose that would pinch and turn white when he was angry, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; no one could remain calm in his presence. I alone perhaps was not afraid of him; he had such a singular fancy for me that he thought everything I did was right. When angry, his brow would knit, and the muscles of his forehead form a horseshoe; this mark was perhaps even more terrifying than the magnetic flashes of his blue eyes. His whole frame quivered, and his strength, great in repose, became almost unbounded.

“He spoke with a strong guttural roll. His voice, as powerful as Nodier’s Oudet’s, put incredible fullness into the syllable or consonant where this burr sounded. Though this faulty pronunciation could sometimes charm, when commanding his men or excited, you cannot imagine, unless you heard it, the force this accent expressed. When the Colonel was calm, his blue eyes were angelically sweet and his smooth brow had a charming look. On parade or with the army of Italy, no man could compare with him. Indeed, d’Orsay himself was eclipsed by our colonel at the last review Napoleon held before the invasion of Russia.

“Everything about this exceptional man was contrast. Passion thrives on contrast. Thus, you need not ask whether he exerted over women the irresistible influence to which our nature yields”—and the general looked at the Princesse de Cadignan—“as vitreous matter is molded under the pipe of the glass-blower; yet, by singular fatality—an observer might explain—the Colonel was neither a lady-killer nor indifferent to such successes.

“To illustrate his violence, I will tell of a brief incident. We were dragging our guns up a narrow road bordered by a high slope and thickets. Halfway up, we met another artillery regiment, its colonel at the head. That colonel wanted our captain to yield, but the captain refused. The other colonel ordered his foremost battery forward; despite the driver’s effort to keep among the scrub, the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him over on the near side of his horse. It happened in an instant. Our Colonel, nearby, sensed a quarrel; he galloped up, riding among the guns at the risk of falling, and reached the spot calling ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam like champagne rose to his lips; he roared like a lion. Incapable even of a word or cry, he made a terrifying signal, pointing to the wood and drawing his sword. The two colonels stepped aside. In two seconds, we saw his opponent stretched on the ground, his skull split in two. The soldiers of that regiment backed away—yes, quickly.

“The captain, nearly crushed and yelping in the puddle where the gun carriage had thrown him, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This aggravated his rage. He felt pledged to protect the husband, bound to defend him as he would the woman herself.

“Now, in the hovel beyond Zembin where I was so well received, this captain sat opposite me, and his wife was at the other end facing the Colonel. This Sicilian was a little woman named Rosina, very dark, with the Southern sun’s fire in her black almond-shaped eyes. At this moment she was terribly thin; her face was dusted like fruit exposed on a highway. Scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair disheveled under a shawl, she still had a woman’s graces; her movements were engaging, her small rose mouth and white teeth, the outline of her features and figure, charms which misery, cold, and neglect had not entirely erased. Rosina’s frame seemed fragile but was wiry and springy. Her husband, a gentleman from Piedmont, had an ironical simplicity in his face that somehow betrayed cruelty beneath phlegm and indolence. He seemed unaware of the connections between his wife and the Colonel for three years. I attributed this to Italian manners or some domestic secret; yet one feature in his countenance filled me with distrust—his thin, restless under lip turned down at the corners, which I thought revealed a streak of cruelty.

“The conversation was not lively when I arrived. My weary comrades ate in silence; they asked questions, shared misadventures, mixed with reflections on the campaign, generals, Russians, and the cold. A minute after I arrived, the colonel, having finished his meager meal, wiped his mustache, bade us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman, saying, ‘Rosina?’ and without waiting, went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of the Colonel’s call was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing all the annoyance she could not feel at seeing her thralldom flaunted without decency, and the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband. Yet there was, in her rigid features and knitted brows, a sort of presentiment; perhaps she foresaw her fate. Rosina remained quietly in her place.

“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was settled in straw, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’

“The tone of this second call was even more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel.

“All sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, after looking at them, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too. ‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband.

“‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, becoming serious again, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’

“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly.

“Thereupon we lay down in the room, and before long, all were sound asleep.

“Next morning, each one, without waking his neighbor or seeking companionship, set out again on his way, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror ever enacted under heaven. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, most of us met again and walked on together, like geese led in flocks by a child’s wilful tyranny. The same necessity urged us all.

“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse, we heard sounds like the roar of lions, the bellowing of bulls—no, a cry beyond comparison. Mingled with this ominous roar, we heard a woman’s feeble scream. We all looked around, seized by terror; we no longer saw the house, but a huge bonfire. The farmhouse had been barricaded and was in flames. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and an indescribable pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain was quietly approaching to join our caravan; we gazed at him in silence, for no one dared question him; but he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’

“We all walked on without a word to him.”

“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.

“It would be frightful to leave with this horrible picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”

“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.

“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.

“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.

“Yes,” I replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption, in its last stage, left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking around four in the morning, signed to me with a friendly smile to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her face was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling sprang from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.

“At this moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. I did not see the impatient gesture he made, expressing regret for having lost sight of his wife for a few of the last granted moments; but it is certain anyone but the dying woman might have misunderstood it. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, the Duke had a thousand odd surface ways which often lead a genius to be mistaken for a madman, explainable by the exacting needs of their intellect. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out, took her husband’s hand and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”

“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”

“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.

PARIS, June 1839-42.
