################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have some appalling stories in my collection. But each one has its proper hour in a conversation—you know Chamfort’s jest to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”

“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.

“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” rose the cry.

The obliging doctor bowed, and silence reigned.

“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house with very high roofs, so isolated that there is nothing near it, not even a tannery or tavern. In front of it is a garden down to the river, where box shrubs once clipped to edge the walks now straggle at will. A few willows rooted in the stream form a half-hidden fence. Weeds have clothed the bank; the fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, their suckers forming a thicket. Espaliers resemble a copse. The paths, once graveled, are overgrown; to be accurate, there is no trace of a path.

“From the hilltop where the ruins of the old castle of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, you can see that this spot must once have been the joy of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture, especially to choice fruit. An arbor, or rather its wreck, with a table still standing, not entirely destroyed, is visible. The aspect of this garden evokes provincial peace, like reading the epitaph of a worthy tradesman. To complete the mournful impression, one wall bears a sundial with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’

“The roof is dreadfully dilapidated; the outside shutters are always closed; swallows hang their nests from the balconies; the doors are shut. Straggling grasses green the flagstones of the steps; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, and peeled off the paint. The dreary silence is broken only by birds and cats, polecats, rats, and mice scampering around. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’

“If you look from the street, you will see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of holes made by children. I later learned the door had been blocked for ten years. Through these breaches, you see the side toward the courtyard in the same ruinous state as the garden side: weeds on the paving-stones; walls scored by cracks; the blackened coping laced with pellitory. Stone steps are disjointed; the bell-cord is rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven could have fallen here? By what decree has salt been sown on this dwelling? Has God been mocked, or was France betrayed? We ask ourselves. Reptiles crawl over it, but give no reply. This empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.

“It was formerly a little domain, held in fief, known as La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me in charge of a rich patient, the sight of this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. Was it not better than a ruin? Certain memories cling to a ruin; but this house, still standing though slowly destroyed by an avenging hand, contained a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once in the evening I climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braved scratches to enter the ownerless garden, and lingered for hours in its disorder. I would not have traded the story that produced this strange scene for any gossip. There I wove romances and indulged in melancholy that enchanted me. Had I known the reason for the neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that intoxicated me. To me, this refuge represented phases of human life shadowed by misfortune: sometimes the peace of a graveyard without the dead, sometimes the home of lepers, sometimes the house of the Atridae; but above all provincial life, contemplative and hour-glass in its existence. I often wept there; I never laughed.

“More than once I felt involuntary terrors at the dull hum of some passing wood-pigeon overhead. The earth is dank; you must watch for lizards, vipers, and frogs wandering with nature’s wild freedom; above all, you must have no fear of cold, for in a few moments you feel an icy cloak settle on your shoulders, like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.

“One evening I shuddered; the wind had turned an old rusty weathercock, and the creaking sounded like a cry from the house, just as I was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this monumental embodiment of woe. I returned to my inn, lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper, the hostess came into my room with an air of mystery, saying, ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’

“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’

“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said, and left the room.

“Suddenly a tall, slim man dressed in black appeared, hat in hand, entering like a ram ready to butt, with a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘whom have I the honor of addressing?’ He took a chair, sat before my fire, placed his hat on my table, and replied while rubbing his hands: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’

“I encouraged myself: seek!

“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’

“‘I am delighted to hear it, monsieur,’ I exclaimed. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’

“‘One moment!’ he said, holding up his hand. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed that you sometimes walk in the garden of la Grande Bretèche.’

“‘Yes, monsieur.’

“‘One moment!’ he repeated. ‘That constitutes a misdemeanor. Monsieur, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I come in her name to beg you to discontinue the practice. One moment! I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you may be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. Nevertheless, you must be a man of education and should know that the laws forbid, under heavy penalties, any trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be quite content to make you free to come and go in the house; but being bound to respect the will of the testatrix, I beg that you will go into the garden no more. I myself, monsieur, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which, as I informed you, is part of the estate of the late Madame de Merret. We have done nothing there but verify the number of doors and windows to assess the taxes I must pay annually out of the funds left by the late Madame de Merret. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in the town.’

“The good man paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility, understanding that administering Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life, his reputation, his glory. As I was forced to bid farewell to my reveries and romances, I declined the official truth.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘would it be indiscreet if I asked the reasons for such eccentricity?’

“At these words pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He drew up his collar, took out his snuffbox, offered me a pinch; on my refusing, he took a large one. He was happy! A man without a hobby does not know the good in life. In that moment I understood Sterne’s charming passion and had the perfect idea of the delight with which my uncle Toby, encouraged by Trim, bestrode his hobby-horse.

“‘Monsieur,’ said Monsieur Regnault, ‘I was head-clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first-rate house—you may have heard of it? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; among others, a wealthy aunt who allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, before my marriage, I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Chateau of Merret. Her maid, now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you that Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, plunging into every dissipation. You understand?

“‘On the day he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted la Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some say she burnt the furniture and hangings—in short, everything used in furnishing the premises now let by the said M.—(Dear, what am I saying? I beg your pardon, I thought I was dictating a lease.)—In short, they say she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’

“‘For about three months before, the Count and Countess had lived very eccentrically; they admitted no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor, Monsieur on the first floor. When the Countess was left alone, she was seen only at church. At home she refused to see friends. She was already much altered when she left la Grande Bretèche to go to Merret. That dear lady—I say dear, for it was she who gave me this diamond, though I saw her but once—was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; many fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was excited when I heard she needed my services. Nor was I the only one interested. That very night, all the town knew I was going to Merret.

“‘The waiting-woman replied vaguely; nevertheless, she told me her mistress had received the Sacrament that day and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the chateau. I went up the great staircase. After crossing some large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors about this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I repeated them), I had imagined a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty seeing her in the great bed where she lay. To light this enormous, old-fashioned room thick with dust, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! but you have not been to Merret. The bed is of the old world, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table by the bed held an ‘Imitation of Christ,’ which, by the way, I bought for my wife, and the lamp. There was also a deep armchair for her confidential maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, not enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.

“‘My dear sir, if you had seen that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt transported into a romance. It was icy—nay, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand theatrically and paused.

“‘At last I saw Madame de Merret under the lamp’s glimmer. Her face was as yellow as wax and as narrow as two folded hands. She wore a lace cap showing abundant hair, white as linen thread. She sat up in bed, keeping upright with great difficulty. Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever and half-dead, hardly moved beneath the bony arch of her eyebrows.—There,’ he added, pointing to his brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; the veins and muscles were perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; but now it was awful—sickness had consumed her into a phantom. Her pale-violet lips seemed not to move when she spoke.

“‘Though my profession has brought me to many deaths to record last wishes, families in tears and the agonies I have seen were as nothing compared with this lonely woman in her vast chateau. I heard no sound; I perceived not the breathing that ought to move the sheets, and I stood motionless, absorbed. In fancy I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back, and she uttered words like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to speak.

“‘“Madame,” I began. She signed to me to be silent. The old housekeeper rose and whispered, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise.”

“‘I sat down. A few moments later, Madame de Merret mustered all her strength, slipped her right hand under the bolster, paused, and with a last effort withdrew a sealed paper, drops of perspiration on her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix, lifted it to her lips, and died.

“‘The expression of her eyes makes me shudder. She must have suffered much! Joy was in her last glance and remained stamped on her dead eyes.

“‘I brought away the will, and when opened found she had appointed me her executor. She left most of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding la Grande Bretèche: she ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from her death, in the state it was at her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if necessary to secure fulfillment. At the expiration of that term, if the will has been duly carried out, the house becomes my heirs’ property, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise, la Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs-at-law, but on condition of fulfilling certain terms set forth in a codicil not to be opened until the fifty years expire. The will has not been disputed, so—’ And without finishing his sentence the lanky notary looked at me with triumph; I made him happy by offering congratulations.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘you have so vividly impressed me I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’

“‘Monsieur,’ said he with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.”

“However, I soon loosened the tongue of the discreet notary, who communicated, not without long digressions, the opinions of Vendôme’s deep politicians. These were so contradictory and diffuse that I nearly fell asleep despite my interest. The notary’s ponderous voice and monotonous accent, used to listen to himself, were too much for my curiosity. Happily, he went away.

“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ said he on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five-and-forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid his first finger to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—you must not be past sixty now.’

“I closed my door, having been roused by this last speech, which the notary thought very funny; then I sat down in my armchair with my feet on the fire-dogs. I had lost myself in a romance à la Radcliffe, built on the juridical base given me by Monsieur Regnault, when the door, opened by a woman’s cautious hand, turned on the hinges. I saw my landlady enter, a buxom, florid dame, always good-humored, who had missed her calling in life. She was a Fleming, who ought to have been born in a Teniers picture.

“‘Well, monsieur,’ said she, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of la Grande Bretèche?’

“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’

“‘And what did he tell you?’

“I repeated in a few words the creepy story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence my hostess leaned forward, looking at me with an innkeeper’s keen scrutiny, a happy compromise between the instinct of a constable, the astuteness of a spy, and the cunning of a dealer.

“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ I said as I ended, ‘you seem to know more about it. If not, why have you come up to me?’

“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’

“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’

“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid everything down, so as never to have difficulties with anyone. He was hot-tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’

“‘Because he was hot-tempered?’ I asked.

“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose, sir, that a man had to have something to show for a figurehead before he could marry Madame de Merret, who, without any reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet-looking, quite a gem. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’

“‘And were they happy together?’

“‘Hm, hm! so-so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail-fellow-well-met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’

“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?’

“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’

“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’

“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When the Emperor sent Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the charge of the Government a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—neither more nor less. He had a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name in my books; you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, which I admired. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, and among others General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to dislike him, though he scarcely spoke; if spoken to, he often did not answer—a way, a mania perhaps they have.

“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the first time, no one imagined purpose. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“One evening, shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys mentioned he thought he saw the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.

“Eventually, one morning, we found he hadn’t returned. Searching his belongings, I discovered a note in his drawer, along with fifty doubloons worth about five thousand francs and a sealed box containing diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note stated that if he didn’t return, he left us this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.

“At that time, my husband went searching for him. Interestingly, he returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He went early, so no one saw him. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced he had escaped.

“The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas believed the Spaniard drowned himself. I, however, think he was involved in the matter concerning Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me that the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was made of ebony and silver; early in his stay, Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, do you think I should feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?’

“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?’ I asked.

“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.’

“After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague, sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread, like entering a dark church at night where a faint light glimmers beneath a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, suddenly appeared vividly in my mind. I felt compelled to explore the mysterious dwelling to uncover the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.

“Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Observing her, I sensed an inner thought beneath her blooming health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a woman haunted by a past crime. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief tucked into her lilac-and-white-striped gown. ‘No,’ I resolved, ‘I will not leave Vendôme without knowing the full story of la Grande Bretèche. To achieve this, I will make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.’

“Rosalie!’ I called one evening.

“Your servant, sir?’

“You’re not married?’ She flinched slightly.

“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!’ she laughed, quickly regaining composure; every woman, from the highest lady to the inn-servant, possesses presence of mind.

“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?’

“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.’

“This response seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to this affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t a matter for ordinary courtship; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance, and from that moment all my attention was directed toward Rosalie. By studying her, I noticed admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm desire can bestow upon a woman. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning, I said to Rosalie:

“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.’

“Oh!’ she replied, ‘I will tell you, but keep it a secret.’

“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.’

“If it’s all the same to you,’ she said, ‘I’d prefer it be with your own.’

“She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. The best tales are told at a certain hour—just as we are all here at table.

“If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarcely suffice. Now, as the event she recounted stands midway between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it concisely.

“The room at la Grande Bretèche where Madame de Merret slept was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been seriously ill, so much so that her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. By chance, he returned home two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. His wife assumed he was in bed and asleep. However, the evening’s animated discussions and his losses at billiards had delayed him.

“For some time, Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to inform him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative responses led him to his room, trusting her completely. But that evening he decided to check on Madame de Merret to share his misfortunes and perhaps seek comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner and thought her convalescence had enhanced her beauty, a realization that often comes too late for husbands.

“Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. His instincts were piqued by a nagging doubt, and he noticed an anxious expression in her eyes.

“‘You’re very late,’ she said, her voice slightly husky.

“Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing the room, arms crossed.

“‘Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?’ his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t respond.

“‘You can go, Rosalie,’ Madame de Merret said; ‘I can manage my curl-papers myself.’ She sensed disaster in her husband’s demeanor and wanted to be alone with him. Once Rosalie left, or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage, Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, stating coldly, ‘Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!’ She calmly replied, ‘No, monsieur.’

“This ‘No’ pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand, stopping him, and said with strange emotion, ‘Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.’

“The extraordinary dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could be legendary on a grander stage.

“‘No, Josephine,’ he said, ‘I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.’—At these words, Madame de Merret looked at him with a haggard expression. ‘Here is your crucifix,’ he continued. ‘Swear to me before God that there’s no one in there; I will believe you—I will never open that door.’

“Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, ‘I swear it.’

“‘Louder,’ he insisted; ‘repeat: “I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.”’ She repeated the words without flinching.

“‘That will do,’ said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, ‘You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,’ examining the ebony and silver crucifix.

“‘I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.’

“‘Indeed,’ said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.

“He waited for her to arrive. ‘Go to bed, all of you,’ he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, ‘When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.’

“Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss the billiards game and the club’s discussions. When Rosalie returned, she found them conversing amiably.

“Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the reception rooms on the ground floor. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a considerable quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.

“‘Gorenflot is here, sir,’ Rosalie whispered.

“‘Tell him to come in,’ he replied aloud.

“Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.

“‘Gorenflot,’ said her husband, ‘go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.’ Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, ‘Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning, I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, which won’t be paid until your wedding day, contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!’

“‘Rosalie,’ Madame de Merret said, ‘come and brush my hair.’

“Her husband quietly paced the room, keeping an eye on the door, the mason, and his wife, without displaying any suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: ‘My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.’ Then she added casually, ‘You’d better help him.’

“Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. This silence was intentional on the husband’s part; he didn’t want to give his wife the chance to say anything ambiguous. On Madame de Merret’s side, it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason took advantage of his master’s back being turned to break one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to Gorenflot. They all three then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.

“Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling ‘Hope.’

“At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.

“The next morning, he casually mentioned, ‘Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.’ He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy.

“‘He will go to Duvivier’s,’ she thought.

“As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, ‘The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.’

“In an instant, Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with an intensity beyond words, she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.

“‘Lay madame on her bed,’ he said coldly.

“Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.

“‘Duvivier,’ Monsieur de Merret asked, ‘didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?’

“‘No, monsieur.’

“‘Very well; thank you,’ he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. ‘Jean,’ he added, turning to his valet, ‘you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.’

“The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet, and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said, without allowing her to speak, ‘You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.’”

After this story, all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some among them had shivered at the last words.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

During the Restoration one of the few drawing-rooms frequented by the Archbishop of Besançon was Baronne de Watteville’s, owing to her devoutness.

Monsieur de Watteville, a descendant of the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After a quiet life in La Franche-Comté, he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family, whose substantial income enriched their household. The match was arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following the death of her grandparents, Madame de Watteville became more devout, adopting a pious life that influenced Besançon’s upper circles.

Monsieur de Watteville, thin and unremarkable in appearance, occupied himself as a turner and collector of local geological specimens. Many observed a certain nobility of soul in him: realizing early he would not dominate his wife, he immersed himself in work and hobbies.

The Rupt residence reflected both families’ standing—grand rooms, elegant furnishings, and fine cuisine—with Monsieur sometimes acting as his own butler. Madame de Watteville’s fortune was considerable, while her husband’s estate was modest. Their intimacy with the Archbishop drew several notable abbés to their home.

At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests warmly received Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit. The Vicar-General explained that the legal success had been unexpected given the political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, for his decisive role.

Curious about Savaron, Madame de Watteville asked about his background. The Vicar-General said Savaron lived nearby but that his origins were unknown, which piqued the guests’ curiosity. Amedee de Soulas, present at the gathering, would later prove significant to the story that followed.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often skirting customs. What is "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual borrowing. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, first derided, became a dominant European fashion for decades. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, yet French styles soon shifted in response.

More recently England supplied French with terms like "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset's poetry, sparked a trend of stylish young men in Paris that spread to the provinces. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt ill at ease in the dull, military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained an ostentatious appearance, keeping a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and lavished expense on his own wardrobe while subsisting on scant income.

Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was adept at economizing, often dining out to save on household meals. His costly grooming and clothes were a façade aimed at impressing the local aristocracy and winning Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and Parisian knowledge helped him cultivate a reputation as a well-informed young man despite financial strain.

Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy, called "the Colony," kept strict social hierarchies. Amedee’s unusual fashions—like wearing trouser-straps—set him apart and bolstered his status as a "lion." The town’s nobles wished to appear modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee enabled that image.

Amedee’s chief ambition was to marry Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict, religious household with little contact with the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye and instructed more in domestic skills than social polish. Sheltered from modern influences, she lacked awareness of the social expectations surrounding courtship.

Despite a passive exterior, Rosalie had a resolute spirit from her lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This dynamic created tension, especially as Amedee courted Rosalie. The Baroness’s strictness led Rosalie to suppress any feelings for Amedee, producing a complex relationship marked by maternal jealousy and Rosalie’s uncertainty.

As Amedee pursued Rosalie, he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies to secure a favorable match while protecting his reputation. The town’s elite regarded him as charming, and gossip about a possible engagement circulated among the nobility. Yet Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence complicated his efforts; she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations around her.

Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus illustrates the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville’s heart amid rigid social structures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness, as he waited for his soup to cool, “one fine morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and chose to stay on the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie and had his name—‘ALBERT SAVARON’—entered on the list of barristers at the Court, leaving his card with local officials.”

“The name Savaron is well-known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville interjected, “as one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”

“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he wishes to claim the Savaron arms, he must add a bar-sinister. The only remaining member of the Brabant family is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”

“The bar-sinister indicates a bastard,” Rosalie added, “but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble.”

“Enough, that will do, mademoiselle!” the Baroness interjected.

“You insisted on her learning heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville remarked, “and she knows it well.”

“Please continue, Monsieur de Soulas.”

“In a town like Besancon, where everything is classified and known, Albert Savaron was accepted without fuss by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to achieve, especially since he sent his card to the judges instead of calling. Three days later, he was forgotten. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron remained overlooked because no one saw him.”

“Does he not go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, breakfasts, continues working, walks in his garden in circles, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”

“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt inquired.

“I live on Rue Neuve, overlooking his house, and my servant communicates with Jerome.”

“And you gossip with Babylas?”

“What else is there to do while riding?”

“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked, turning to the Vicar-General.

“The President of the Court assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man’s innocence and exposed the real culprits, leading to their arrest. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant subsequently entrusted him with a difficult case, which he won. Given our situation, Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron, predicting our success. Upon meeting him, I felt confident— and I was right.”

“Is he truly extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.

“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.

“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in his outer room, decorated like old oak and lined with law books. The room was simply furnished with an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, and a green carpet. I did not expect my advocate to be young. The setting matched his appearance; he wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”

“The devil’s colors!” exclaimed Madame de Watteville.

“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. His black hair is streaked with gray, thick and curly; he has a round white throat, a splendid forehead marked by deep lines of thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and fiery eyes. Despite his passion, he has a calm demeanor and a sweet voice that can thunder when needed.”

“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”

“The second time I visited, he received me in his shabby bedroom. The third time, he was having a frugal breakfast in his library; I observed him closely. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”

“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”

“After studying his fine head,” the Abbe replied, “and hearing him respond to a prominent Parisian lawyer, I believe he will eventually make a significant impact.”

“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.

The conversation shifted, and Albert Savaron was no longer mentioned. The Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie, who found the romance of his character intoxicating. For the first time she encountered someone exceptional, unlike the mundane Count who had courted her. Albert Savaron presented many enigmas.

“Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him. She replayed the Abbe’s words, fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, scrutinizing the men at the table, finding none that compared.

“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.

“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she replied, lost in thought about the prelate’s hands.

She recalled a lighted window she had seen from her bed and realized it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.

“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull.

“What does that matter to you?” her mother replied, sensing her daughter’s unusual interest.

“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” the Abbe answered.

“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit during dinner,” Madame de Watteville remarked.

“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting laughter. “But Monsieur de Grancey was so engrossed that I became interested.”

After dinner, Rosalie listened for any mention of Albert Savaron, but aside from congratulations to the Abbe for winning his suit, he was not discussed. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse Albert’s windows. When the hour arrived, she felt a thrill watching the light from his candles through the trees. She saw him writing and imagined the color of his furniture.

“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.

The education of girls poses serious challenges, since the future of a nation rests on mothers. The University of France has long avoided this issue: Should girls be informed about everything? Should their minds be restrained? The religious system often imposes such restraint. Enlightening them too much can lead to premature maturity, while suppressing their thoughts can produce explosive outbursts, as in Moliere’s Agnes. Rosalie’s crisis was triggered by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.

The next morning, while dressing, Rosalie looked out to see Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered.

Having seen him, Rosalie was determined to enter his house, uncover his mystery, hear his voice, and glimpse his eyes. She resolved to achieve this.

Throughout the day she embroidered with the concentration of a girl who seems to think of nothing yet is deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.

She visited her father in his workshop, suggesting he turn larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. As he delighted in the idea, she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding.

“Don’t worry!” he replied, sharing her frustration with his wife.

Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.

The eagerly awaited Sunday arrived, and Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled.

“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.

“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.

As she crossed the courtyard, her heart raced with anticipation. She had never walked the streets like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and would forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church before Mass, praying briefly before checking if the Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.

No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression. His solemn demeanor suggested a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. Rosalie now fully understood the Vicar-General’s words. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a bold moment she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.

After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a place to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced Monsieur Giroud’s arrival, the time in the confessional felt fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended, and Albert had gone.

“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”

With this new desire, Rosalie focused intently on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.

From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie eagerly supported the plan for the Belvedere.

“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”

“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”

“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”

“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”

“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”

“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.

She approved the design, choosing a spot in the garden that could not be seen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but would allow them to see into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor, while Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.

“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumors spread in Besancon.

“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”

“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.

“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”

“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” added an Abbe.

“No,” the young man corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”

“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt remarked sourly, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.

Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the Belvedere’s success, sensing superiority over others. No one suspected that the girl thought to be a witless goose had simply devised the plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.

Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, increasing the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger in Besancon. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal skill.

On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was also launching a new venture. Through quiet connections among local merchants, he was starting a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, convinced of the need to promote Besancon as a trade hub.

To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas resonated with the merchants, who liked to believe in their own enterprise.

Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, letting his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.

To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while letting him think he was gaining. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.

After the third issue, the Review gained recognition among French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, sparking discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though higher circles of Besancon dismissed it for liberal views.

“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”

Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie who had shown him so much affection, subscribed and lent her the four issues already published. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact on her cannot be judged by ordinary standards. She hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages, she became convinced it reflected him, as critics noted Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In 1823 two young men set out from Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland, planning to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking views of mountains, rivers, and meadows. As they passed the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window. He decided to stay in Gersau while his friend Leopold continued the tour.

Rodolphe and Leopold were close, having studied law together. Leopold was destined to be a notary in Paris; Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more troubled background. His mother had sacrificed everything for his education, hoping to secure his future. Rodolphe was passionate and sensitive, often driven by his desires, in contrast to Leopold’s calm demeanor.

In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple willing to take him in. He learned the woman was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were of modest means; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.

The next day Rodolphe sought permission to visit the gardens of the Bergmanns, who rented to the Lovelaces. After initial hesitation he was allowed to explore. He noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who seemed to have an unusual background. Eager to meet Fanny, he met resistance from the protective Bergmanns.

That evening Rodolphe heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later encountered her and Gina, but their conversation was cut short when Rodolphe was attacked and wounded by an unknown assailant. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken back to safety.

While he recovered Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.

Fanny later revealed that her husband was not as old as he had seemed and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his feelings and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing he had great potential.

Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past, sent news and eventually arrived. Tito’s presence stirred rivalry and fear in Rodolphe of losing Fanny’s affection.

Despite these challenges Rodolphe and Fanny’s connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances—Rodolphe’s ambition, Fanny’s loyalty to her husband, social constraints—loomed over them. As they navigated their feelings Rodolphe grappled with the tensions between love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions, even as the realities of their lives threatened to overshadow their dreams.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For twenty days Rodolphe spent his time at the Bergmanns’, observing Francesca without intending to. He recognized in her the youthful imprudence and unbroken character of a woman, sometimes resisting love and at other times yielding. The old man treated her like a father, and Francesca’s gratitude stirred her innate nobility. Rodolphe found both the situation and Francesca an intriguing enigma.

Those days held secret joys and melancholic moods, delightful quarrels as pleasing as their harmony. Rodolphe grew increasingly captivated by Francesca's constant tenderness, often jealous over trifles.

One evening, when Francesca declared she wished to leave Gersau because it lacked luxuries, Rodolphe asked whether she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating her love for luxury with her love for art and beauty, insisting she had never complained during hardships. She mischievously suggested it was natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, revealing her charming personality.

Francesca then insisted Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing the town's gossip and her desire to protect her husband’s reputation. She professed her love for Rodolphe but emphasized it must remain hidden. She kissed him and left him astonished.

The next day Rodolphe learned the Bergmanns’ lodgers had departed early. Restless, he went to Vevay and took a longer route to Geneva by the end of October. To avoid the town’s discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiry was about Italian refugees, particularly a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord said the only recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.

Rodolphe’s first walk took him to Villa Diodati, whose beauty struck him. As he approached, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady, which left him dizzy. Learning they were at the Colonna residence, he felt a veil lift, realizing the implications of their past interactions.

He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca, confronting the reality of her noble lineage. He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by the false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from asking to see the Princess, returning to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety.

That evening his passion grew, and he felt the need to raise his social standing to match Francesca's. When he finally saw her at the concert she was radiant, and their eyes met in an electric moment. Francesca’s beauty and grace captivated him; he felt a profound connection and realized she had remained true to their past despite her rank.

After the concert Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who playfully called herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of their time in Gersau; he was overwhelmed by her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.

As days passed Francesca acknowledged Rodolphe's deep love, which felt natural to her. She said their love should be faithful and persistent, whatever distance separated them. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition—he wanted to achieve greatness so Francesca could be proud of him.

Francesca, with her blend of gaiety and tenderness, was remarkable. Born destined for a cloister, family tragedies forced her into marriage with Prince Gandolphini to preserve their status. Despite her noble background she had a taste for independence and liberal ideas and sympathized with revolutionary sentiments.

Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva, where time flew in the company of the intelligent, beautiful Francesca. Yet as spring neared he felt inner turmoil: Francesca’s calm contrasted with his emotional unrest, and he longed for more demonstrative feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.

One day on the terrace, Rodolphe voiced bitterness about others’ happiness, particularly that of a noblewoman. Francesca, wounded, drew back; Rodolphe followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she rebuffed him and asserted her dignity.

Francesca then ordered Rodolphe to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Though her command was stern, Rodolphe complied, recognizing the strength of her will.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Returning to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, who had previously refused to paint several women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade the artist, but Francesca charmed him into producing an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced this in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca—their sole indulgence.

Driven by ambition and love, Rodolphe invested all his resources in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years of struggle and perseverance. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but first traveled to Naples to heal his wounds and regain his courage at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their positions. The three months there, filled with hope and renewed courage, revived him. This restored some calm and confidence before the next trials unexpectedly confronted him again.

He then tried to rebuild his fortune. His talents were recognized, and as a reward for his dedication and past efforts a high post was promised. But the storm of July 1830 again thwarted his ambitions.

Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts and daring attempts of this young man—endowed with fine qualities but without fortune's favor. Sustained by love, he returned to face new challenges, guided by an ever-supportive presence and a faithful heart.

Lovers! Pray for him!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie’s cheeks burned and tears flowed. This novel, in the popular style of its day, was Rosalie’s first encounter with such a story: love narrated by a man recounting his own experience, and it stirred her deeply. Her agitation came from jealousy toward Francesca Colonna, whom she believed to be Albert’s beloved.

Consumed by curiosity, Rosalie felt a fierce urge to win Albert despite her insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent. Afraid he would not love her, she became determined to learn the truth about his affections. That night her imagination filled with schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert’s garden, writing to him, even visiting her father’s land dispute to contrive an encounter.

From Albert’s window she saw a scene that promised insight into his secrets: Jerome, Albert’s servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noticed Mariette’s plainness but also her relative financial security, which made her appealing to Jerome. The sight taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson—that the constraints her mother imposed could be bypassed by the actions of servants.

The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jerome. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, unexpectedly, offered assistance in exchange for access to letters Albert sent through Jerome. Relieved by Rosalie’s discretion, Mariette agreed.

Rosalie’s obsession deepened when she read a letter Albert had sent to his friend Leopold. It revealed his noble lineage and past struggles and offered a portrait of ambition and constancy that enthralled her. She was absorbed by his striving for success while remaining devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca.

As Rosalie read more correspondence, she grew more aware of her inadequacy and the moral cost of her actions. She struggled with guilt over invading Albert’s privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion hardened into a perilous infatuation that led to penances and self-imposed suffering as attempts to demonstrate her devotion.

One evening Mariette brought another letter from Albert to Francesca, sending Rosalie into a spiral of jealousy and doubt. Torn over whether to read it, she ultimately yielded to the desire to know Albert’s feelings. The letter confirmed his ongoing love for Francesca and outlined his ambitions and hopes for their future.

The revelation made Rosalie’s heart ache as she took in the depth of Albert’s devotion to another. Though she had at first resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself caught between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a clear moment she decided to step back, recognizing both the nobility of their bond and her own shortcomings.

As she prepared to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her emotions and on the nature of love. She felt sorrow and resignation but also a yearning for the selfless, enduring love Albert and Francesca shared, even as she faced the reality of her unrequited feelings.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Rosalie spent the winter of 1834-35 in turmoil, but by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude, that struggle fueled her passion and dark thoughts, prompting numerous plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist, and this story serves as a cautionary tale for them.

During that winter, Albert de Savarus made strides in Besancon, eagerly anticipating the dissolution of the Chamber. He had secured support from a wealthy contractor influential in the moderate party. The Romans had long invested in water supplies for their towns, and Besancon sourced water from Arcier, a distant hill. Restoring an aqueduct to drink the same water as the Romans seemed absurd in a town already served by the Doubs River. Yet if convinced, the citizens would authorize large expenditures that would benefit the contractor.

Savarus argued the river water was unfit for drinking and promoted Arcier water. Articles in the local Review echoed this, and townspeople across the political spectrum agreed on drinking the Romans’ water. The issue became a focal point in Besancon, driven by hidden interests. Opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned, while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town, gaining influence without popularity.

Throughout the winter he won several lawsuits for local priests, and ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in personal gain masked a form of moral usury, as he anticipated a much greater reward.

In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Leopold Hannequin, which also qualified him for election. The Abbe de Grancey, who saw Savarus’s ambitions, remarked on his remarkable nature and suggested he had taken a wrong turn in life.

Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hotel de Rupt, initially content to observe him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, was worth ten thousand francs a year, but its management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant, Modinier. The Baron's indifference stemmed from his wife's substantial income, and they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot created by the famous Watteville.

Watteville had constructed a dam and lake, enhancing the area’s beauty, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, which had long enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.

Rosalie’s efforts were complex and subtle. In May 1835 she left Besancon with her father for les Rouxey, captivated by the lake’s beauty and associating it with love. She suggested a boat trip, during which her father confided about legal troubles with the village of Riceys over land rights. Rosalie encouraged him to hire Savarus as a lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate.

She argued that a lawsuit would add excitement to their lives and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey. Rosalie’s determination to secure her future there was clear; she envisioned creating beautiful gardens.

Four days later the Abbe de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbe urged him to consider the political advantages, especially the potential for votes.

Rosalie was determined to keep Savarus in Besancon and prevent his election. She wrote a letter to the Prefet, revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the Prefet disregarded it. Rosalie understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.

As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besancon decided to nominate Savarus, much to his surprise. He was initially unaware of the political machinations. The Abbe de Grancey, observing the situation, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.

Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, captivating his audience. However, he faced challenges as the Prefet worked to undermine him, leading to a decline in his voter base. Despite his efforts, the political tides shifted against him, and he struggled to maintain his position.

On the day of the first voting the results disappointed Savarus, who felt the weight of his ambitions and the pressure of the election. He returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.

Just as he neared hopelessness, the Abbe de Grancey arrived with news of hope. The priest had been working behind the scenes to secure support, urging Savarus to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election.

The Abbe revealed that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes, and he encouraged Savarus to pursue a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to further solidify his political standing. Savarus was initially resistant but recognized the potential benefits.

The Abbe’s guidance proved invaluable, and Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, aware that his future depended on choices he would make in the coming days.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By nine o’clock the following evening, Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were filled with the aristocracy of Besançon, assembled to discuss the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. It was known that the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, would be presented. Madame de Chavoncourt attended with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed, while her elder sister, secure in her relationship, had not made an effort with her appearance. In small towns, such details are noted. The Abbe de Grancey moved among the groups, listening and offering incisive comments that steered the conversation.

“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” The statesman said Berryer, alone on his bench, was uncertain and could disrupt the Government if he rallied support. They discussed the nomination of the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse and how voting for Monsieur Savarus might align Republicans with them instead of the Moderates.

By nine-thirty, Albert had still not arrived, and Madame de Watteville took his tardiness as disrespect. Madame de Chavoncourt advised her not to let small matters affect serious issues, suggesting Albert might be detained by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother’s exchange with Madame de Chavoncourt, noted her leniency toward Albert, hinting at a possible match between Sidonie and him.

At ten o’clock Albert was still absent and frustration grew. Some gentlemen began to play cards, while the Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to the window where Rosalie was hidden, exclaiming, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.

“Jerome!” Rosalie called to the servant below. When he confirmed that Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe de Grancey exclaimed, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s expression betrayed joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended not to. He wondered about her involvement.

Back in the drawing-room, Monsieur de Watteville announced the shocking news of Albert’s departure without explanation. By half-past eleven only fifteen guests remained, including Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas, and a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election. The Abbe de Grancey placed himself near the Baroness to watch Rosalie, whose normally pale face now showed agitation.

“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. At that moment, a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did, he noticed Rosalie turn pale, recognizing the handwriting. He folded the letter and discreetly pocketed it, having exchanged glances with Rosalie that hinted at her feelings.

“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie stopped him, saying, “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” When he questioned how she knew the writing, her passionate reply revealed her: “I love him! What is the matter?” He told her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Rosalie, stunned, begged him to keep the information secret, realizing that if the election ended, so would the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In the morning on her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette about the circumstances of Albert’s sudden disappearance. An elderly gentleman from Paris had arrived at the Hotel National and visited Monsieur Savaron. The lawyer turned pale on seeing the visitor’s card; they met more than an hour. Afterwards the gentleman left with a large package, Savaron appeared distressed, went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other work.

The next day Savaron’s disappearance stirred Besançon; the Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The episode fueled local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.

By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, noting how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness worried about finances. A match with Monsieur de Soulas was arranged, but Rosalie rejected it, insisting she was content as she was.

Conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, and they spent a tense summer at les Rouxey. After Mariette and Jerome wed, the Baron improved the estate—actions the Baroness viewed with resentment. As tensions mounted, the Vicar-General tried to mediate.

One day Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had sabotaged Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters, causing their separation. The Vicar-General was horrified at the moral gravity of her deed, urging her to seek forgiveness and to grasp the consequences.

Rosalie’s guilt deepened when she learned of Albert’s fate. A clerk from Paris, acting for Albert, came to sell his belongings and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness for Rosalie and a wish to remain in solitude.

When the Vicar-General shared this, he urged Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept Monsieur de Soulas. Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to ruin her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.

Now of age, Rosalie encouraged her mother to marry, securing her own financial independence. With a substantial fortune she entered Parisian society, resolved to confront the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball she publicly revealed her part in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.

Rosalie returned to Besançon having exposed the truth, yet the emotional cost weighed heavily. The tale of love, betrayal, and the consequences of ambition unfolded, leaving a lasting impact on all involved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her estate of les Rouxey, riding, hunting, and improving the land, making her notable in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger, while her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has aged considerably. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”

Mademoiselle de Watteville is known for her eccentric “vagaries.” Each year she visits the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps inspired by a grand-uncle who once broke monastery walls to gain freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon, reportedly to marry, though the true reason remains unclear. She returned after a steamboat boiler explosion on the Loire that cost her her right arm and left leg and left disfiguring facial scars. Her health deteriorated; in constant pain, she now devotes herself to religious practices at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.

PARIS, May 1842.

Produced by John Bickers and Dagny

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

By Honore de Balzac

Translated by R. S. Scott

DEDICATION

To George Sand

Your name, dear George, adds a reflected radiance to my book, not from self-interest or diffidence, but to witness our enduring friendship, which has triumphed over the world's malice. This bond is unlikely to change. The names attached to my works bring me pleasure amidst the vexation of their increasing number. Each new book brings fresh annoyance, as if my pen could rival the world from which I draw my models! Would it not be wonderful if future antiquarians found only great names and generous hearts among my friends? I take pride in this possession rather than in unstable popularity. For those who know you well, it is happiness to sign myself, as I do here,

Your friend,  
DE BALZAC.  
PARIS, June 1840.

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

FIRST PART

I. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.

Sweetheart, I too am free! I am the first to write, unless you have already sent a letter to Blois.

Raise those great black eyes of yours, fixed on my opening sentence, and save your excitement for the letter that will tell you of my first love. By the way, why always "first?" Is there a second love?
