To the right, the canal Saint-Martin stretches, bordered by reddish quays and lime trees, with public granaries in a Roman style along its banks. Beyond, the smoke-laden slopes of Belleville, dotted with houses and windmills, merge with the clouds. Between that horizon and the valley's rooftops lies a vast city, hidden yet alive, echoing with sounds that declare its presence.

When sunlight floods this part of Paris, it transforms the landscape, illuminating windows, brightening tiles, and creating a magical atmosphere. The scene is idyllic, filled with the sounds of humanity and nature, a harmonious blend that captivates the soul. Beneath the cypress trees of Père-Lachaise, the entire capital lies in peaceful splendor.

One morning, as I admired this view, I reflected bitterly on those who disdain our homeland, the wealthy who travel abroad only to mock France. I gazed lovingly at modern Paris until the sound of a kiss interrupted my reverie. I saw a young woman, simply yet elegantly dressed, with a handsome young man and a charming little boy. Their joy was palpable, their connection evident in movement and expression.

A second child, a little girl, stood apart, her demeanor suggesting a deeper, more troubled nature. As I watched, the contrast between the siblings became clear. The girl, perhaps seven or eight, had dark hair and a serious expression; her younger brother was fair and carefree. Their clothing was similar, but the girl's plain collar hinted at a hidden preference from their mother.

The boy approached his sister with a small toy, but she scowled, revealing a complexity beyond her years. Her gaze was intense, as if burdened by thought. I was drawn to her, intrigued by the shadows of maturity on her young face.

As the couple continued their playful interaction, the little girl’s expression shifted from indifference to something darker. When her brother called to her, she reacted hostilely, pushing him away. In a tragic instant he stumbled and fell into the river, disappearing beneath the murky water. The scene unfolded with terrifying speed; I rushed to help, but it was too late. The mother arrived frantic; there was no hope of saving the child.

The incident left a haunting mark on the mother, who would carry the consequences of this tragedy home. The weight of her grief and the implications of her daughter’s actions loomed, a silent witness to the unfolding drama.

Years later, the Marquis de Vandenesse, now in mourning for his father, hosted a dinner with a notary present. The notary, oblivious to the tension, monopolized the conversation, unaware of the Marquise’s impatience to leave and join her children at the theater. Despite the couple’s hints to end the discussion, he persisted, oblivious.

As the clock struck nine, the Marquis finally interrupted, insisting the notary leave so he could escort his wife. The notary still failed to grasp the gravity, prolonging the talk and prompting a frustrating exchange. The Marquis, exasperated, attempted to correct the notary’s misunderstandings, but the atmosphere grew tenser.

Just then the Marquise’s husband returned unexpectedly with their children, who appeared upset. The little girl’s eyes were red from crying; the boy was fretful. The Marquise’s concern deepened as she realized the children’s distress stemmed from the play they had attended. The boy recounted a scene from the theater, revealing the source of their sudden return.

The Marquise’s face hardened as she processed the implications of her daughter’s emotional state. The notary, attempting to lighten the mood, only exacerbated matters with his comments about family dynamics and preferences, heightening tension.

Overwhelmed, the Marquise sent her daughter away, while the notary, still speaking, remained oblivious to his words’ weight. The Marquis, unable to contain his anger, confronted him and demanded he leave. Confused and flustered, the notary exited, leaving the couple to grapple with the evening’s emotional fallout.

As the notary reflected afterward, he failed to grasp the underlying issues, attributing the tension to aristocratic whims. His wife recognized the truth, advising him to stick to business in future dealings. The notary’s inability to perceive social nuance threatened further misunderstandings in his professional life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One of Napoleon’s orderly staff-officers, called the General or the Marquis, spent the spring at Versailles. Having amassed a fortune during the Restoration and because of his court duties, he took a country house near Montreuil, seeking solitude close to Paris. The house, once a retreat for a nobleman’s fleeting romances, had expansive grounds; its entrance still faced the street, suggesting a once-quiet road.

One winter evening the family was alone; the servants had gone to Versailles for a wedding. It was Christmas time, and the revelers lingered, so the General felt unease as the clock struck eleven without their return. Outside, a deep silence lay over the countryside, broken only by the wind and distant footsteps.

Inside, the family enjoyed a cozy evening. The General, relaxed in a high-back armchair by the fire, watched his youngest son, who resisted his mother’s attempts to undress him, preferring to play with his sister. The children’s laughter filled the room; their mother, beautiful and content, observed them with affection. The General, a rugged soldier, found joy in their innocence and the warmth of family life.

At a nearby table their thirteen-year-old son read the Arabian Nights, absorbed in the tale. His sister, Helene, worked on a tapestry; her beauty was striking and her demeanor thoughtful. The family’s dynamic was rich, the children’s play contrasting with the older siblings’ more serious reflections.

As the evening wore on, the General expressed concern over the absent servants, and the Marquise, sensing tension, urged their son to bed. Then a series of hurried knocks echoed from the street, signaling urgency. The General, armed and cautious, answered the door to find a breathless man seeking refuge, claiming he was being pursued.

The stranger, desperate and disheveled, insisted on secrecy and water. The General, despite hesitation, agreed to shelter him, feeling an inexplicable pull toward the man. The stranger, with bloodied hands, explained he had been involved in a duel. As the General led him to a hidden room, he felt a mix of unease and obligation.

Meanwhile, the Marquise sensed something amiss and questioned her husband about the stranger. The General, keeping his promise of secrecy, urged the family to remain unaware. But Helene, curious and concerned, overheard their low conversation and felt compelled to investigate.

Approaching the locked room, she was struck by the presence of the stranger, who, despite his grim state, exuded a magnetic power. Their eyes met and an unspoken connection formed, leaving Helene intrigued and unsettled. She returned to the family, her mind racing with thoughts of the man she had seen.

As the night advanced, the General’s thoughts were consumed by the implications of harboring a fugitive. Tension in the household grew palpable; the Marquise, sensing her daughter’s distraction, pressed Helene to explain. Torn between loyalty to her family and fascination with the stranger, Helene struggled to articulate her feelings.

The General, now fully aware of the danger the stranger’s presence invited, resolved to protect his family at all costs. He called for the servants to return, angered as he demanded answers about their delay. The atmosphere shifted, charged with a sense of impending crisis.

Gendarmes arrived seeking the fugitive, and the General prepared to confront them, his protectiveness for his family surging. The stranger, aware of the growing tension, remained silent, contemplating his next move. The General’s resolve hardened as he faced the reality that his sheltering choice might endanger his household.

In the ensuing chaos Helene’s feelings for the stranger deepened, leading her to question her desires and the life she had known. The night culminated in a dramatic confrontation that forced the General to confront the moral complexities of his actions and their consequences for his family.

Ultimately, the General’s hospitality led to unforeseen consequences: Helene’s connection to the stranger would alter the course of their lives. What began as an idyllic evening transformed into a night of turmoil, revealing the fragility of their happiness and the weight of the choices they had made.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
It was a June day in 1844 when the Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman of about fifty, walked in the garden of her daughter’s Paris mansion. She lingered by a row of windows, sitting at times to gaze at the dome of the Invalides and her own grounds. The neighborhood was quiet; the aristocracy was only beginning to stir.

Having lost her two sons, Gustave and Abel, the Marquise had given her fortune to her daughter, the Comtesse Moïna de Saint-Héreen. Her affection for Moïna was profound, a stark contrast to her faint memories of her deceased children. Society regarded the Marquise’s devotion as a kind of superstition and largely overlooked her past and her coolness toward her daughter-in-law.

Despite her sacrifices, the Marquise was often alone; Moïna was absorbed in her social life. Her love for her daughter was clear but shaded by sadness and a sense of loss. Gossip suggested she might regret giving up her fortune, yet most praised her devotion. Rarely attending social events, she felt eclipsed by Moïna’s youthful charm when she did appear.

As she sat in the garden, the Marquise reflected on her life; her face showed the weight of experience. Beauty had faded from sorrow and time. She was aware of Moïna’s flirtation with Alfred de Vandenesse, a man she regarded with disdain, and feared it could end in heartbreak.

The Comte de Saint-Héreen had been away for months, and Moïna’s flirtation seemed perilous to the Marquise. Feeling powerless, she knew her warnings would likely be ignored. Her heart ached at the thought of her daughter on a precipice she could not prevent.

In a moment of despair she remembered the small, painful moments that had accumulated over the years, each one marking Moïna’s growing distance. She went to Moïna’s room hoping to speak, only to meet indifference. The Countess’s dismissive attitude stung, and the Marquise felt the weight of contempt.

When she finally confronted Moïna, their conversation grew tense. The Marquise warned of the risks posed by Alfred; Moïna’s flippant replies deepened the Marquise’s sorrow. Leaving the room, the Marquise felt a physical pain in her heart, realizing her daughter had lost respect for her.

Outside, overwhelmed by grief and the sense of being irretrievably estranged, she collapsed on a bench. Moïna, unaware of the gravity of the situation, believed she could repair things with a few kind gestures. The Marquise’s condition worsened; she was carried inside, lifeless.

Struck by remorse as she watched her mother decline, Moïna suddenly grasped how she had taken that love for granted. It was too late for reconciliation. As she wept, the Marquise, in her final moments, managed a smile at her daughter — a testament to a mother’s enduring love and forgiveness.

Servants rushed for a physician, but the Marquise’s fate was sealed. Moïna, now fully aware of her loss, stood before her family hollow with regret, realizing the depth of her mother’s sacrifice and devotion.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In the spring of 1822, Parisian doctors sent a young man, M. le Baron Gaston de Nueil, to Lower Normandy to recover from an inflammatory illness, probably caused by overwork or excess. Seeking rest, a light diet, and fresh air, he settled in Bayeux, where his relatives welcomed him warmly and treated his arrival as a blessing.

After a few evenings with his cousin, Mme. de Sainte-Severe, Gaston quickly met the local elite, who embodied the typical characters of provincial France. One was an ancient but impoverished noble family: a patriarch who was a determined sportsman, contemptuous of modernity and the new political order; a loud, domineering wife who assumed their daughters’ fortunes rested solely on their name. They clung to obsolete customs, representing a nobility without wealth or influence.

In contrast stood a wealthier, more recent family who embraced contemporary Parisian trends. The wife was a society woman—slightly behind the latest fashions—while the husband, a member of the Departmental Council, was shrewd with finances and well versed in political gossip. Their Royalism was less zealous than the old nobles’ as they engaged with modern life while maintaining ancestral ties.

The local bishop, a former Vicar-General, mediated between these families: respected for his office but often reminded of his plebeian origins. Retired military officers and their families also featured in the circle; they preserved a veneer of aristocracy but focused more on local affairs than national politics. The women often dressed as if for court life, yet their days were governed by routine and tradition.

This exclusive social set included a few wealthy townspeople who had infiltrated the aristocracy, regarded skeptically by the older generation. Ecclesiastics added variety, bringing wit and a bourgeois element to otherwise staid gatherings.

Collectively, the society was marked by antiquated beliefs and resistance to change. Conversations returned to the same themes, members issued immutable judgments, and intellectual life stagnated. New ideas were unwelcome; any outsider who criticized their way of life was quickly labeled a misfit.

Before his arrival, Gaston had already been appraised by the local elite: his cousin had disclosed his fortune and family background. At twenty-three, he was welcomed as a promising young man with an income of eighteen thousand livres from his land in the Auge valley; his father was negotiating to buy more woodland, further securing their status.

Yet Gaston’s family situation was complicated by an elder brother who was consumptive and likely dying—a fact Mme. de Sainte-Severe conveniently omitted. At first Gaston amused himself by observing and sketching the quirks of his acquaintances, enjoying their quaintness. But as weeks passed he grew weary of their monotony, feeling trapped in a cloistered existence that drained his spirits.

This confinement produced a crisis of spirit: ennui and mounting discontent. As he adapted to the unchanging environment, Gaston began losing touch with the vibrant life he had known in Paris. The once vivid memories of intellectual engagement dimmed, and he risked becoming just another fossil among the fossils—content to exist in a dull routine, like the companions of Ulysses who settled for complacency.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One evening, Gaston de Nueil sat between a dowager and a vicar-general in a gray-paneled drawing-room where family portraits looked down on four card tables and sixteen people playing whist. As he digested a pleasant dinner, he appreciated the provincial customs around him and why these people clung to old cards and threadbare tablecloths. There was a philosophy in their monotonous routine and indifference to luxury that made him question the value of opulence. The vibrant life of Paris felt distant.

His attention was caught by a young lady with red hands and a shy demeanor, at first awkward and unattractive. But a chance remark about Mme. de Beauseant stirred something in him, like a passage of good music in a tedious opera. An elderly lady noted the Vicomtesse was unwell and had declined an invitation to dinner, which piqued Gaston’s curiosity. The conversation revealed Mme. de Beauseant’s scandalous past with M. d’Ajuda-Pinto and her resulting isolation in Courcelles. Despite noble lineage, she was shunned because of her separation from her husband, a situation that intrigued Gaston.

He became captivated by the idea of Mme. de Beauseant, seeing her as a figure of hope and desire amid the dullness of provincial life. An obsession grew; he imagined her as a woman worth pursuing in a stagnant world. His thoughts turned to how to gain access to her, aware of the obstacles posed by her intelligence and social standing. The next morning he wandered near Courcelles, hoping for a chance encounter, his imagination fueled by romance.

He considered writing but feared rejection. Instead he planned to enlist M. de Champignelles to arrange an introduction. He told the Marquis he had a delicate matter to discuss with the Vicomtesse and asked him to inquire if she would receive him. Gaston’s charm and sincerity convinced the Marquis, who was eager to oblige a pretty woman. M. de Champignelles hurried to Courcelles and, conveying Gaston’s request, aroused Mme. de Beauseant’s curiosity despite her initial reluctance.

After deliberation, she agreed to meet Gaston, intrigued by the mystery surrounding him. Meanwhile Gaston was filled with excitement and anxiety about the meeting, hoping to impress her. He prepared meticulously, believing his appearance would shape her perception—unaware that true attraction would depend more on wit and character than looks.

As he approached Courcelles he felt a mix of confidence and nervousness, conscious that entering a room with one woman was more daunting than facing a crowd. When he was announced, he entered with a blend of ease and trepidation, ready to engage the enigmatic Vicomtesse.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A great fire burned on the hearth despite the mild weather, casting a soft light over a young woman seated in a high-backed bergère by the flames. The Vicomtesse, with graceful movements, stirred the coals and picked up a fallen glove, then pointed with her delicate hand to a chair for M. de Nueil. Her charm and poise combined a fastidious carelessness with high-bred elegance, distinguishing her from the automatons he had encountered during his two-month exile in Normandy. In her presence he felt transported back to Paris, immersed again in the beauty and sophistication he had missed.

Mme. de Beauseant had fair hair, dark eyes, and a pale complexion that gave her a regal air like a fallen angel; her hair, gathered into a crown of plaits, heightened that noble appearance. Yet beneath this beauty was an underlying sadness, a trace of past unhappiness and lost love. Alone in her silent salon, she seemed cut off from the world, haunted by memories of a youth once vibrant but now emptied. M. de Nueil felt humbled by her presence, captivated by the mixture of beauty, nobility, and misfortune that surrounded her.

When she acknowledged him, he felt the weight of his deception. He had come to deliver a message from M. de Champignelles, but the consciousness of his dishonesty made him flush. Summoning his courage, he confessed, “Madame, I do not deserve the happiness of seeing you. I have deceived you basely.” His apology, however, met with disdain; she rang for her servant to escort him out, and her manner shifted from gracious to icy.

As he left, he realized that if he went away now he would never return, and the Vicomtesse would think him a fool. He hesitated, considering that she might repent her dismissal. On a sudden impulse he turned back, claiming he had forgotten something; the servant, ignorant of the true reason for his return, followed him back into the salon.

“Jacques lighted me to the door,” he said with a half-sad smile, which softened Mme. de Beauseant’s demeanor. She invited him to sit; he did so eagerly, his eyes shining with happiness. She felt a thrill at his delight, grateful for his apparent comprehension of her complex emotions.

“Madame,” he began, “my blunder you know, but you do not know how much I am to blame.” She interrupted him with a playful gesture, warning him to be careful. Her expression then darkened as she reflected on the change in her life. “This is all very ridiculous! I cannot do as I like; I am obliged to think. What brings you here? Is it curiosity? If so, I am paying dearly for a fleeting pleasure.”

She accused him of disrespect and asked whether he had come to pity her, asserting she would rather be scorned than pitied. The tension in the room grew as she expressed her pain at his intrusion, urging him to see the impropriety of his conduct. She commanded him not to return, fearful that any further contact would provoke gossip about their relationship.

M. de Nueil acknowledged his fault, attributing it to youthful enthusiasm and a craving for happiness. He explained that his longing to see her had been natural—the weariness of exile and the inner fires that drove him. He painted himself as a young man yearning for love, who had made her the ideal woman of his dreams. He spoke of the feelings and wild thoughts that had led him to seek her out, hoping to awaken in her some indulgence for the folly he had committed in her name.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
An impassioned voice resonated in the chill solitude, bringing warmth and youthful charm. Mme. de Beauseant, stirred by the genuine expression of feeling, found herself captivated by M. de Nueil’s noble countenance, untouched by the world's harsh realities. Gaston, in the prime of his youth, seemed to embody the ideal man, untainted by ambition or vanity. Both were drawn to each other, reflecting on their feelings while trying to conceal their thoughts. M. de Nueil saw in the Vicomtesse a rare woman, whose beauty was eclipsed by her depth of feeling and goodness. Mme. de Beauseant, listening to Gaston’s youthful troubles, recognized the struggles of those who, through hard work, remain untainted by the corrupting influences of society.

As they delved into their sentiments, Gaston’s explorations were instinctive, while Mme. de Beauseant had a purpose, seeking to understand his views by presenting opposing opinions. Her wit and grace put him at ease, leading Gaston to exclaim, “Oh! madame, how could any man have left you?” The remark struck her, reviving a sense of innocence and condemning the world for her past. It offered absolution and validation, helping her rise above her fall.

She looked at the clock and, with a smile, acknowledged the fleeting nature of their encounter. Gaston, sensing the moment’s gravity, wanted to prolong their time together. Mme. de Beauseant, however, explained she must preserve her solitary life, fearing any further connection would tarnish her reputation. She spoke candidly of her past, revealing the pain of forsaken love and the lessons of solitude.

Her eloquence and beauty captivated Gaston, who longed to declare his feelings but restrained himself, fearing rejection. Torn between admiration and the dread of appearing foolish, he eventually confessed his love and wished to heal her past wounds. Mme. de Beauseant, though moved, reminded him they must remain strangers, stressing the pain of being unable to follow the man she loves through life’s changes.

After their conversation, Gaston left feeling more distant, wondering whether her charm was merely coquetry. He returned home consumed by thoughts of her, oscillating between despair and hope. At twenty-three, he grappled with youthful diffidence, feeling unworthy of her love and anxious about expressing himself.

That night he was restless and began writing letters to her, none satisfying him. The next day he returned to Courcelles by night, hesitant to be seen. After much deliberation, he penned a heartfelt letter, exposing his deep feelings and the impact she had on his life. He implored her not to dismiss his words, laying bare his vulnerability and desperation.

As he awaited her response, doubts gnawed at him, fearing she would laugh at his letter and at his love. He wished he could retract his words, feeling they were inadequate and pretentious. Overwhelmed by emotion, he longed for friends who might distract him from his turmoil.

In this state of despair, Gaston confronted the harshness of unrequited love, bearing the weight of youthful passion and the uncertainty of a future without Mme. de Beauseant.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At length, M. de Nueil received a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, marked with the arms of Burgundy. He locked himself away to read and re-read her words:

“You are punishing me for my attempt to spare you a rebuff and for my attraction to intellect. I trusted in your youth's generosity, but you have disappointed me. I spoke frankly about my position to show you that I am not to be swayed by youthful passion. My distress is heightened by my interest in you. I am kind-hearted, but circumstances force me to act otherwise. Another woman might have discarded your letter, but I read it and am responding. My answer will clarify that while I acknowledge your feelings, I do not share them. I wish to use the authority you give me over your life to help you see clearly.

“I am nearly thirty; you are just over twenty. You cannot know what your thoughts will be at my age. The vows you make lightly now may become burdensome later. I believe you would give your life for a moment of happiness, but as you age, you will find it hard to make sacrifices for me, and I would feel humiliated to accept them. A day will come when everything will urge you to leave me, and I have said that death is preferable to desertion. I must tell you that I do not love you; I cannot, and I will not. It is too late for me to yield to a blind impulse of the heart. My solace comes from God, not from earthly attachments. I read hearts too clearly to accept your friendship. I forgive your youthful folly, but for your sake and mine, I urge you to stay in your own country. Do not ruin a fair life for an illusion that cannot last. In the future, when you have achieved your destiny, you will appreciate my answer, even if you find it harsh now. You will cherish the friendship of an older woman, untainted by passion, and it will be sweet and precious to you. Farewell; think of me as you would an absent friend.”

Gaston de Nueil read her letter and wrote back:

“MADAME,--If I could cease to love you and accept the ordinary life you propose, I would deserve my fate. I will not do as you ask; my oath of fidelity will only be absolved by death. Take my life, unless you fear to carry remorse.”

When the messenger returned, M. de Nueil asked where he had left the note.

“I gave it to Mme. la Vicomtesse; she was in her carriage, about to leave.”

“For the town?”

“I don’t think so; she had post-horses.”

“Ah! then she is going away,” said the Baron.

“Yes, sir.”

Gaston de Nueil immediately prepared to follow Mme. de Beauseant. She traveled to Geneva, unaware of his pursuit. His mind was consumed with one question: “Why did she leave?” He speculated that if she cared for him, she would choose Switzerland, where they could be free from judgment.

The Vicomtesse rented a small house by the lake. One summer evening, Gaston visited her. Jacques, the servant, announced him without surprise. Mme. de Beauseant dropped her book in shock, giving him a moment to approach her and express his joy at following her.

For three years, they lived in the villa by Lake Geneva, alone and without visitors, enjoying a happiness that seemed like a dream. Their home was simple yet perfect for lovers, with beautiful views of the lake and mountains. They cherished their time together, but weighty matters soon called M. de Nueil back to France after the deaths of his father and brother. They bought the house, wishing they could take it with them.

Mme. de Beauseant followed him, selling her property to buy an estate near Manerville, adjacent to Gaston’s lands. They lived together, with Gaston graciously allowing his mother to occupy Manerville, enjoying their secluded happiness once more.

For nine years, they experienced bliss that transcended description. However, Mme. de Beauseant’s husband, the Marquis, remained in good health, complicating their situation. He was a methodical man, aware of the benefits of his life, which made it impossible for the Marquise to marry M. de Nueil.

After nine years of happiness, they found themselves in a precarious situation. Mme. la Comtesse de Nueil, Gaston’s mother, would never accept Mme. de Beauseant. The Marquise understood that the dowager would try to pull Gaston away from their relationship. She considered selling her estate and returning to Geneva but could not bring herself to do so, fearing it would show distrust in M. de Nueil.

A young heiress, Mlle. de la Rodiere, moved nearby, and Gaston met her at Manerville. This prompted a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, revealing her inner turmoil:

“My beloved angel, it seems absurd to write when we are together, but there are things I cannot say face to face. I feel anguish. You have erased my past pain, and I did not know what love was until I met you. I have never felt jealousy in our nine years together, and I have known infinite happiness. But now, I fear that you may feel burdened by our relationship. You are thirty; I am forty. This age difference weighs heavily on me. You may have thought of your future, of a marriage that would bring you wealth and children. To owe your love to pity is a painful thought.

“Have you considered leaving me? I sensed your hesitation, and it made me cry. I am too proud to let you see my tears, but I did not reproach you. I should not bind your future to my fading life. But what if I am mistaken? If you still love me, come to me after reading this letter. I will love you more than ever. Speak openly; do not deceive me. If you wish for your freedom, I will not let you see my suffering. I love you enough to prioritize your happiness over mine.”

When Mme. de Beauseant learned that her letter was in M. de Nueil’s hands, she felt overwhelmed, her thoughts numbing her senses. Meanwhile, M. de Nueil, reading her letter, found himself in a difficult position, a crisis that many young men face.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By this time he had nearly yielded to his mother’s pleadings and the attraction of Mlle. de la Rodiere, a plain young woman with a dowry of forty thousand livres. Mme. de Nueil, with maternal concern, urged him toward this respectable match, stressing the honor of being chosen by a woman who had refused many distinguished suitors. She reminded him to consider his future: he might one day secure eighty thousand livres from land and thus an easier life. If Mme. de Beauseant truly loved him, she should encourage his marriage. Using every argument, his mother left him torn.

Just as Gaston’s attachment to Mme. de Beauseant resisted the pull of convention, a letter from her changed everything. He resolved to end their liaison and marry. “One must live a man’s life,” he told himself, though he knew the choice would wound Mme. de Beauseant. Vanity and conscience compounded his sorrow; he felt genuine pity for her. He decided to prepare her gently for their separation, at first sacrificing Mlle. de la Rodiere to ease the transition. He planned to marry later, to honor Mme. de Beauseant’s wishes, and meant to write her a letter to quiet her suspicions. But he knew a woman of such perception might be devastated by a mere note.

When Jacques delivered the letter, Mme. de Beauseant trembled, dread of impending loss closing in. If he did not come to her in person, she feared all was lost; yet the hopes of a woman in love can endure much pain. As she read, an inner voice cried, “He lies!” She saw the words were not a true declaration but a calculated message. In despair she tore the letter and cast it into the fire, feeling betrayed and abandoned.

M. de Nueil went to fetch Jacques, who soon returned with a note. “Madame la Marquise has left the chateau,” he said. M. de Nueil opened the note and found it was his own letter from nine years earlier, in which he had sworn eternal fidelity to Mme. de Beauseant; at the bottom Claire de Bourgogne had written, “Monsieur, you are free.” Stunned, he went to his mother and, within three weeks, married Mlle. Stephanie de la Rodiere.

If the tale ended there it would seem trivial. But the notoriety of this true tragedy resonates with those who have known love’s profound joys and losses. After their parting Mme. de Beauseant never left Valleroy. She lived in seclusion, rarely seen except by servants, and attended mass daily.

M. de Nueil, soon after his marriage, fell into a state of emotional indifference—interpreted variously as happiness or unhappiness. His mother insisted he was content. Mme. Gaston de Nueil, like many young brides, was sweet but shallow. A month after their wedding she expected a child, fitting social expectations. Two months later, however, M. de Nueil grew increasingly pensive.

After seven months of this lukewarm contentment a seemingly trivial incident hinted at deeper turmoil. One day after hunting, M. de Nueil crossed Mme. de Beauseant’s park and asked Jacques if she still enjoyed game. Jacques confirmed, and M. de Nueil paid him to set aside whatever he shot for her. Jacques saw no harm in this, and for days M. de Nueil returned with an empty bag while Jacques discreetly delivered the game to the Marquise.

Eventually M. de Nueil dared to write Mme. de Beauseant, but his letter was returned unopened. One evening, as his wife played the piano, he suddenly rushed to the Marquise’s room. There he found her in despair, her posture emptied of hope. When she saw him, she threatened to throw herself from the window if he approached. Terrified, M. de Nueil fled.

Back home he wrote another letter, insisting it was a matter of life and death, and sent it with his servant. An hour later the servant returned with the unopened note. In despair M. de Nueil retired to another room and shot himself.

The tragic end, though at odds with young France’s conventions, was perhaps inevitable. Those who have experienced a profound soul-connection will understand Gaston de Nueil’s suicide. Love requires time and nurturing; when a man abandons deep love for a loveless marriage, he faces either despair or a cold, material life.

Mme. de Beauseant likely did not foresee that her friend’s anguish might lead to such an extreme act. She may have believed herself the only sufferer. Still, she rightly refused to accept humiliation; a wife may compromise for social reasons, but a mistress cannot submit to such a degradation, for the purity of her passion demands respect.

ANGOULEME, September 1832.

ADDENDUM

- Champignelles, De: The Seamy Side of History

- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman, Albert Savarus

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

La Grenadiere is a quaint house on the right bank of the Loire, about a mile downstream from the bridge of Tours. The broad river, dotted with green islands, flows between cliffs crowned with white stone country houses amid gardens and vineyards. With a favorable southern exposure, the region yields some of the finest fruit, thanks to generations who terraced the cliffs to grow warm-climate produce outdoors.

A church spire marks the nearby village of Saint-Cyr; further along, the Choisille flows into the Loire through a fertile valley. La Grenadiere stands halfway up the hillside, roughly a hundred paces from the church. It is an old-fashioned house of two to three hundred years, typical of picturesque Touraine. A fissure in the rock provides steps down to the “dike,” the embankment that keeps the Loire in its bed and serves as a causeway from Paris to Nantes. At the top of the steps a gate opens onto a narrow footpath between terraces with retaining walls; trees almost conceal the path, and the river view widens as one ascends.

At the path’s end a crumbling Gothic archway leads into a small garden of flowers and rose bushes hewn from the rock on the highest terrace. A wooden summer-house, draped in jessamine and honeysuckle, stands nearby. The house sits centrally in this garden above a vine-covered flight of steps, with an arched doorway giving access to extensive cellars carved into the rock. Trellised vines and pomegranate trees—whose fruit gives the property its name—surround the house. The front has two large windows flanking a rustic door and three dormer windows in a steep slate roof; the walls are yellowwashed with green-painted doors and shutters.

Inside, a crooked staircase rises to a lobby. To the right is a large dining room with white tile floors; to the left, a sitting room of similar size with saffron-colored walls and exposed walnut rafters. The first floor holds two whitewashed bedrooms with simple stone chimneypieces. Most doors and windows face south, except one that opens toward the vineyard. A small timber-framed kitchen, a later addition, connects to the house and contains a rustic pump and well, suggesting La Grenadiere was originally a vintner’s retreat for townsfolk of Tours who came for picnics or the grape harvest.

The property includes two acres of vineyard, with a steep slope at the back. A vinedresser’s cottage leans against the western gable, and stone walls or espaliers enclose and maximize the land. Nature fills any neglected corners with fig trees, wildflowers, or strawberries.

La Grenadiere is modest yet dignified, rich in fruit and fragrances, and embodies Touraine’s beauty. Its vines produce various grapes alongside figs, peaches, and melons, while Spanish broom and Italian oleanders add an exotic touch. The Loire flows below, and evening breezes carry scents of the sea and distant flowers. Clouds alter the light and the landscape, revealing views of the south bank, Tours, and surrounding hills dotted with châteaux and parks.

Owned by a prosperous burgess of Tours, this vintage house appeals to the imagination as a peaceful retreat for lovers and dreamers. Its atmosphere is suffused with happiness; the river’s sounds invite contemplation, and the owner can sit among perennial flowers and fruit, feeling the vibrant world around him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
If an Englishman rents La Grenadiere for the summer, he pays a thousand francs for six months, excluding vineyard produce. If he wants orchard fruit, the rent doubles; for the vintage, it doubles again. What could La Grenadiere be worth? With its stone staircase, triple terrace, two acres of vineyard, flowering roses, and well-head, it seems priceless. It has not been on the market since it was sold in 1690 for forty thousand francs, remaining a cherished family possession since then. The view from La Grenadiere embraces three valleys of Touraine and cathedral towers, making it impossible to assign a monetary value to the health and beauty it offers.

In the spring of a bright year during the Restoration, Mme. Willemsens arrived in Tours with her housekeeper and two children, a thirteen-year-old and an eight-year-old. She chose La Grenadiere, perhaps drawn by its distance from town. She converted the drawing-room into a bedroom, gave the children the two rooms above, and the housekeeper slept in a closet. The dining-room served as a combined sitting and drawing room, simply and tastefully furnished with plain walnut furniture; the house's charm lay in neatness and harmony rather than luxury.

Mme. Willemsens was hard to classify socially; her plain dress invited assumptions, while her manners suggested higher status. Her reserve piqued local curiosity. She was tall and slender, with delicate features and a melancholy expression. Dark eyes, sometimes revealing secret anguish, were framed by premature wrinkles. She wore a long black gown, a silk ribbon at the waist, and an English-style bonnet, signaling mourning. Her health appeared fragile, and she walked with her children to the bridge of Tours to enjoy fresh air and sunsets.

During her time at La Grenadiere she visited Tours only twice: once to inquire about the best teachers for her children and again to arrange their lessons. Her occasional appearances on the bridge interested the townspeople, but little was known of her status. The owner of La Grenadiere mentioned to friends that she signed the lease as Augusta Willemsens, Countess of Brandon, hinting at her husband's name; this revelation stayed within the business community.

To the local elite she remained a mystery. Her natural manners and sweet voice charmed those around her, but her sadness and beauty made her seem almost untouchable. Several young men fell in love, yet timidity kept them from approaching. She burned all letters she received, indicating a wish for solitude and a life free from outside concerns.

The three tutors allowed at La Grenadiere admired the close bond between Mme. Willemsens and her children. The boys, resembling their mother, drew envy from local mothers. Louis-Gaston, the elder, had dark hair and a robust demeanor, while Marie-Gaston, the younger, had almost golden hair and a delicate appearance. Their contrasting looks echoed differing personalities, Louis showing a protective nature toward his brother.

The boys were well-behaved and attentive, reflecting careful upbringing. They rose early, prayed, and observed a scrupulous morning routine. Their interactions with their mother were full of affection, laughter, and an intuitive understanding of her needs. Mme. Willemsens fostered a nurturing atmosphere, ensuring her children felt loved and valued.

Mornings included playful moments, with Louis often taking charge to protect Marie from fatigue. He noticed signs of his mother's declining strength—small worries she sought to hide—and though she reassured him, he remained vigilant. Their bond was profound, characterized by a shared life of love and mutual understanding, largely untouched by external influences.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mme. Willemsens dressed during her children's early breakfast and play because she wanted to be a pleasing vision for them. She was always ready to hear their lessons from ten in the morning until three in the afternoon, with a midday lunch in the summer-house where they all ate together. After this meal the children played while she rested on a sofa, enjoying the beautiful landscape of Touraine.

The children explored the orchard and garden, chasing lizards and investigating plants and insects, and often ran to their mother with eager questions. Mme. Willemsens embroidered during their lessons, following attentively without interrupting. She concentrated on her eldest son, Louis, treating him with respect and encouraging his ambition, while she asked little about Marie. When the lessons ended she would walk the tutor to the gate and inquire about Louis’ progress; the tutors answered frankly about his strengths and weaknesses.

Their days struck a careful balance of work and play, leaving no room for discouragement or quarrels. Her love smoothed over any difficulties and taught her sons moderation and submission through steady nurturing. She often shed tears of joy as she watched them play, reflecting on their happiness and on her own.

The hours in the garden-house were precious, full of laughter and the children’s voices. They spoke both English and French, thanks to their English nurse, and their mother skillfully guided their learning so they could understand the world around them. She supplied them with interesting books about great figures in history and used those stories to teach them about ambition and success.

While the children listened to these readings, little Marie would often fall asleep on her mother’s knee. Afterwards Mme. Willemsens would sometimes grow pensive and tearful. One evening Louis noticed her tears and asked why she was crying. She explained that she feared for their future, that she might leave them alone and without resources, and she expressed the deep love that made her sorrow all the more poignant.

Louis, puzzled, promised he would take care of Marie. She reassured him that her love kept her alive and hoped that the sunny air of Touraine might help her recover. From that day, as she spoke more of her impending death, Louis became more focused on his studies and adapted the family’s life to her melancholy. The once vibrant household grew quieter; the old servant Annette watched with growing concern as Mme. Willemsens’ health declined.

By August their life had changed markedly. Mme. Willemsens remained still beautiful but increasingly frail, and she made an effort to preserve her appearance. The house, once lively, felt somber and they rarely ventured far. Louis, feeling a deep connection to his mother, noticed her exhaustion and often suggested shorter walks.

With autumn’s sunny days and ripe fruits, Mme. Willemsens spent her time almost entirely with the children, cherishing each moment. Meanwhile Louis worked late into the night, making steady progress in his studies in preparation for the entrance examination to the Ecole Polytechnique. He frequented the bridge of Tours, where a retired naval officer told him tales of adventure; Louis asked many questions and learned of the hardships of military life. Encouraged by his mother, he began taking long walks to build endurance, climbed trees and learned to swim, slowly changing from a boy into a young man whose sunburned face reflected a new purpose.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When October arrived, Mme. Willemsens could only rise at noon. The warmth of the Loire made the air feel almost like the Bay of Naples, prompting the faculty to recommend the area. At midday, she sat in the shade with her two boys, who never left her side. With lessons over, they sought a life of closeness, free from outside worries. The elder boy lay beside her, basking in her affection, while Marie, the more restless child, gathered flowers for her. Mme. Willemsens, with tired eyes and languid movements, never complained, smiling at her vibrant children—a poignant scene enhanced by autumn's melancholy beauty.

Eventually, the doctor ordered Mme. Willemsens to stay in her room. It was brightened daily by her favorite flowers, and her children were always near. One early November day, she sat at the piano for the last time, gazing at a Swiss landscape above it and her children outside. Her fingers danced over the keys, full of passionate feeling. When the doctor arrived, he insisted she stay in bed, leaving her stunned.

After he left, she asked her older son, Louis, to take her out to the terrace for one last view of her homeland. As they stepped outside, her gaze turned skyward, captivated by the clouds. A look of anguish crossed her face as she clutched her children's hands, lamenting their uncertain future. She feared for them, wondering what they would think of her life when they grew older. After a moment of solitude, she saw Louis and Marie kneeling beside her, their love evident. Wishing to take their smiles with her, she returned to bed, never to rise again.

Days passed in a blur, with Annette and Louis keeping vigil over her. The atmosphere was heavy with the dread of impending loss. On the fifth day, the doctor banned flowers from her room, signaling the decline of life. As her condition worsened, Louis refused to leave her side. One quiet Sunday night, he noticed her hand move the curtain, and she spoke solemnly, telling him that tomorrow would be her last day. She instructed him to retrieve important papers from her table, including their birth certificates and a letter she wished to send to Lord Brandon, her estranged husband.

As she dictated the letter, she hesitated at how to sign it, finally deciding on "Louis-Gaston." She then revealed that she had twelve thousand francs saved, all that remained for them. She urged him to keep the money close, as everything would be sealed after her death. Louis vowed to care for Marie, planning to send her to school while he apprenticed at sea, determined to return successful.

Her eyes sparkled with joy at his resolve, and she felt a surge of hope. However, her strength waned, and she requested a priest. Louis fetched Annette, who hurried to find one. The next morning, surrounded by loved ones, Mme. Willemsens received the sacrament, her children kneeling beside her. As her breathing slowed, the room fell silent, and she eventually passed away, leaving her children in shock.

After her death, Annette and the vinedresser’s wife prepared her body for burial, following local customs. Only a few mourners attended her funeral, a stark contrast to the grand send-off she would have received had she lived differently. Marie, still too young to grasp the finality of death, cried as they buried her.

A simple cross marked her grave, inscribed with a plea for prayers. After the burial, the children returned to La Grenadiere for a final look at their home before leaving with Annette, who took them to her cousin’s house in Tours. Louis shared his plans with Annette, entrusting her with Marie’s birth certificate and the money for her care. The next day, he took Marie to school, offering her heartfelt advice before parting ways.

A month later, Louis-Gaston, now an apprentice on a warship, watched the French coast fade into the horizon. Alone on the vast ocean, he felt lost but was comforted by an old sailor's words. With newfound resolve, he embraced his life at sea, determined to honor his mother’s memory.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

I have always wanted to tell a simple, true story that would terrify two young lovers, driving them to seek refuge in each other’s hearts, like children clinging together at the sight of a snake. I admit that announcing my intention may spoil the tale and make me seem vain.

I played a part in this almost ordinary tragedy, so if it fails to engage you, the fault is partly mine and partly the mundane nature of reality. Life often lacks interest; the artist’s task is to select from reality those elements with poetic potential.

In 1819 I was traveling from Paris to Moulins and, for want of money, took an outside seat on the coach. An Englishman, who seemed better off, joined me, and we quickly bonded over our love of fresh air and the landscape. Conversation turned to women and love, and we soon admired “the woman of a certain age,” specifically those between thirty-five and forty. A poet would have found our exchanges rich with passion and tender confidences. Our shy fears and slight interruptions showed a youthful charm I no longer possess.

We agreed a birth certificate was meaningless and that some women were younger at forty than girls at twenty. This belief opened a boundless exploration of love. As we described our mistresses—young, charming, devoted—my companion revealed his lady was thirty-eight; I confessed to adoring a woman of forty. This freed us to share romantic follies, each trying to outdo the other in sentiment.

One of us had traveled six hundred miles for a brief meeting; the other had risked danger to meet his lady in secret. We recounted our joys and perils, reliving those moments of delight. My friend’s countess had lighted a cigar for him; mine made chocolate and wrote daily when we were apart. Our countesses were adored by their husbands, whose simplicity provided just enough danger to heighten our pleasure. But soon, our laughter was swept away by the wind.

At Pouilly I observed my new friend closely. He was well-proportioned, with an expressive face, dark hair, blue eyes, and a pallor suggesting recent illness. He had shapely hands, seemed well-informed and clever, and was worthy of a countess. He was a Vicomte with a respectable income; many a girl would have desired him.

About a league out of Pouilly the coach overturned. My companion jumped to escape but miscalculated and was crushed beneath the vehicle. We carried him to a peasant’s cottage, where, in agony, he entrusted me with a sacred task—delivering the news of his death to his mistress. He feared the shock she would feel if she learned of his death from a newspaper. He asked me to retrieve a key he wore around his neck, which I found embedded in his flesh. After giving me directions to his home in La Charite-sur-Loire for his love letters, he grew speechless and died, leaving me with the burden of his last wish.

The coachman said it was partly my friend’s fault, but I was consumed by the task ahead. At La Charite I fulfilled his wishes, but his mother was away, leaving me to comfort an old servant who fainted at the news. I took the sealed letters, burdened by the sorrow of a woman who had lost her last love.

The Countess’s chateau lay eight leagues beyond Moulins, and I had little money for the journey. Nevertheless, youthful enthusiasm propelled me to walk as fast as possible, eager to deliver the bad news before anyone else. As I neared the Chateau de Montpersan, unease about my role as the bearer of such tragic news increased.

When I reached the chateau, the open doors unsettled me. I entered and encountered barking dogs and a servant-maid who directed me to the park where Madame was. I felt lost and unsure how to find her. A little girl overheard my inquiry and, with a playful air, led me through the winding paths of the park, calling for her mother.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
I paused behind the last shrub on the avenue, adjusting my shabby attire so the lady would not mistake me for a local rate collector. Thinking back now amuses me.

Just then I spotted Juliette and her husband among the flowers; the lady's pace quickened at her child's ambiguous remark. Surprised by my presence, she regarded me coolly, disappointment visible. I fumbled for the elegant phrases I had prepared.

That hesitation let her husband approach. I awkwardly asked if they were indeed M. le Comte and Mme. la Comtesse de Montpersan, using the questions to size them up. The husband was a typical provincial nobleman: large shoes that left a deeper impression than his worn coat and trousers, a mixture of self-importance and bad manners, led by his wife but fancying himself master.

The Countess was the opposite: petite, graceful, a delicate figure that inspired desire. Dressed in white muslin with rose-colored accents, she seemed youthful and lively, reminding me of the Comtesse de Lignolles and the Marquise de B----—types familiar to any young man who has read Louvet's romance.

Choosing diplomacy, I took the Count aside with an air of mystery and requested a private word. He obliged, confident his wife could learn his secrets at will, and left her to wander off.

I told the Count of my traveling companion's death, noting his genuine affection. He expressed concern for his wife and insisted on caution in delivering the news. I explained I could not approach the Countess without first informing him, emphasizing the sacredness of the message entrusted to me. Flattered, he allowed me to proceed.

As we neared the house, Juliette glanced at us with a mix of curiosity and disdain, likely due to my appearance and the unexpected guest. I recalled her beauty in the sunlit alley and sighed.

"Alas, madame, I have made a difficult journey solely for you," I began. Her face paled.

"Is he ill?" she asked, voice dropping.

"Yes, but control yourself. He entrusted me with secrets concerning you; I am a discreet messenger."

"What is wrong with him?" she pressed.

"What if he no longer loves you?" I replied, and she reacted immediately.

"That is impossible!" she exclaimed, a faint smile flickering. Panic followed. "Is he alive?"

Her question hit me; I only nodded. Distress overwhelmed her.

"Give me an answer!" she cried.

"Yes, madame."

"Is it true? Tell me the truth! Any pain would be better than this suspense."

I could only respond with tears; she leaned against a tree, crying out in despair.

"Your husband is coming!" I warned.

"Do I have a husband?" she fled from sight.

The Count called me to dinner; I followed him into a dining room set with a lavish meal, though the Countess was notably absent. The canon of Saint-Denis inquired after her, but the Count shrugged and hastily served dinner.

After a while, a maid reported the Countess missing. Alarmed, I rushed out with the canon and the maid to search the garden and park. As we called, I recounted the tragic accident; the maid's concern for her mistress was evident.

At last we heard a muffled cry from a granary. Inside we found Juliette buried in hay, sobbing. The maid and I helped her to her room; I instructed that she not be disturbed, claiming she had a headache.

Returning to the dining room, I was surprised the Count still ate, seemingly unfazed. His indifference was explained: a serious medical condition had left him ravenous, overshadowing his concern for Juliette.

The evening was somber. I was exhausted; the canon pondered the cause of Juliette's tears while the Count, preoccupied with his meal, accepted the maid's vague explanation. We retired early.

Passing the Countess's room, I asked the servant for news. Juliette called me in, tried to speak but could not. I left, feeling the weight of her agitation.

Later that night I was awakened by the sound of curtain rings. The Countess sat at the foot of my bed, pale and drawn.

"Is it true?" she asked, voice trembling. "I don't know how I can live after this blow, but I want to know everything."

She had lost her youthful glow; her eyes were red and swollen. I briefly recounted the accident, avoiding distressing details. She listened intently, leaning forward to catch every word.

When I related the dying man's fears and the message he had entrusted to me, her tears dried into a deep pallor. I handed her letters from him; she took them mechanically, lamenting that she had burned all his other letters and now had nothing.

"I cut this lock of his hair," she said, revealing a precious token. I gave it to her; her tears fell on my hands, a moment of profound gratitude and connection.

"Ah! You love! May you always be happy," she said, clutching the lock and fleeing with her treasure.

The next morning felt surreal. I searched under my pillow for the letters but found nothing. I spent the day with Juliette, witnessing her noble spirit and selflessness.

That evening the Comte de Montpersan accompanied me to Moulins and asked if I could deliver a sum of money to a contact in Paris. I agreed, taking a rouleau of twenty-five louis d'or, which covered my journey back. On arrival I appreciated the delicate way Juliette had arranged the loan, revealing the depth of her love and thoughtfulness. What a joy to share this adventure with a woman who cherished me, saying, "Oh, my dear, not you! You must not die!"
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

DEDICATION

To M. le Baron Barchou de Penhoen.

Among all the pupils of the Oratorian school at Vendome, we are, I think, the only two who have afterwards met in mid-career of a life of letters. When we met, you were engaged upon your noble works on German philosophy, and I on this study. So neither of us has missed his vocation; and you, when you see your name here, will feel as much pleasure as I do in inscribing my work to you. Your old schoolfellow, 1840 De Balzac.

GOBSECK

It was one o’clock in the morning during the winter of 1829-30, and in the Vicomtesse de Grandlieu’s salon two guests not of her family remained. A young man took his leave; as the departing carriage died away, the Vicomtesse turned to her daughter Camille, who stood by the chimney-piece, ostensibly looking at a fire-screen but listening to the sounds from the courtyard, which stirred maternal anxiety.

“Camille,” the Vicomtesse said, “if you continue to treat young Comte de Restaud as you have this evening, I shall have to prevent him from visiting. Trust my love and let me guide you. At seventeen one cannot judge the past or the future or social standings. M. de Restaud has a mother, Mlle. Goriot, who squandered her father’s fortune and is of no birth. Though he behaves admirably toward her and his siblings, any family would hesitate to entrust their daughter’s future to him while she lives.”

“I overheard part of your conversation with Mlle. de Grandlieu,” interposed Derville, a family friend. “I have won, M. le Comte,” he added to the departing man. “I shall assist your niece.”

“See what it is to have an attorney’s ears!” exclaimed the Vicomtesse. “How could you know what I was saying to Camille?”

“I knew it from your looks,” Derville replied, settling by the fire.

Camille’s uncle approached, and the Vicomtesse sat on a hearth-stool between them.

“It’s time for a story that may change your view of Ernest de Restaud’s prospects,” Derville said.

“A story?” Camille cried. “Please begin!”

Derville’s look at the Vicomtesse showed the tale was meant for her. The Vicomtesse de Grandlieu was a leading figure in the Faubourg Saint-Germain by birth and fortune. That a Paris attorney could be so intimate with her seemed odd only until one knew the facts.

After returning to France with the Royal family, the Vicomtesse had lived on a pension from Louis XVIII, a situation she found intolerable. The Hotel de Grandlieu had been sold during the Revolution. Derville discovered defects in the title, pursued legal action, and reclaimed it for her. He also secured judgments that restored significant property, raising her income to about sixty thousand francs plus indemnities. Derville, a man of integrity and modesty, became a close family confidant.

His management of the Vicomtesse’s affairs gained him esteem in the Faubourg Saint-Germain and brought many aristocratic clients. Yet he did not exploit this popularity for personal advancement. The Vicomtesse urged him to accept a magistracy; he declined, preferring to keep his practice and social ties. His steadfast service had revealed his talents, and since Ernest de Restaud’s arrival—especially as Camille showed interest—Derville had been visiting the Hotel de Grandlieu more often.

At a recent ball Derville had taunted Camille: “It’s a pity that young man doesn’t have two or three million francs, isn’t it?”

“Is it a pity? I don’t think so,” she answered. “M. de Restaud is capable and well educated. He will have as much money as he wishes when he comes into power.”

“But what if he were already rich?” Derville persisted.

“Rich already?” Camille blushed. “All the girls would be vying for him,” she said, watching the dancers.

“Then Mlle. de Grandlieu might not be the one he favors,” Derville teased. “That blush says it all! You like him, don’t you? Speak up.”

Camille suddenly rose to leave.

“She loves him,” Derville thought.

Since that evening Camille had been unusually attentive to Derville, who approved of her feelings for Ernest. Though she felt deep gratitude for his services to her family, she had previously shown respect rather than warmth toward him, keeping a polite distance. Gratitude often weighs heavily on the next generation, which may seek to escape it.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“This adventure,” Derville began, “brings to mind the one romantic event in my life. You may find it ridiculous that an attorney speaks of romance, but once I was twenty-five and had seen some strange things. I should start by telling you about someone you could not possibly know—a usurer.

“Can you picture his sallow, wan face? I wish the Academie would allow me to call such faces the lunar type. His hair was iron-gray, sleek, and carefully combed; his features were as impassive as Talleyrand’s. His little yellow eyes, devoid of eyelashes, peered from under a shabby cap, as if afraid of the light. He had thin lips like Rembrandt’s alchemists and a sharp nose like a gimlet. His voice was low and suave; he never lost his temper. It was hard to tell his age; he seemed to have either aged prematurely or conserved his youth.

“His room was as clean and threadbare as that of an elderly spinster. In winter, the fire smoldered in a bank of ashes, never producing a flame. He moved through his day with the regularity of a pendulum, stopping mid-sentence when a cart passed to save his voice. He was thrifty with his pulse, concentrating all sensibility within himself.

“His life flowed soundlessly, like the sands of an hourglass. His victims sometimes erupted in rage, then fell silent, much like a kitchen after a fowl’s neck has been wrung.

“Toward evening, this bill of exchange incarnate would take on a human shape, and his metals would transform into a human heart. When satisfied with his day’s business, he would rub his hands, and his inward glee would escape through the wrinkles of his face, akin to a soundless laugh. Even in joy, his conversation was limited to monosyllables, and he kept a non-committal expression.

“This was the neighbor Chance found for me in the Rue de Gres, where I lived as a second clerk finishing my studies. The house was damp and dark, with all windows facing the street, resembling an old convent. It was so gloomy that the gaiety of eldest sons vanished before they reached my neighbor’s door. He and his house were alike; even so does the oyster resemble its rock.

“I was the only person he communicated with; he would borrow a light or a book, and in the evenings he would allow me to visit. These gestures resulted from four years of neighborly interaction and my sober conduct. I lived much like he did due to lack of funds. Did he have relatives or friends? Was he rich or poor? No one could answer. I never saw money in his room; his capital was likely in the Bank. He collected his debts himself, running all over Paris on skinny legs. Once, when gold fell from his pocket, another lodger returned it.

“‘That isn’t mine!’ he exclaimed, surprised. ‘If I were rich, would I live as I do?’

“He made his coffee every morning on a cast-iron chafing dish; his dinner came from a cookshop, and our old porter’s wife tidied his room at the prescribed hour. A whimsical chance named him Gobseck. When I later did business for him, I learned he was about seventy-six when we met. Born around 1740 near Antwerp to a Dutch father and a Jewish mother, his name was Jean-Esther Van Gobseck. You may recall the murder case of La belle Hollandaise? I mentioned it to him, and he replied without interest, ‘She is my grandniece.’

“That was the only remark he made about the death of his sole surviving relative. Reports said La belle Hollandaise was named Sara Van Gobseck. When I asked how his grandniece bore his surname, he smiled, ‘The women never marry in our family.’

“He had never cared to trace relatives beyond four generations on the female side. The thought of heirs was abhorrent; the idea of his wealth passing to others after his death was inconceivable.

“He was ten when his mother sent him as a cabin boy to the Dutch Straits Settlements, where he spent twenty years. The inscrutable lines on his sallow forehead concealed secrets of horrible adventures, sudden panic, unanticipated luck, and lost fortunes. He had known notable figures: Admiral Simeuse, M. de Lally, Tippoo Sahib. A long stay in St. Thomas brought him into contact with notorious pirates. He had left no stone unturned in pursuit of fortune, even trying to find treasure in Buenos Ayres. He had personal knowledge of the American War of Independence. Yet when he spoke of the Indies or America, he seemed to view it as indiscretion and would repent afterward. If humanity and sociability are a religion, Gobseck might be considered an infidel; despite my efforts to understand him, his nature remained impenetrable.

“Did he adhere to his mother’s religion? Did he view Gentiles as legitimate prey? Had he converted to Catholicism, Lutheranism, or something else? I never learned his religious opinions; he appeared indifferent rather than incredulous.

“One evening, I visited this man who had turned himself to gold, the usurer whom his clients called Daddy Gobseck, perhaps ironically. He sat motionless in his armchair, staring at the mantel-shelf, seemingly reading his statements. A lamp burned, but his face appeared paler against the background. He gestured to a chair for me but said nothing.

“What thoughts could this being have?” I wondered. “Does he know God exists? Does he know about feeling, women, happiness?” I felt pity for him, akin to pitying a diseased creature. Yet I knew that while he had millions at his command, he possessed the world in idea—having explored, ransacked, weighed, appraised, and exploited it.

“‘Good day, Daddy Gobseck,’ I began.

“He turned his face toward me, slightly contracting his bushy black eyebrows, a look that conveyed as much as a jubilant smile on a Southern face.

“‘You look as gloomy as you did when you heard about the failure of that bookseller you admired, though you were one of his victims.’

“‘One of his victims?’ he repeated, astonished.

“‘Yes. You refused to accept composition at the creditors’ meeting until he promised to pay you in full, and he gave you bills accepted by the bankrupt firm. When he resumed business, he paid you the dividend on those bills.’

“‘He was sharp, but I got it out of him.’

“‘Do you have bills to protest? Today is the 30th, I believe.’

“It was the first time I mentioned money to him. He looked at me ironically, then replied blandly, ‘I am amusing myself.’

“‘So you amuse yourself now and again?’

“‘Do you think the only poets are those who print their verses?’ he asked, pityingly.

“‘Poetry in that head!’ I thought, knowing little of his life.

“‘What life could be as glorious as mine?’ he continued, his eyes lighting up. ‘You are young; your visions are colored by youthful blood. You see women’s faces in the fire, while I see only coals. You have beliefs; I have none. Keep your illusions if you can. I will show you life with the discount taken off. You can go wherever you like or stay at home with your wife, but eventually you settle into a groove. Happiness consists in applying your faculties to realities. Anything more is false. My principles have varied with different men; I had to change them with every latitude. What we admire in Europe is punishable in Asia, and a vice in Paris becomes a necessity elsewhere. There are no hard-and-fast rules, only conventions adapted to climate. Throw a man into various social melting pots, and convictions and moral systems become meaningless. The only constant is self-interest. If you lived as long as I have, you would know that the only concrete reality worth caring about is GOLD. Gold represents every form of human power. I have traveled and found either hills or plains everywhere; place is irrelevant. Manners are universal; the battle between the poor and the rich is inevitable. It is better to exploit than to be exploited. Everywhere, you find the laboring man and the idle man; pleasures are the same, and when all sensations are exhausted, only Vanity remains. Vanity is satisfied only by gold. Our dreams require time and means to realize. Gold contains everything in embryo; it realizes all things for us.

“‘Only fools and invalids find pleasure in shuffling cards for a few pence. Only dupes think they are useful laying down political rules amid unpredictable events. Only simpletons delight in discussing stage players and repeating their sayings. What is all this but Parisian life summed up? Let us seek a higher outlook. Happiness consists in strong emotions that drain our vitality or in methodical occupation that makes existence like clockwork. A higher happiness is noble curiosity, a wish to learn Nature’s secrets, or to imitate Nature. In two words: Science and Art, or passion and calm. Every human passion, heightened in the struggle for existence, parades before me while I live in calm. As for scientific curiosity, I replace it with insight into the springs of action in man and woman. In summary, the world is mine without effort, and it has no hold on me. Listen,’ he continued, ‘I will tell you about my morning, and you will understand my pleasures.’

“He got up, bolted the door, drew a tapestry curtain across it, and sat down again.

“‘This morning,’ he said, ‘I had only two amounts to collect; the rest of the bills due I gave away instead of cash yesterday. So much saved, you see, for when I discount a bill, I always deduct two francs for a hired brougham—collection expenses. It would be absurd for my clients to make me trudge all over Paris for a few francs of discount when no man is my master, and I only pay seven francs in taxes.

“‘The first bill for a thousand francs was presented by a young fellow, a dandy with a spangled waistcoat, eyeglass, tilbury, and English horse. The bill bore the signature of one of the prettiest women in Paris, married to a Count, a great landowner. How did that Countess come to sign a bill of exchange, legally worthless but practically good business? These women, poor things, fear the scandal of a protested bill and would rather pay than fail. I wanted to find out what that bill truly represented—stupidity, imprudence, love, or charity?

“‘The second bill, signed “Fanny Malvaut,” came from a linen-draper on the brink of bankruptcy. No one with bank credit comes to me. The first step to my door means desperation; the news of his failure will soon emerge, and he has been everywhere else first. The stag is always at bay when I see him, and a pack of creditors is on his tail. The Countess lived in the Rue du Helder, and my Fanny in the Rue Montmartre. I made many conjectures as I set out this morning. If these two women could not pay, they would show me more respect than their fathers. What tricks would the Countess try for a thousand francs? She would be so nice to me, speaking in that ingratiating tone of endorsers, pouring out coaxing words, while I...’ (here the old man turned his pale eyes on me)—‘and I would remain unmoved, inexorable! So much for hypotheses. I reached the house.

“‘“Madame la Comtesse is asleep,” said the maid.

“‘“When can I see her?”

“‘“At twelve o’clock.”

“‘“Is Madame la Comtesse ill?”

“‘“No, sir, but she returned home at three o’clock this morning from a ball.”

“‘“My name is Gobseck; tell her I will call again at twelve,” and I left, leaving muddy footprints on the carpet. I enjoy leaving mud on a rich man’s carpet; it’s not spite, but a reminder of Necessity. In the Rue Montmartre, I entered a poor-looking house and looked into a dark courtyard where sunlight never shone. The porter’s lodge was grimy, the window like a shabby gown—greasy, dirty, and full of holes.

“‘“Mlle. Fanny Malvaut?”

“‘“She has gone out; but if you’ve come about a bill, the money is waiting for you.”

“‘“I will look in again,” I said.

“‘As soon as I knew the porter had the money, I wanted to know what the girl was like; I pictured her as pretty. The rest of the morning, I spent looking at prints in shop windows along the boulevard; then, just as it struck twelve, I entered the Countess’ antechamber.

“‘“Madame has just rung for me,” said the maid; “I don’t think she can see you yet.”

“‘“I will wait,” I said, sitting in an easy chair.

“‘Venetian shutters opened, and the maid hurried back.

“‘“Come in, sir.”

“‘From the sweet tone of the girl’s voice, I knew the mistress was not ready to pay. What a handsome woman I saw! She had thrown an Indian shawl over her bare shoulders, covering herself while revealing the outlines beneath. She wore a loose gown trimmed with ruffles, indicating her laundress’ bills amounted to about two thousand francs a year. Her dark curls escaped from a bright handkerchief knotted carelessly around her head. The bed lay in disorder, suggesting broken slumber. A painter would have paid to see the scene: a crushed pillow in an eider-down quilt, satin slippers gleaming on a bear-skin rug, a crumpled gown, twisted stockings, ribbon garters, a half-open fan on the chimney-piece. Drawers stood open; flowers, diamonds, gloves, a bouquet, a girdle were scattered about. The room was filled with a vague sweet perfume. Beneath all the luxury and disorder, I saw Misery lurking—she felt the edge of those fangs. Her tired face epitomized the room strewn with relics of past revelry. The scattered gewgaws, pitiable this morning, had turned heads the night before.

“‘What efforts to drink from the Tantalus cup of bliss I could read in these traces of love struck by remorse—in this visible representation of a life of luxury, extravagance, and riot. There were faint red marks on her young face, signs of fine skin; her features were coarsened, and dark circles surrounded her eyes. Yet Nature was vigorous in her; these traces of past folly did not mar her beauty. Her eyes glittered. She resembled a da Vinci Herodias, magnificently full of life and energy; nothing was starved or stinted in feature or outline; she awakened desire; it seemed there was a passion in her stronger than love.
