I was left to navigate the complexities of my heart.

In the end I understood that choices have consequences. I was forced to confront the true nature of love and sacrifice; my desires had duties attached, and the harm I caused those I cared for weighed heavily on me.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
I know nothing more destructive than the wit of an Englishwoman; it carries the pompous conviction with which the British mask their prejudices. French wit is like lace, adorning life’s joys and quarrels; English wit is corrosive, stripping flesh until only bone remains. Arabella wielded her power like a sultan, cutting down rivals with her words.

“My angel,” she said, “I can speak morality too. I’ve questioned whether my love for you is a crime, and I find it both natural and pious. God created beauty to inspire adoration. The real crime would be not to love you. That other woman insults you by comparing you to men; the laws of morality do not apply to you, for you are above them. Am I not drawing closer to divine love by loving you? Will God punish a woman for seeking the divine? My love is a weakness, while that woman’s is strength. Don’t frown; I do not wish her ill. I adore the morality that has freed you for me—because you are mine forever, aren’t you?”

“Yes.”

“Forever and ever?”

“Yes.”

“Ah! I have found favor in my lord! I alone understand his worth! You say she knows how to cultivate her estate. I leave that to farmers; I cultivate your heart.”

I try to recall this intoxicating babble to convey Arabella and the events that followed. How to describe the passion mingled with her words? She sought to obliterate the impressions left by Henriette’s dignified love with impetuous affection. Arabella’s fierce attack revealed fear and admiration for her rival. The next morning she was tearful, lamenting a sleepless night.

“What troubles you?” I asked.

“I fear my love will ruin me,” she replied. “I have given all. That woman possesses something you still desire. If you prefer her, forget me; I will not trouble you. I will go far and die, like a plant deprived of sunlight.”

She extracted reluctant declarations from me and filled with joy.

“Ah!” she exclaimed, drying her eyes. “I am happy. Go back to her; I do not want to owe you to the force of my love, but to your own will. If you return, I shall know you love me as much as I love you, which I always doubted.”

She persuaded me to return to Clochegourde. The false position I placed myself in did not strike me under her influence. Had I refused to return, I would have given Lady Dudley a triumph over Henriette. Arabella would have taken me to Paris; to go to Clochegourde now insulted Madame de Mortsauf—Arabella was sure of me. Would any loving woman forgive crimes against love? Unless she were an angel, a loving woman would rather see her lover die than know him happy with another. Thus, my leaving Clochegourde for the Grenadiere was fatal to my true love and beneficial to the transient affection that held me. Lady Dudley had calculated skillfully.

When I met the countess that morning, she was pale and depressed, like one who had not slept. The perception still given to generous hearts revealed the nature of actions the world ignores but lofty spirits call criminal. Like a child playing near a precipice, I realized she and I were separated by a universe. A wail rose within us, echoing the woeful “Consummatum est” of Good Friday. Henriette’s illusions were shattered; her heart had endured its passion. She refused to look at me, denying me the light that had shone on my life for six years. I felt the wrong of bringing into this roof, where pleasure was unknown, a face that had known joy. If I had let Lady Dudley depart alone and returned to Clochegourde, perhaps Henriette would not have resolved to treat me as a brother. But now she paid me ostentatious attentions, humiliating, like caring for a sick man.

“You were out walking early,” said the count. “I hope you have brought back a good appetite, you whose stomach is not yet destroyed.”

That remark, which made Henriette smile, completed my sense of ridicule. Day and night I could not be at Clochegourde and Saint-Cyr. By day, it was difficult to become friends with the woman I had long loved; the transition, though possible with time, felt like an illness. I was ashamed; I cursed the pleasure Lady Dudley gave me; I wished Henriette would demand my blood. I could not tear her rival apart before her, for she avoided speaking of Arabella. Had I mentioned her, Henriette would have despised my infamy. After five years of delightful intercourse, we had nothing to say; our words had no connection with our thoughts; we hid intolerable pain from each other.

Henriette feigned cheerfulness, but she was sad. She called herself my sister, yet we had no common ground and remained in constrained silence. She increased my misery by pretending to be the only victim.

“I suffer more than you,” I said at one point.

“How so?” she asked haughtily.

“Because I am the one to blame.”

Eventually her manner grew so cold that I resolved to leave Clochegourde. That evening on the terrace I bade farewell. As I took my horse, she said, “Let us walk down the avenue together, alone.” We walked slowly. At the grove marking the boundary, she stopped.

“Farewell, my friend,” she said, resting her head on my chest and wrapping her arms around me. “Farewell, we shall never meet again. God has given me the sad power to see the future. Do you remember the terror when you first returned, so young, so handsome? I saw you turn your back on me as you do now. Once again, I have seen into the future. Friend, we speak for the last time. I can hardly say a few words; part of me speaks. Death has seized something in me. You have taken the mother from her children; I ask you to take her place. You can; Jacques and Madeleine love you, as if you had always made them suffer.”

“Die!” I cried, frightened at the fire in her eyes. “Henriette, I command you to live. Swear to me you will send for Origet and obey him in everything.”

“Would you oppose God’s mercy?” she cried, misreading me.

“You do not love me enough to obey blindly, as that miserable Lady Dudley does?”

“Yes, yes, I will do all you ask,” she cried, goaded by jealousy.

“Then I stay,” I said, kissing her eyelids.

Frightened, she escaped my arms and walked home without looking back. I followed her; she was weeping and praying. We reached the lawn; I took and respectfully kissed her hand. This submission touched her.

“I am yours—forever, and as you will,” I said; “for I love you as your aunt loved you.”

She trembled and wrung my hand.

“One look,” I said, “one last of our old looks! The woman who gives herself wholly,” I cried, “gives less of life and soul than I have now received. Henriette, thou art my best-beloved—my only love.”

“I shall live!” she said; “but cure yourself as well.”

The look erased Arabella’s memory. Thus I was the plaything of two irreconcilable passions; I was influenced alternately. I loved an angel and a demon; two women equally beautiful—one adorned with virtues we decry through hatred of our imperfections, the other with vices we deify through selfishness. Returning down the avenue, seeing Madame de Mortsauf surrounded by her children waving handkerchiefs, I felt pride in being arbiter of two such destinies; proud of inspiring such great passions that death must come to whichever I abandoned. That passing conceit was doubly punished.

I do not know what demon prompted me to remain with Arabella and await the moment the count’s death might give me Henriette, for she would ever love me. Her harshness, tears, remorse, and Christian resignation testified to a feeling that could not be erased. Walking down that avenue, I was no longer twenty-five; I was fifty. A man passes quickly from youth to middle age. Perhaps these evil thoughts came from the Tuileries, from the king’s cabinet. Who could resist the polluting spirit of Louis XVIII?

When I reached the end of the avenue, I rushed back—Henriette was still there, alone. I went to bid her a last farewell, bathed in repentant tears, given, though unconsciously, to noble loves forever lost, to virgin emotions that cannot bloom again. Later, a man gives nothing; he receives; he loves himself in his mistress; but in youth he loves his mistress in himself. Woe to the man who has not had his Henriette. Woe to him who has never known a Lady Dudley. Joy to him who can find the two women in one.

After my return to Paris, Arabella and I became more intimate. We soon abandoned the conventional restrictions I had tried to keep—the external comedies and concealments that renew desire and protect the heart. Young passions, like youth, raze their forests instead of cutting timber. Arabella adopted none of these bourgeois strategies; she wished to exhibit me to all Paris as her “sposo.” She used her powers of seduction to keep me under her roof, not content with rumored scandal whispered behind fans. Seeing her happy in an imprudence that frankly admitted her position, how could I not believe her love?

But once plunged into the comforts of our illegal union, despair seized me; I saw my life bound to a course defying Henriette’s ideas and advice. From then on I lived in a rage like consumptives who, knowing death near, cannot bear scrutiny. There was no corner of my heart where I could escape suffering; an avenging spirit filled me with thoughts I dared not dwell on. My letters to Henriette depicted this moral malady and did her infinite harm. “At the cost of so many treasures lost, I wished you to be at least happy,” she wrote in the only answer I received. But I was not happy. Happiness is absolute; it admits no comparisons. Once first ardor fades, comparisons arise. All great passions first check character’s asperities; later, when lovers are accustomed, moral physiognomies reappear and natural antipathies surface. This period began with me. Less blinded, I undertook a critical examination of Lady Dudley that harmed her.

First, she lacked the qualities of mind that distinguish Frenchwomen. When a Frenchwoman loves, she metamorphoses: coquetry adorns love; she abandons dangerous vanity and claims no merit but loving well. She espouses her lover’s interests and acquires experience in a day; heedless and prodigal by nature, she manages with grace. She becomes mother, adviser, doctor, giving transformations a grace that reveals infinite love. She combines other nations’ special qualities and unifies them with wit that enlivens and varies love, relieving monotony. The Frenchwoman loves always, in public or solitude; in public she speaks by silence or looks; if speech and look fail, she finds other means. The Englishwoman, by contrast, makes her love bend to the world. Educated to icy manners, she opens and shuts her heart with a British mechanism. Passionate when no eye sees her, she is cold before the world. A lover doubts his empire seeing her immobility, her aloof countenance, the calm voice with which she leaves her boudoir. Hypocrisy becomes indifference; she has forgotten all.

A woman who can lay aside her love like a garment may be thought capable of changing it. What tempests in a man’s heart when he sees a woman take and drop her love like embroidery. These women, too completely mistress of themselves, never wholly belong to you; society’s influence prevents you reigning supreme. Where a Frenchwoman comforts with a look, an Englishwoman’s silence irritates and frets. They are so on their dignity that fashion often reigns over pleasures. An Englishwoman forces everything into form; Protestantism’s doubt and reasoning kill belief; it is the death of art and love. Passionate hearts flee worldliness.

You can understand my shock when I found Lady Dudley could not live without the world, and the English system of two lives was natural to her. When she loved, she loved madly—no woman could compare; but when the curtain fell, she banished memory of it. In public she never answered a look or smile; she was ambassadress-like, folding her phrases and elbows; her composure outraged my heart. She degraded love to a need rather than raising it to an ideal. She expressed neither fear nor regret nor desire; then, at a given hour, tenderness reappeared like a sudden fire.

Which of the two should I believe? By a thousand pin-pricks I felt the infinite differences between Henriette and Arabella. Madame de Mortsauf’s slightest departures reminded me of her: the sound of her gown, her lowered eyelids, her musical voice—a continual caress—her consistent thought. She did not halve her soul for two atmospheres. Arabella’s mind existed for the world, spent in sarcasm; she loved to rend and bite to satisfy craving. Madame de Mortsauf would have hidden her happiness; Lady Dudley chose to exhibit hers to all Paris yet kept an impenetrable English mask in public. This mixture of ostentation and dignity, love and coldness, wounded me. When I complained, she turned her tongue with triple sting, mingling boasts of love with cutting English sarcasms. In opposition she made amusement of hurting me, kneading me like dough. To any remark about keeping a medium, she caricatured my ideas. She asked if I wished her to kiss me at the opera before all Paris, seriously enough that I trembled she might. Despite passion, she was never meditative, self-contained, or reverent like Henriette; she was insatiable for excitement, noise, and celebrity; everything concerned herself, not me.

Henriette’s letter you have read—proof of how this virtuous woman obeyed the genius of a Frenchwoman—shows how she had studied my prospects, political relations, moral conquests, and with what ardor she took hold of my life in permissible directions. Lady Dudley affected the reticence of an acquaintance. She never informed herself about my affairs or likings as a man. Prodigal for herself without being generous, she separated self-interest and love too decidedly. I knew, without proving it, that to save me a pang, Henriette would have sought for me what she would never seek for herself. In any great misfortune I would have gone to Henriette for counsel; I would have been dragged to prison sooner than say a word to Lady Dudley.

The contrast extended to outward things. In France luxury expresses the man; it reproduces his ideas and gives attention value between lovers. English luxury, which at first allured by choiceness, proved mechanical. The thousand attentions at Clochegourde Arabella considered servants’ business; each had a duty as if horses. She never attached to servants; the death of the best would not affect her; money could replace him. Her selfishness was candid and created laughter. Crimson draperies covered an iron nature. The siren of amorous folly could make a young man forget the hard Englishwoman; only gradually I discovered the stony rock where my seeds were wasted. Madame de Mortsauf had seen this at a glance. She was right; Arabella’s love became intolerable to me. I have since remarked that many women who ride well have little tenderness. Like Amazons, they lack a breast; their hearts are hard in some direction.

When weariness took possession of me, when I comprehended the sanctity true feeling imparts to love, when memories of Clochegourde brought the fragrance of roses, the warmth of the terrace, and the warble of nightingales, I received a blow whose echo still wakes in my heart.

I was working in the king’s cabinet when the Duc de Lenoncourt entered. The king asked news of the countess. I raised my head hastily; the king, offended, gave me the look that precedes harsh words.

“Sire, my poor daughter is dying,” replied the duke.

“Will the king deign to grant me leave of absence?” I cried, tears in my eyes.

“Go, my lord,” he answered, withholding reprimand in satire.

More courtier than father, the duke asked no leave but got into the carriage with the king. I left without bidding Lady Dudley goodbye, leaving a note that I was sent on a mission by the king. At the Croix de Berny, I met his Majesty returning. He threw me an ironic look seeming to say: “If you mean to be anything in politics, come back; don’t parley with the dead.” The duke waved sadly. The pompous equipages rolled away to cries of “Vive le Roi!” It seemed the court had driven over the dead body of Madame de Mortsauf with utter insensibility. Though the duke was an excellent man, he would no doubt play whist with Monsieur after the king retired. The duchess had long ago given her daughter the first stab by writing of Lady Dudley.

My hurried journey was like a ruined gambler’s dream; I despaired at receiving no news. Had the confessor excluded me? I accused everyone. Beyond Tours, on the poplar-bordered road to Poncher, I met Monsieur Origet. He guessed I was going to Clochegourde and told me: “I doubt if you find her living. She is dying a frightful death—of inanition. When she called me last June, no medical power could control the disease; she had the symptoms Monsieur de Mortsauf knows, for he thinks he has them. Madame la comtesse was not in a transient condition; her disease had reached a point where science is useless; it is the incurable result of grief. Her physical condition is produced by inertia of an organ as necessary as the heart. Grief has done the work of a dagger. Don’t deceive yourself; she is dying of hidden grief.”

“Hidden!” I exclaimed.

“No,” he said, looking at me, “and since she was so seriously attacked, Monsieur de Mortsauf ceased to torment her. I am no longer needed; Monsieur Deslandes is all-sufficient; nothing can be done; her sufferings are dreadful. Young, beautiful, and rich, to die emaciated—for she dies of hunger! During the last forty days the stomach has rejected all nourishment.”

Origet pressed my hand with respect. “Courage, monsieur,” he said, lifting his eyes.

These words were like poisoned arrows. I sprang into the carriage and promised a reward if I arrived in time.

Despite impatience, the distance seemed short as bitter reflections crowded my soul. Dying of grief, yet her children were well? Then she died through me! My conscience arraigned me. What weakness in human justice, which avenges open deeds but forgives murderers of the soul! How many victims—Madame de Beauseant, the Duchesse de Langeais, Lady Brandon—our period teems with such events. Who does not remember the young woman who poisoned herself from jealousy? Society and science are accomplices in crimes without assizes. The world declares no one dies of grief; our scientific nomenclature provides labels that serve as passports to the coffin. Is there some law we do not know? Must the centenary pitilessly strew earth with corpses? My God! do I belong to the race of tigers?

Remorse gripped me as I entered Clochegourde’s avenue on a damp October morning. Jacques, Madeleine, and the Abbe Dominis knelt at the foot of a wooden cross. I sprang from the carriage toward them, my heart aching. The old huntsman stood apart.

I kissed Jacques and Madeleine, who gave a cold look. The abbe rose; I took him by the arm.

“Is she still alive?” I implored. He bowed sadly and said it might be better to wait; she was greatly changed. “It is necessary to prepare her for this interview, or it might increase her suffering—death would be a blessed release.”

He told me how for six months the countess had suffered more despite Origet’s care. The doctor had come every evening for two months. “To heal the body, the heart must first be healed,” the doctor had said. As the illness increased, the countess’s words grew bitter. She called on earth to keep her, then repented. Such alternations rend the heart. “She falls, but each time she stumbles she rises higher toward heaven,” he said.

I cried, “Surely this pure lily will flower in heaven?”

“You left her still a flower,” he answered. “You will find her consumed, purified, pure as a diamond buried in ashes.”

The count, white-headed, hurried out, declaring, “She was right! He is here! ‘Felix, Felix, Felix has come!’ she kept crying. My dear friend, death is here. Why did it not take a poor madman like me?”

I walked toward the house but the Abbe Birotteau stopped me. “Madame la comtesse begs you will not enter yet.” Inside, servants moved, all dumb with grief. Manette searched for Madeleine; we paced the lawn. I saw the dear “mignonne” gathering autumn flowers. Overcome, I sat, broken.

“Poor Felix,” said the count, “she forbade me to write to you. She knew how much you loved her.”

All was gloom. The servants wept; work was left undone; the place seemed uninhabited. The count, speaking in bursts, blamed and then wept for his wife, mixing despair with accusations. Madeleine told me her mother was ready; the Abbe Birotteau accompanied me. He warned, “I have done all to prevent this meeting. The salvation of this saint required it. I shall be present to protect you against yourself and perhaps against her. Respect her weakness. She is dying of hunger and thirst. Since morning she suffers feverish irritation. Pray God she die not in a moment of doubt.”

We reached the chamber. Henriette sat in white, before the fireplace, flowers about her. The abbe’s amazement at the room’s restored tenderness showed she had prepared to receive me. Her shrunken face had the greenish pallor of a magnolia; hollow eyes shone; her forehead kept proportion; inward fires issued despite waxy features. The smile on her whitened lips resembled death’s grin. Her robe showed emaciation. She knew she was changed; thought filled her with bitterness. No longer the sublime Madame de Mortsauf, she was a nameless thing struggling against annihilation. I took her dry, burning hand to kiss. Her discolored lips tried a smile—the forced smile of one seeking to hide irony, expectation, or fury.

“Ah, my poor Felix, this is death,” she said, “and you do not like death; odious death. This is the end of love; I knew it would be so. Lady Dudley will never see you thus surprised. Ah! why have I so longed for you, Felix? You have come at last, and I reward your devotion by the same horrible sight that made the Comte de Rance a Trappist. I, who hoped to remain beautiful and noble in your memory, destroy your illusions! True love cannot calculate. But stay; do not go, stay. Monsieur Origet said I was better this morning; I shall recover. Your looks will bring me back to life. When I regain strength and nourishment, I shall be beautiful again. I am scarcely thirty-five; there are many years of happiness before me—we will leave Clochegourde and go to Italy.”

Tears filled my eyes; I turned to the window. She put her burning lips to my ear: “My anguish has been in not seeing thee! Did you not bid me live? I will live; I will ride on horseback; I will know life, Paris, fêtes, pleasures!”

Those tones pictured a lifetime’s battles, the anguish of true love lost. The countess rose with the motion of a child. The confessor fell on his knees and prayed. She flung an arm around my neck, kissed me violently, and said, “You shall not escape me now!” She fainted; I laid her on the bed. “You carried me thus before,” she murmured.

Deslandes entered, surprised at the decorations. “We must suffer much to die,” she said faintly. The doctor felt a pulse and murmured that it was the forty-second day since she had eaten or drunk. The Abbe Birotteau took me to the garden. “Let us leave her to the doctor,” he said; “they will wrap her in opium. Well, you have heard her—if indeed it is she.”

“No,” I said, “it is not she.”

Stupefied, I hid in a punt to be alone. I tried to detach myself from my life—torture like that which afflicts adulterers. My life was a failure. Despair suggested to die beside her or bury myself at Meilleraye among Trappists. I looked at the windows where Henriette was dying, fancying I saw the light burning the night I betrothed my soul to hers. Ought I not to have followed the simple life she created, keeping faithful while I worked in the world? Had she not bidden me become great to be saved from shameful passions? Chastity! Love as Arabella understood it disgusted me.

Madeleine walked slowly and sat on a bench. When I approached, she rose and returned to the house, hating me; she fled from her mother’s murderer. At the portico Jacques sat, expressionless. Young persons carrying death are often unmoved at funerals. I longed to question Jacques.

“You know, Jacques,” I said, “that in me you have a most devoted brother.”

“Your friendship is useless; I shall follow my mother,” he replied with a sullen look.

He showed me his handkerchief stained with blood.

They each had a fatal secret. The brother and sister avoided each other. Henriette lay low; all was ruins at Clochegourde.

“Madame is asleep,” Manette said, happy that the countess was out of pain. Small joys matter then. “Monsieur Deslandes ordered the flowers taken away; they excited Madame’s nerves,” Manette added. She thought the flowers caused delirium.

I returned as the sunset gilded Azay’s roofs. All was calm and pure. A soft light lit the bed where my Henriette lay wrapped in opium. The body seemed annihilated; the soul reigned. Blanche and Henriette, two sublime faces of one woman, reappeared. The abbes sat at the bed. The count stood stupefied. I took a seat on the sofa. We exchanged looks of admiration mingled with tears. The Abbe Dominis and I communicated in signs: yes, angels were watching. Her face cleared; the green of suffering gave way to pure white pallor; she seemed to embrace the divine.

Madeleine knelt, adoring, crying “My mother!” Jacques smiled; he would follow her. “She is entering the haven,” said the Abbe Birotteau. The Angelus rang; Nature’s poetry joined the sacred hour. We burst into tears. Even servants knelt and prayed. The doctor signaled that this sleep was the hour without pain; it was time for the last sacraments. At nine o’clock she awoke quietly with gentle eyes, and we beheld her in former beauty.

“Mother! you are too beautiful to die—life and health are coming back to you!” cried Madeleine.

“Dear daughter, I shall live—in thee,” she answered, smiling.

They embraced. The count kissed his wife. She colored when she saw me.

“Dear Felix,” she said, “this is, I think, the only grief I have ever caused you. Forget all I may have said—I, a poor creature beside myself.” She held out her hand; I took and kissed it. “As in the old days, Felix?”

We left the room during the last confession. I approached Madeleine. In the presence of others she could not escape civility; but, like her mother, she looked at no one.

“Dear Madeleine,” I said softly, “what have you against me? Why such coldness before death, which should reconcile us?”

“I hear in my heart what my mother says,” she replied, with a look like Ingres’ “Mother of God.”

“And you condemn me when your mother absolves me—if indeed I am guilty.”

“You, you—always yourself!” Her voice revealed determined hatred, implacable as Corsican judgment.

An hour passed in silence. The Abbe Birotteau returned after receiving the countess’s confession. We found her sitting up, dressed in the long white garment to be her shroud. She had had her letters burned—sacrifice she had delayed until this hour. She smiled with eyes moist and inward lucidity; she saw the promised land.

“Dear Felix,” she said, pressing my hand, “stay with us. You must be present at my last scene; be not absent from the least painful among many things in which you are concerned.”

She made a sign and the door closed. She knelt before the astonished count, then laid her head on his knees.

“Though I have been a faithful wife to you,” she said faintly, “I have sometimes failed in my duty. I have given a friendship outside my family more affectionate care than to you. Perhaps I have irritated you by comparisons. I have had a deep friendship which no one—not even he who has been its object—has fully known. Though virtuous by human laws and an irreproachable wife, other thoughts, voluntary or involuntary, have crossed my mind, and in this hour I fear I welcomed them too warmly. But having loved you and continued submissive, I now pray for your blessing with a clean heart. I shall die without a bitter thought if I can hear a tender word for your Blanche, for the mother of your children—if you forgive her those things she did not forgive herself till reassured by the great tribunal which pardons all.”

“Blanche, Blanche!” cried the broken man, shedding tears on her head. “Would you kill me?” He raised and kissed her solemnly. “Have I no forgiveness to ask? Have I never been harsh?”

“Perhaps,” she said. “But dear friend, indulge a dying woman’s weakness; tranquillize me. Will you grant that I leave to our friend here that pledge of my affection?” She showed a letter on the mantel. “He is now my adopted son, and that is all. The heart makes bequests; my last wishes impose a duty on that dear Felix. If you do not wish to marry Madeleine, at least seek my soul’s repose by making Monsieur de Mortsauf as happy as he can be. Farewell, long farewell like that I gave last night to our valley, where I shall rest and where you will often—will you not?—return.

Henriette.”

I fell into abyssal reflections as the depths of her life were lighted by this expiring flame. My clouds of egotism rolled away. She had suffered as much as I—more, for she was dead. She believed others would be kind to her friend; she was blind to her daughter’s enmity. That last proof of tenderness pained me. Poor Henriette wished to give me Clochegourde and her daughter.

From that dread day when I first followed my noble Henriette to the grave, the sun has been less warm, nights more gloomy, movement less agile, thought more dull. Some departed we bury in earth; others are buried within us, their memory mingling with our heartbeats; they are in us by a metempsychosis of love. A soul is within my soul. When some good thing is done by me, that soul acts and speaks. All that is good within me issues from that grave; sarcasm and bitterness are mine. Natalie, when next my eyes are darkened or my lips silent, do not ask, “Of what are you thinking?”

I ceased writing for days; these memories were too bitter. I owe you an account of events that followed; they need few words. Henriette’s letter set a star of hope before me. To live at Clochegourde with Madeleine, consecrating my life to her, seemed a rescue. But it was necessary to know her true feelings. As I bade the count farewell, we walked the terrace. He spoke of his loss but soon of the future; he feared his daughter, who lacked her mother’s gentleness. Madeleine’s firm character alarmed him; he foresaw a struggle between father and daughter that would end his days in bitterness. His son was to leave; his daughter marry; what son-in-law might she have? Though talking of dying, he feared being alone without sympathy.

Madeleine could see me from the terrace but would not come down. I seized a moment to beg the count to call her, saying I had a last wish of her mother to convey. The count fetched her and left us on the terrace alone.

“Dear Madeleine,” I said, “if I speak to you, it should be here where your mother listened to me. I know your thoughts; but are you not condemning me without knowledge of facts? My life and happiness are bound up in this place; you know that, yet you seek to banish me by coldness instead of brotherly affection that death should have strengthened. If my mother’s name still has power, in her name I beg you never to return me to Clochegourde so long as you are in it. The sight of me causes a repugnance I cannot express, which I shall never overcome.”

She stopped me.

“Monsieur,” she said, voice trembling, “I know all your thoughts; but I shall not change my feelings. I would rather fling myself into the Indre than ally myself to you. If my mother’s name has power, in her name I beg you never to return while I am here. The sight of you causes repugnance I cannot overcome.”

She bowed with dignity and returned to the house without looking back. The searching eye of that young girl had discovered her mother’s secrets; her hatred of the man she fancied fatal to her mother may have been increased by a sense of complicity.

All before me was chaos. Madeleine hated me without considering cause or victim. The edifice of my happiness fell in ruins. I alone knew the life of that unknown woman; none had fathomed her heart. Strange truth: how many families have their Henriette! How many noble feelings leave earth with no historian to measure their depths! Mothers know their children little; so with husbands, lovers, brothers. When Madeleine disappeared, I left with a broken heart. Bidding farewell to my host at Sache, I started for Paris, following the right bank of the Indre.

################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

At the story's outset, the Stanhope press and ink-distributing roller were not yet common in small provincial printing houses. In Angoulême, tied to Parisian typography by its paper mills, only primitive wooden presses were used. “The press groans” was literal: leather ink-balls were still employed, and the pressman inked by hand. This old-world mechanism, cherished by Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, is central to the tale.

Séchard, once a journeyman pressman, survived the Requisition of 1793 and became the sole worker in a printing house after the master’s death. Illiterate, he was unexpectedly granted a master printer’s license by a Representative of the People, allowing him to buy the business at a bargain. He partnered with a nobleman, M. le Comte de Maucombe, who helped print decrees while staying hidden from authorities.

By 1795, after the Terror, Séchard needed a new compositor and hired an Abbé who later became a Bishop. Despite his lack of education, Séchard learned to manage the business, developing a keen sense for costs and profits. His avarice grew as he recognized the potential for wealth; he bought paper when prices fell and became his own landlord, owning the old printing house.

Séchard had one son, David, whom he sent to grammar school hoping to train a successor. David excelled academically, and Séchard—who scorned book learning—insisted he economize while studying in Paris. David completed his education without costing his father anything.

The printing house held a monopoly on official printing in the region, but the Cointet Brothers sought a second printer’s license, threatening Séchard’s business. Confident in David’s abilities, Séchard looked forward to retirement, believing his son would meet the competition.

Now in his seventies, Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard had developed a fondness for alcohol, which his late wife had once kept in check. His appearance reflected his drinking: a bloated face, cunning gaze, three-cornered hat, and old-fashioned clothing suited his character.

When David returned home, Séchard welcomed him with feigned warmth, eager to discuss business. Despite David’s request to postpone serious matters, Séchard was set on negotiating the sale of the business, viewing his son not only as offspring but as a potential adversary in business dealings.

The printing house, established since Louis XIV’s reign, was a large room with a glass door opening to the workshop. Customers often stumbled through the shop, distracted by the printing process. The premises included a yard with vines and a kitchen, where inky runoff mixed with kitchen waste, giving the place a notorious reputation.

The living quarters above the printing office were simple and cluttered, reflecting Séchard’s greed. The dining room was sparsely furnished, and the bedroom contained old furniture unchanged since the predecessor’s time. The state of the house mirrored the old printer’s character and relentless pursuit of profit.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The sitting-room had been partially modernized by the late Mme. Séchard, with powder-blue wainscoting and Oriental scene wallpaper. The furniture was sparse: a few lyre-backed chairs with blue leather cushions. The arched windows overlooking the Place du Mûrier were bare, and the room lacked a clock, candle sconces, or a mirror, for Mme. Séchard had died before finishing her decorating plans. Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, unable to see the value in improvements that didn’t yield financial returns, left the room as it was.

Jérôme-Nicolas brought his son David into the room and pointed to a valuation of the printing plant on the table. “Read that, my boy,” he said, his gaze wandering drunkenly between the paper and David. David read the inventory aloud, noting the old presses and their valuation. “These presses are old and not worth much; they’re only fit for firewood,” he exclaimed, and dropped the paper.

“Old tools?” Séchard retorted. “Let’s go downstairs, and you’ll see how they work. You’ll regret calling them sabots. They’ve served me twenty years and will last you a lifetime.”

He staggered down the staircase and, to David’s surprise, demonstrated the presses’ functionality with agility. “Where’s the English press that can match this?” he challenged. He boasted that the presses could generate a good income without a foreman and dismissed David’s interest in newer machinery, claiming it would ruin the type. David was skeptical about the valuation of the type, deeming it overpriced.

“After twelve years of wear, that type is as good as new,” Séchard insisted, defending the quality from M. Vaflard’s foundry. David noted the price was excessive compared to competitors, but Séchard remained adamant about his equipment’s value.

The inventory itemized every tool and amounted to thirty thousand francs, including license and goodwill. David questioned whether he could afford it. Séchard grew anxious over his son’s silence, preferring a heated argument to quiet, and continued to extol the virtues of old tools, claiming they were worth more than new ones.

Feeling cornered, David realized he had to accept the deal or walk away. He knew his father had bought the business for ten thousand francs, making the current asking price seem exorbitant. “Father, you’re cutting my throat!” he exclaimed.

Séchard argued that the license alone was worth a fortune, citing income from advertisements and other work. He insisted David was foolish to haggle. Attached to the valuation was a partnership deed: Séchard would rent the premises to David for twelve hundred francs a year and reserve a room for himself.

David recalculated the license and goodwill’s worth and thought he might manage to pay off the debt. He accepted the terms, surprising Séchard, who wondered whether his son had savings. The next day, Séchard had the apprentice move David’s belongings to the attic, leaving him with three empty rooms and no funds for wages. When David asked for financial help, Séchard refused, claiming he had already paid his share.

David felt humiliated by his father’s refusal to aid him. He realized he was alone and that his father sought to profit from him. Desperate, he mentioned his mother’s fortune, but Séchard dismissed it as merely her beauty and intelligence. David understood that any claim to his mother’s inheritance would require a long, costly legal battle. Accepting his situation, he resolved to work hard, reminding himself he was ultimately building his own future.

Séchard then mentioned a “treasure” he was leaving David—Marion, a capable country girl who managed many tasks in the printing house. Séchard set off for the countryside, uneasy about payment for the business, trusting David’s education would ensure he fulfilled his obligations.

Séchard had bought a vineyard in Marsac, where he now spent his days. He fretted over the thirty thousand francs and often visited David’s workshop to check business, sensing his son was not managing well, especially as the Cointet Brothers gained ground in the printing trade.

David, indifferent to the political climate, struggled to secure work. The Cointets, aligning themselves with the local clergy, began monopolizing the market while David’s business dwindled. They slandered him as a Liberal and untrustworthy because of his father’s reputation.

As David’s business faltered, the Cointets offered to buy the printing house, prompting Séchard’s intervention. He insisted David keep the paper, arguing it was essential for their connection with the Liberal clientele. Séchard negotiated a price of sixty thousand francs for the business to protect David’s interests.

Ultimately David was forced to sell the paper to the Cointets, marking the decline of the Séchard establishment. Séchard, now detached, visited less frequently though he still felt a connection to the old printing house.

David’s situation worsened when he met Lucien Chardon, an old school friend fallen into poverty after his father’s death. Lucien struggled to find his place; David offered him a position as a printer’s reader in an effort to help.

Their friendship deepened as they shared dreams: Lucien aspired to be a poet, David to pursue natural science. They discussed Lucien’s father’s ideas for refining sugar and making paper, which sparked David’s interest in potential ventures.

Their ambitions, however, distracted them from the printing house’s pressing needs. While the Cointet Brothers thrived, Séchard & Son scraped by, making only a fraction of what they needed. The Cointets, recognizing David’s lack of business acumen, let his establishment continue operating at a loss, ensuring no serious competition.

The condition of the printing house reflected Séchard’s avarice: repairs were neglected and maintenance ignored. The building was dilapidated, a testament to years of disregard, and the workshop was cluttered and grimy, mirroring the struggles of its occupants.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One day in May 1821, David and Lucien stood by the window overlooking the yard, waiting for the men to leave for dinner. David, uneasy in the office’s atmosphere, led Lucien outside to sit under the vines, where sunlight revealed their contrasting features and characters.

David had a robust, fighter’s build: broad shoulders, a swarthy face suggesting struggle, thick black hair and deep-set eyes that hinted at wisdom laced with melancholy and an awareness of social limits born of humble origins. Beside him, Lucien was graceful and beautiful, with a refined profile and striking blue eyes that held youthful innocence. His delicate features and slender frame gave him an almost feminine look, yet his ambition and sharp intellect were clear; he examined society pragmatically and could contemplate morally dubious means to succeed.

Though both were low in social standing, they aspired to higher ideals, which made them bitter as they faced reality. Lucien, with a Gascon temperament, was bold and adventurous; David, physically strong, was more introspective and cautious. Their friendship blended admiration and imbalance: David often yielded to Lucien’s charm, viewing himself as the more ordinary.

For three years they shared a bond nourished by literature and art, immersing themselves in great writers and thinkers. They worked tirelessly, dreaming of fame as a temporary escape from harsh circumstances.

One day David excitedly brought a new volume of poetry by André de Chénier, which moved them both. The verses offered solace and a sense of kinship with the poet’s struggles; as they read, the yard seemed to fill with the promise of their aspirations and the possibility of transcending their circumstances.

Their reverie was broken by Cérizet, an apprentice who brought a stranger wanting to print a monograph. Lucien, eager to impress, engaged the author, who mentioned Mme. de Bargeton, a woman Lucien admired. Lucien blushed and thanked the author for her interest, revealing his admiration.

After the author left, David questioned Lucien about Mme. de Bargeton; Lucien confessed his passionate love. David warned him of social barriers, but Lucien hoped love could surmount them. He declared he would not visit Mme. de Bargeton again unless David was welcomed too, showing loyalty to his friend.

As Lucien walked through Angoulême, he reflected on the distance between his class and Mme. de Bargeton’s, feeling both hope and anxiety about her reaction. The city, perched on a crag, was split between the upper town, home to nobility, and the lower town, where trade thrived. That division bred tension: the upper class looked down on merchants and tradespeople, resisting change and viewing outsiders with suspicion, which created deep animosity between classes.

Despite government efforts to modernize Angoulême, the upper town remained stagnant, clinging to old values. The social hierarchy was rigid: the nobility kept their exclusivity, and the bourgeoisie resented their disdain. This animosity would later fuel social upheaval as the rising middle class sought recognition and respect.

In this climate, Lucien’s desire to gain acceptance in Mme. de Bargeton’s world was a major challenge. His origins as a druggist’s son made him an outsider in her circle, yet his literary ambitions and charm set him apart. Mme. de Bargeton, a woman of artistic inclination, had a history that shaped her character and influenced Lucien’s path.

As Lucien navigated love and ambition, societal barriers loomed, but his determination to bridge the gap between his world and hers drove him. David’s mixture of admiration and concern reflected the complexity of their friendship: he understood Lucien’s depth of feeling but saw the obstacles clearly. Lucien’s pledge to include David in any future visits underscored both his loyalty and his hope that friendship might soften social distance.

The story of Lucien and Mme. de Bargeton would unfold against a society grappling with divisions and shifting fortunes—an Angoulême where tradition and modernity, nobility and bourgeoisie, ambition and constraint met. Their relationship, and Lucien’s struggle for acceptance, would be shaped by those tensions and by the personal qualities that set him and David apart.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
M. de Bargeton was the great-grandson of a Bordeaux alderman ennobled under Louis XIII. His line suffered decline: his grandfather, M. de Bargeton the Waster, squandered the family wealth. By 1789 the current Bargeton inherited entailed lands and a house in Angoulême but had lost most seignorial rights, retaining only modest rental income. In 1805 he married Mlle. Marie-Louise-Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, a noblewoman from a once-prominent family. Her father, M. de Nègrepelisse, lived modestly on his wife's estate, farming and distilling brandy.

Mme. de Bargeton developed a love of music and literature under the care of Abbé Niollant, who educated her during the Revolution. He taught languages, music, and other subjects, fostering her independence; but his influence also made her haughty, convinced of her superiority over those around her. Her father's thrift and provincial isolation reinforced this attitude, and she resisted marriage, valuing autonomy over submission to a man.

Determined to find a suitable match who would not challenge her intellect, M. de Nègrepelisse chose M. de Bargeton: a man of sufficient breeding but limited mind. Naïs accepted, aiming to pursue her social ambitions within the constraints of the match. Despite an age gap—she was thirty-six, he fifty-eight—the marriage appeared mutually advantageous.

Settled in Angoulême, the couple awaited Naïs's inheritance to move to Paris. Their modest income and provincial life, however, stifled her intellectual yearnings and produced absurdities in her character. She became overly dramatic, expressing emotions in theatrical language and seeking validation through grand gestures. Passions that might once have been elevated became trivialized in the small-town setting.

Mme. de Bargeton’s life turned into a cycle of unfulfilled hopes and isolation. As the reality of her stagnant existence set in, her longing for love intensified. The rise of the Empire and the fame of military heroes rekindled her romantic dreams. She fell for a young officer, but his death in battle left her heartbroken, amplifying her sense of loss and regret.

For years she lived in solitude, her dignity growing into affectation. Then M. du Châtelet arrived: charming, ambitious, and disruptive. He ingratiated himself into her circle, using music to win her favor and stirring a renewed sense of youth in her. He recognized her potential as a wealthy widow and hoped to advance his own aims through the connection.

Du Châtelet’s presence shocked the local elite, who regarded her salon as a bastion of high society; his outsider status challenged that order. Despite the scandal, Mme. de Bargeton welcomed him, drawn to his charm and the excitement he brought. His entrance altered the dynamics of her social world, revealing the tensions of ambition, love, and provincial expectations.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Political opinion was verbose and often exaggerated; the Quotidienne showed a lukewarm loyalty, while Louis XVIII was viewed as a Jacobin. The women in society were mostly awkward and poorly dressed, lacking completeness in appearance and conversation. Châtelet, however, found some solace in company because of his interest in Mme. de Bargeton. The noble spirit and traditions of good breeding in the countryside overshadowed many deficiencies, and their loyalty to the House of Bourbon was a mark of honor. Their political steadfastness gave them a sense of elevation, akin to the value placed on cowrie shells by Bambara Negroes.

Some women, flattered by Châtelet, saw in him the qualities absent in their own men, which soothed their vanity. They all aspired to be near the Imperial Highness. Châtelet endured much insolence but maintained his position by engaging the clergy and indulging the queen of Angoulême in her local foibles, sharing new literature and poetry with her. Mme. de Bargeton, enamored with the revival of the Bourbons, longed for the genius of Paris, which led Châtelet to tell her of a young poet in Angoulême, claiming he was a promising talent.

Lucien, the poet, was unaware of the attention he would receive. His headmaster had praised his work, and Lucien was thrilled at the prospect of being welcomed into the Hôtel de Bargeton. His sister Eve, secretly supporting him, saved money to buy him new shoes and clothes, preparing him for this significant moment. She advised him on how to behave in aristocratic circles, and as he left she felt a mix of pride and anxiety about his future.

Upon arriving at the Hôtel de Bargeton, Lucien found the house less grand than he had imagined, but he was captivated by Mme. de Bargeton, who embodied the poetry he admired. She was impressed by his beauty and diffidence, and their meeting was filled with a mutual admiration. Lucien was enchanted by her, overlooking her faded appearance, and he felt a connection that promised future encounters.

Châtelet, sensing a rival in Lucien, tried to assert his influence over the young poet, offering unsolicited advice about the social dynamics of Angoulême. He expressed his own feelings for Mme. de Bargeton, claiming she would soon be his. Despite Châtelet's attempts to intimidate him, Lucien continued to visit Mme. de Bargeton, growing accustomed to the condescension of the nobility, which at first flattered him but later stirred resentment.

Lucien's infatuation with Naïs (Mme. de Bargeton) deepened as she flattered him and treated him as a project to nurture. Their relationship was a mixture of familiarity and pride, with Naïs sometimes acting as patroness and at other times showing tenderness. Lucien experienced the tumult of first love, and as their bond grew Naïs began to address him more intimately, further solidifying their connection.

One evening Lucien discovered Naïs looking at a portrait of a deceased lover, which stirred jealousy in him. Despite this, he boldly declared his feelings, and their relationship progressed, with Naïs encouraging Lucien's literary ambitions. She introduced him to music and literature, fostering his growth while also revealing her own vulnerabilities.

As their relationship became public, the townspeople reacted with shock and gossip, leading to a social uproar. Châtelet, feeling threatened, sought to undermine Lucien's standing. Mme. de Bargeton defended her poet, arguing that genius could arise from humble beginnings, and she used her wit to counter the nobles' disdain.

Lucien's presence in society became more pronounced as Mme. de Bargeton hosted gatherings to showcase his talent. She encouraged him to adopt the noble name Rubempré, promising to help him gain favor at court. This idea excited Lucien, who began to abandon his earlier ideals of equality in favor of social ambition.

Under Naïs's influence, Lucien's aspirations grew, and he resolved to achieve fame and success, even at the cost of his principles. He wrote a heartfelt letter to Naïs, expressing his desire for her to help his friend David, revealing his deep emotional struggles and ambitions. Lucien's dual desire for love and recognition drove him to navigate the complexities of his relationships, as he sought to balance his artistic ambitions with the realities of social advancement.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As Lucien descended from Angoulême, he worried whether the great lady was angry with him and if she would receive David. He perceived the vast gulf between a queen and her favorite, recognizing that David could not easily traverse the same social terrain. Lucien did not know that receiving David could ruin Mme. de Bargeton: mingling with the lower classes would bring her ostracism. While many sins are pardoned for the powerful, breaching social law by associating with commoners was unforgivable.

Lucien’s aristocratic instinct sensed trouble, and he felt alarmed. He understood that noble bearing does not always accompany noble feelings; many notable men did not fit the aristocratic mold. David lacked the social graces Lucien possessed, and Lucien feared the ridicule David would face. After a poetic reverie, Lucien shifted to worldly wisdom, regretting his impulsive letter to Mme. de Bargeton. He foresaw the harsh realities of success and the sacrifices required, longing for the simpler pleasures of his past life.

He thought it nobler to win success by his own efforts rather than through a woman’s favor. Inspired by Napoleon’s rise from humble origins, Lucien resolved to pursue his ambitions without relying on patronage. Still, he felt shame about his father’s shop, seeing it as an obstacle to his aspirations.

Back home, Lucien was greeted by his father, M. Postel, who reminisced about Lucien’s father’s past achievements. Lucien felt a sting of resentment at the mention of that legacy. He eagerly searched for Mme. de Bargeton’s reply among the shop’s items and found a letter that expressed her willingness to help David while also revealing her prejudice against lower social standing. Lucien read this as a victory, convinced David would shine in her company.

Eve, Lucien’s sister, noticed his elation and voiced hopes for his happiness. Lucien, absorbed in his ambitions, reassured her they would all be happy together, though Eve sensed his priorities shifting. David, feeling unworthy of the social elevation Lucien sought, decided to decline Mme. de Bargeton’s invitation. He affirmed his commitment to his own path as a printer, emphasizing the social differences between them.

David’s humility and loyalty moved Lucien, who felt a deep bond with his friend. This moment of connection made Lucien reflect on the sacrifices of those who supported him. Yet his ambition began to eclipse his gratitude, and he grew ever more focused on his own advancement.

As Lucien prepared for his first public reading, David suggested choosing a different poem, fearing Lucien’s biblical piece might not resonate with the audience. Eager to impress, Lucien agreed. Meanwhile, David and Eve shared a quiet moment that hinted at mutual affection.

At the reading, Lucien arrived early and encountered M. de Bargeton, who was socially awkward and ineffectual in conversation. His limited intellect and reliance on superficial pleasantries underscored the social dynamics at play. Devoted to his wife, he let her manage their social life and followed her lead without question.

Lucien’s exchanges with M. de Bargeton exposed the challenges he faced in navigating high society. The evening was marked by Lucien’s ambition and the complex relationships around him, setting the stage for the conflicts and aspirations that would shape his future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
There was discussion about nominating the mute gentleman for a deputy, but Lucien had not yet uncovered the depths of this enigmatic character, having frequented the house only briefly. M. de Bargeton, reclining in his chair, seemed to observe everything with a dignified silence that awed Lucien. Imaginative by nature, Lucien viewed M. de Bargeton not as a mere man but as a formidable sphinx, prompting him to show respect.

“I am the first comer,” Lucien said, bowing more than usual.

“That is natural enough,” replied M. de Bargeton.

Lucien interpreted this as a clever remark, thinking the husband was jealous, which made him self-conscious about his appearance. M. de Bargeton continued, “You live in L’Houmeau, and people who live far away always come earlier than those nearby.”

“What is the reason for that?” Lucien asked politely.

“I don’t know,” M. de Bargeton replied, returning to silence.

Lucien tried to revive the conversation, mentioning that Mme. de Bargeton was likely dressing. M. de Bargeton confirmed this, and Lucien, feeling awkward, noticed the room’s preparations for something special and fretted about his attire. He turned his attention to a Japanese jar on a console table, seeking common ground.

“You seldom leave the city, monsieur?” Lucien ventured.

“Very seldom,” came the curt reply.

Silence fell again, with M. de Bargeton watching Lucien closely and making him uneasy. Just then, the old servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered confidently, greeting M. de Bargeton and nodding at Lucien, who felt overshadowed by the Baron’s elegance.

Sixte du Châtelet wore fashionable white trousers and a black coat, his appearance reflecting the faded charm of a once-stylish beau. He scrutinized Lucien’s attire, deepening Lucien’s discomfort. M. de Bargeton, sensing tension, seized the moment to ask Châtelet if there was any news.

“The latest is M. Chardon,” Châtelet said with a hint of malice. “Have you brought a charming poet for us?” he added, adjusting his hair.

“I should have asked you whether I succeeded,” Lucien replied, feeling overshadowed by Châtelet’s experience.

“Pshaw! Just a few vaudevilles and songs,” Châtelet dismissed, downplaying his own work.

At that moment, Mme. de Bargeton appeared, dressed elaborately, captivating Lucien with her theatrical style. Châtelet showered her with compliments, which pleased her, but she barely acknowledged Lucien, favoring Châtelet instead.

As guests arrived, the Bishop and his Vicar-General entered, both dignified yet contrasting. Following them were Mme. de Chandour and her husband, a couple almost fictional in eccentricity. Amélie de Chandour, posing as a rival queen, and her husband, M. de Chandour, were striking figures; his exaggerated dress drew attention.

M. de Saintot, known as Astolphe, came next with his solemn wife, Élisa. Astolphe was considered a scientific man, though his knowledge was superficial. M. de Bartas, another guest, presented himself as a musical expert and sought praise for his singing.

M. Alexandre de Brébian, an artist, and his wife, entered with M. de Bartas, each showing quirks. The women, Lolotte and Fifine, were obsessed with fashion, while their husbands appeared dowdy in comparison.

Among others were M. le Comte de Senonches, a haughty gentleman, and his wife Zéphirine, tall and striking despite ill health. Francis, a friend of the family who managed their household, was devoted to Zéphirine, prompting speculation about their relationship.

The wealthiest couple, the Marquis de Pimentel and his wife, arrived with the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac. Their presence stirred jealousy, especially as Mme. de Bargeton paid them special attention, highlighting the social dynamics.

The Prefect and the General arrived last, along with a nervous country gentleman who brought a treatise on silkworms. He struggled to fit into the setting, eager to discuss his work but met with indifference.

Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard, noble yet impoverished, were present, their attempts to make connections marked by desperation. M. de Séverac, a childless widower, engaged them about his silkworm nurseries, while Mme. du Brossard sought to promote her daughter’s talents.

As the evening progressed, Lucien felt increasingly scrutinized by the assembled guests. He noticed most referred to him as M. Chardon, not M. de Rubempré, which stung his pride. The audience seemed more interested in their own conversations than his poetry, leaving him isolated.

When it came time to read, Lucien felt excitement mixed with anxiety. He announced he would read the works of a recently discovered poet, André de Chénier, which the audience misinterpreted as a pretext to spare his feelings.

Lucien began with Le Malade, which received mild applause, but his reading of L’Aveugle fell flat as the audience struggled to engage. The lack of connection made the experience torturous; he sensed their disinterest and impatience.

Despite his efforts, Lucien felt discouraged, drenched in sweat from the pressure. A supportive glance from Louise gave him some courage, and he pressed on, reading Chénier’s Iambes, which finally captured some attention, though many still failed to grasp the meaning.

During a break, Zéphirine sent Francis to investigate the poetry, leading to a discussion among the women about Lucien’s background and the nature of his work. Amélie’s comments about Lucien’s noble name and his printing background sparked gossip, complicating perceptions of his status.

As the evening unfolded, Lucien remained oblivious to the mounting tension and misunderstanding around his poetry, lost in the beauty of his art while the audience remained largely indifferent.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Lucien attempted to immortalize his love for Mme. de Bargeton in an ode he believed rivaled Chénier. Proud as he presented it, he felt secure under her protective gaze while she secretly worried for him. The audience, however, was unimpressed: the Baron dismissed the poem as youthful verse, full of grandiose imagery but lacking substance. The women feigned admiration; their indifference was palpable, and they exchanged sarcastic remarks about Lucien's ambitions.

Amid the mockery, Francis, a cunning observer, sought to undermine Lucien by spreading rumors about his mother’s influence on his poetry. The Bishop, unwittingly complicit, praised Lucien, but his words only deepened the aristocrats’ scorn. Lucien, feeling out of place and overwhelmed, struggled to engage with the crowd, often misidentifying people and sensing the weight of their disdain.

As the evening wore on, social dynamics shifted: Lucien’s poetry was eclipsed by card games and music. Mme. de Bargeton, stung by her peers’ contempt for Lucien, retreated to her boudoir and offered him solace, encouraging him to endure for the sake of future greatness. She romanticized suffering as a precursor to artistic immortality and urged him to channel his pain into profound work.

Meanwhile, Lucien’s sister Eve and her lover David discussed his ambitions and the risks of his relationship with Mme. de Bargeton. David worried Lucien’s aspirations might lead to ruin; he was ill-equipped for high society’s demands. Eve, however, remained optimistic, believing they could support Lucien until he succeeded.

Their conversation turned to their own romance when David proposed. Eve hesitated over their differing social standings but ultimately agreed, motivated by love and a desire to help Lucien. David planned to provide for Lucien so he wouldn’t struggle alone.

On the way home, Lucien shared his frustrations about the evening, declaring his determination to win Mme. de Bargeton’s love and rise above the aristocrats’ scorn. News of Eve’s engagement to David felt like a setback; he feared their marriage would complicate his ambitions.

David then sought his father’s approval, only to meet skepticism. His father, focused on finances, dismissed Eve’s worth and urged a more advantageous match. David stood firm, asserting his love for Eve and commitment to their future despite his father’s objections.

Ultimately David left his father’s house disheartened, realizing he could not rely on paternal support. He resolved to build a life with Eve, determined to make their plans real even if it meant facing hardships alone. Lucien remained caught between his lofty aspirations and the harsh realities of his social standing, torn between dreams and the contempt of those around him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In Angoulême, the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s response dominated conversation, elevating Lucien to the status of a local hero. Walking through Beaulieu, young men envied him, and he overheard flattering remarks that inflated his ego. Lucien sought a private moment with Louise to share news of his sister Eve’s impending marriage, hoping her kindness would bring happiness. Louise greeted him with passionate affection, praised his reading, and promised to create a poetic oasis for him despite the envy they both faced.

Buoyed by family and friends, Lucien developed an inflated sense of self-worth. He playfully asked Louise if she would be his Beatrice; she responded with a teasing promise of future love and urged him toward greatness. Lucien confided in her about David and Eve’s love, and Louise reassured him that their bond transcended family ties. When M. de Bargeton entered, Lucien hesitated, but Louise invited him to stay for dinner and a reading, reinforcing Lucien’s sense of belonging.

As Lucien settled into life as M. de Rubempré, he enjoyed the luxuries of his relationship with Louise and the support of M. de Bargeton. His confidence grew, and he believed his literary ambitions would soon succeed. Eve and David postponed their wedding to support Lucien, enchanted by his charm and persuasion. However, David worried Lucien might change with his new status.

Their relationship attracted scrutiny from Châtelet, who sought to expose their intimacy. He watched closely, hoping to catch them in a compromising situation. Despite innocent interactions, provincial gossip and speculation led to misunderstandings. The constraints of provincial life made it hard for them to express love freely, and Louise began to feel the weight of societal expectations.

Determined to declare his love, Lucien faced the barrier of their environment. Louise, pressured by their situation, contemplated escaping provincial constraints. Châtelet continued to manipulate the narrative, sowing doubt about their innocence.

One day Lucien’s emotional outburst produced a vulnerable moment witnessed by Stanislas, who misinterpreted the scene. Gossip spread rapidly, with exaggerated tales circulating through Angoulême. Châtelet seized the opportunity to further his agenda, while Stanislas relished his role in the drama.

Mme. de Bargeton, aware of the rumors, felt compelled to defend her honor. She confided in M. de Bargeton, urging him to confront Stanislas for slanderous remarks. Motivated by his wife’s distress, M. de Bargeton prepared to challenge Stanislas despite his own insecurity.

Approaching Stanislas’s house, M. de Bargeton wrestled with how to assert himself without appearing foolish. His quiet demeanor and thoughtful approach ultimately earned respect as he navigated the tense gathering. The stage was set for a confrontation that would further entangle the lives of all involved, revealing the complexities of love, honor, and societal expectation in their small town.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Châtelet understood the implications of a late-night visit, especially with M. de Bargeton already in bed. It was clear Naïs had instigated it. Châtelet, with enough standing in the household to intervene, approached M. de Bargeton and asked if he wished to speak to Stanislas. The old gentleman, relieved to have a mediator, was directed to Amélie’s bedroom, anticipating a confrontation that could leave Mme. de Bargeton a widow and widen the rift between her and Lucien, the source of the trouble.

Châtelet warned Stanislas that Bargeton would likely confront him about remarks regarding Naïs and advised him to remain decorous and polite. When they returned, Bargeton demanded Stanislas retract his statements about Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré. Feeling cornered, Stanislas agreed to a duel, choosing pistols.

The room’s atmosphere shifted as news of the duel spread among the guests; opinions divided on who was right. Châtelet stayed composed while Stanislas appeared shaken. Amélie, sensing the tension, felt unwell and was escorted to her room. The men debated the duel’s implications, some recalling Bargeton’s past as a skilled marksman.

The next morning, Lucien had breakfast with David when Mme. Chardon arrived, alarmed by the duel. She reported Bargeton had nearly killed Stanislas, who had accused him of compromising behavior with Mme. de Bargeton. Lucien vehemently defended her innocence. The duel’s details were grim: Bargeton had shot Stanislas in the neck, leaving him injured.

As Lucien processed the news, he received a note from Louise warning him to stay away from her home because of the duel’s fallout. She suggested he spend the day with her, hinting at the emotional turmoil it had caused. David, preparing for his upcoming marriage, noted Lucien’s frequent visits to Mme. de Bargeton might need curtailing.

Compelled to visit Mme. de Bargeton, Lucien found her at breakfast with her husband and M. de Nègrepelisse, who scrutinized him, trying to gauge his relationship with his daughter. Afterward, Louise confided she planned to go to Paris to secure a political position for Bargeton; their absence from Angoulême was meant to mitigate the duel’s consequences.

Louise urged Lucien to join her in Paris, emphasizing opportunities in the capital and its vibrant intellectual life compared with provincial stagnation. Lucien felt drawn to this new life and to Louise, but his sister’s upcoming wedding weighed on him. Louise dismissed his concern, questioning why he would prioritize a family event over their future together. Torn between family loyalty and aspirations with Louise, Lucien felt overwhelmed.

After a heated exchange, Louise fainted; Lucien apologized and expressed desperation. She insisted he be discreet and prepare to leave with her the next night. Grappling with his decision, he returned to David’s home, feeling the weight of his family’s sacrifices.

Discussing plans with Eve and their mother, they expressed sorrow at his impending departure, especially with Eve’s wedding approaching. Lucien considered borrowing money to support himself in Paris, but Eve and their mother revealed their own financial strain from wedding expenses.

David, overhearing, offered to help financially despite the strain on his marriage. He agreed to endorse a loan for Lucien, demonstrating loyalty and friendship. Lucien felt guilty for the burden but remained determined to pursue his dreams.

As Lucien prepared to leave, he felt the emotional weight of severing ties with his family. David and he devised a plan to discreetly send Lucien’s belongings to Paris, while Châtelet, suspicious of Mme. de Bargeton’s departure, sent someone to follow her.

Lucien set out early the next morning with David, who pretended to be on business. They waited for Mme. de Bargeton’s carriage; as it approached, Lucien felt a mix of excitement and dread about the journey. David, sensing the gravity, wished him well, fearing for Lucien’s future in Paris.
